topics.title: Definition and Importance of Consumer Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition and Importance of Consumer Behaviour ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus-Standard Definition:** > **Consumer Behaviour (CB)** is the formal study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy their needs and desires. In the context of **Food Marketing**, it specifically refers to the dynamic, multi-dimensional process through which consumers make purchase, consumption, and disposal decisions related to food and beverage products. **Authoritative Definitions by Renowned Scholars:** 1. **Leon G. Schiffman & Leslie L. Kanuk** — *"Consumer behavior is the behavior that consumers display in searching for, purchasing, using, evaluating, and disposing of products and services that they expect will satisfy their needs."* 2. **Michael R. Solomon** — *"Consumer behavior is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants."* 3. **Philip Kotler** — *It encompasses the study of how people make decisions about what they buy, what they want, what they need, or how they act toward a product, service, or organization.* 4. **James F. Engel, Roger J. Blackwell & David T. Kollat** — *They define CB as "the acts of individuals directly involved in obtaining and using economic goods and services, including the decision processes that precede and determine these acts."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "Define Consumer Behaviour," always include: > 1. The **multidisciplinary** nature (psychology, sociology, economics, anthropology). > 2. The **process orientation** — it is not just purchase, but the entire journey from need recognition to post-consumption. > 3. The **decision-making focus** — CB studies *why* consumers behave the way they do. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) Imagine you walk into a supermarket to buy **breakfast cereal**. > **Real-World Analogy — "The Breakfast Cereal Story"** You do not just magically grab the first box you see. Instead, you: - **Realize** you are hungry in the morning (need recognition). - **Compare** brands, check nutritional labels, recall advertisements (information search). - **Evaluate** options: taste vs. health vs. price vs. brand trust (evaluation of alternatives). - **Decide** to buy the oats pack you saw your favorite influencer promote (purchase decision). - **Experience** the product — was it tasty? Would you buy again? (post-purchase behavior). - **Dispose** of the empty box or reuse the container (disposal behavior). The *entire* above sequence — from the rumbling stomach to the empty box in the recycling bin — **IS Consumer Behaviour.** > **Simple Intuition:** > $$ \text{Consumer Behaviour} = \text{What people buy} + \text{Why they buy} + \text{When they buy} + \text{Where they buy} + \text{How they buy} + \text{How often they buy} $$ In food marketing, CB helps companies answer the trillion-dollar question: *"What does the consumer really want on their plate, and why?"* --- ### 1.3 Core Terminology for KTU 2024 | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Consumer** | An individual or organization that purchases goods/services for personal/household use. | | **Customer** | A consumer who has actually made a purchase (existing relationship). | | **Buyer** | The specific individual who executes the purchase transaction. | | **End-User** | The person who ultimately consumes or uses the product. | | **Decision-Maker** | The person who actually decides what, when, where, how to buy. | | **Initiator** | The one who first suggests or identifies the need for a product. | | **Influencer** | A person whose opinion affects the buying decision. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always differentiate between *Consumer* and *Customer*. A customer is always a consumer, but a consumer is not always a customer (e.g., a child who eats cereal bought by parents is a consumer, but the parent is the customer). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "5W + 1H" Framework of Consumer Behaviour (Schiffman) Consumer Behaviour can be systematically analyzed by answering six fundamental questions. This is the **KTU high-yield analytical scaffold** for any question on consumer behaviour fundamentals. $$ \boxed{ \text{CB}_{\text{framework}} = f(\text{What}, \text{Why}, \text{When}, \text{Where}, \text{Who}, \text{How}) } $$ **Step-by-step Logic:** 1. **What do consumers buy?** → Product attributes (taste, packaging, size, brand). 2. **Why do they buy it?** → Motivation, perception, attitudes, cultural factors. 3. **When do they buy?** → Occasion-based purchases (festivals, seasons, breakfast time). 4. **Where do they buy?** → Retail format (supermarket, online, kirana store). 5. **Who buys?** → Demographics, psychographics, family roles. 6. **How do they buy?** → Payment mode, quantity, frequency. --- ### 2.2 Nature / Characteristics of Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour, as a subject, exhibits the following **defining characteristics** — these are **frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and short-answer questions**: - **Dynamic Process:** CB is not static; it evolves with technology, culture, and personal experience. - **Multi-disciplinary Field:** Drawn from psychology, sociology, anthropology, economics, and demography. - **Involves Multiple Roles:** A consumer plays the role of initiator, influencer, decider, buyer, and user. - **Complex and Varied:** Two individuals rarely behave identically for the same product. - **Affected by Multiple Variables:** Cultural, social, personal, and psychological factors influence CB. - **Focuses on Individuals and Groups:** Studies both individual buying and family/organizational behavior. - **Includes Both Pre- and Post-Purchase Behavior:** Need recognition → Post-consumption evaluation. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — it is the single most important quick-reference for Module 1 questions.** | **Concept** | **Definition / Formula** | **Application to Food Marketing** | |---|---|---| | **CB Definition (Schiffman)** | Behavior displayed in searching, purchasing, using, evaluating, disposing of products | Maps directly to food lifecycle: grocery shop → buy → cook → eat → compost | | **Black Box Model** | Stimulus → Buyer's Black Box → Buyer Response | Used to predict food purchases from marketing mix inputs | | **5W + 1H Framework** | What, Why, When, Where, Who, How | Standard structure for analyzing any food consumer | | **Multidisciplinary Roots** | Psychology + Sociology + Anthropology + Economics + Demography | Helps design food products using multiple lenses (e.g., cultural food habits) | | **CB Process Stages** | 5 stages: Need Recognition → Info Search → Eval → Purchase → Post-Purchase | Forms basis for marketing-mix decisions in food companies | | **Consumer vs Customer** | Consumer = user; Customer = payer | Child eats cereal (consumer); Parent buys (customer) | | **Importance to Marketers** | Better targeting, positioning, segmentation | Kraft Heinz uses CB to launch region-specific flavors | | **Importance to Consumers** | Better informed choices, value-for-money | Enables comparison of organic vs. processed foods | | **Importance to Society** | Improved welfare, sustainable consumption | Encourages eco-friendly packaging adoption | | **Importance to Policymakers** | Consumer protection, fair trade laws | FSSAI regulations for food labeling in India | **Note on Units & Boundaries:** In marketing analytics, CB metrics are dimensionless *indices* — common units include **% (percentage market share)**, **NPS (Net Promoter Score, range -100 to +100)**, and **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score, 1–5 scale)**. --- ### 2.4 The Importance of Studying Consumer Behaviour Consumer Behaviour study is critical for **four key stakeholders**: #### **A. Importance to Marketing Managers / Companies** - Helps in **segmentation, targeting, and positioning (STP)**. - Enables designing of **marketing mix (4Ps/7Ps)** tailored to consumer needs. - Predicts **consumer response** to new food products. - Builds **brand loyalty** and **customer retention**. - Reduces **product failure rate** by understanding needs before launch. #### **B. Importance to Consumers** - Empowers **informed decision-making**. - Provides **better value for money** through comparison. - Protects from **deceptive marketing practices**. - Helps in selecting **products aligned with personal values** (organic, cruelty-free). #### **C. Importance to Public Policy / Government** - Enables formulation of **consumer protection laws** (e.g., Consumer Protection Act 2019 in India). - Guides **food safety regulations** (FSSAI in India, FDA in USA). - Supports **fair trade practices** and **anti-monopoly laws**. - Assists in **public health campaigns** (e.g., anti-tobacco, junk food warnings). #### **D. Importance to Society and Researchers** - Promotes **sustainable consumption** (reducing food waste, plastic use). - Advances **academic knowledge** in marketing science. - Improves **quality of life** through better products. - Helps in understanding **socio-cultural shifts** (e.g., rise of veganism). --- ### 2.5 Why is CB especially important in Food Marketing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Food is unique** because it is **physiologically essential, culturally embedded, emotionally charged, and frequently purchased.** Unlike a car or laptop, food is bought **every few days**, making CB data more dynamic and rich. Specific reasons CB is critical in food marketing: 1. **Perishability** — Decisions must be fast; consumer impulse matters. 2. **Health & Safety** — Trust and perception of safety drive loyalty. 3. **Cultural Sensitivity** — A consumer in Kerala will prefer rice-based products; a North Indian prefers wheat. 4. **High Involvement vs. Low Involvement** — Some food purchases are habitual (bread), some are planned (wedding cake). 5. **Emotional Connection** — Food evokes nostalgia, comfort, identity. 6. **Sustainability Concerns** — Modern consumers demand eco-friendly packaging and ethical sourcing. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The Consumer Decision-Making Process (5-Stage Model) The **Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model** and **Kotler's 5-Stage Model** are the most widely taught frameworks. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of each stage with logical transitions. $$ \boxed{ \text{Consumer Decision Journey} = N \rightarrow I \rightarrow E \rightarrow P \rightarrow PP } $$ Where **N** = Need Recognition, **I** = Information Search, **E** = Evaluation of Alternatives, **P** = Purchase Decision, **PP** = Post-Purchase Behavior. --- #### **Stage 1: Need Recognition (Problem Identification)** **Logical Derivation:** - A consumer does not buy a product without first sensing a *gap* between their **desired state** and **actual state**. - This gap is the **need** or **problem**. - Need can be triggered by **internal stimuli** (hunger, thirst, fatigue) or **external stimuli** (advertisement, peer influence, social media). $$ \text{Need} = \text{Desired State} - \text{Actual State} $$ **Example:** You feel hungry (internal) → see a pizza ad on Instagram (external) → need recognition occurs. **Types of Needs (Maslow's Hierarchy applied to food):** | Level | Need Type | Food Example | |---|---|---| | Physiological | Hunger, thirst | Bread, water | | Safety | Safe, hygienic food | Packaged, certified food | | Social | Eating with family, status | Restaurant dining, branded food | | Esteem | Self-image, prestige | Premium imported chocolates | | Self-actualization | Ethical, sustainable consumption | Organic, vegan, fair-trade foods | --- #### **Stage 2: Information Search** Once the need is recognized, the consumer seeks information to resolve it. This stage has two sub-levels: $$ \text{Information Search} = \text{Internal Search} + \text{External Search} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Internal Search:** Recall from memory — *"What brand did I buy last time?"* 2. **External Search:** If internal memory is insufficient, the consumer seeks external cues: - **Personal sources:** Family, friends, peers. - **Commercial sources:** Advertisements, salespeople, websites. - **Public sources:** Consumer reports, reviews, social media. - **Experiential sources:** Free samples, in-store trials. **Example:** You search "best organic honey brand India" on Google → read reviews → ask friends. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The level of search depends on **involvement** and **perceived risk**. High-priced food items (wedding cake) trigger extensive search; low-priced habitual items (salt) trigger minimal search. --- #### **Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives** The consumer now compares the gathered options using a set of **evaluative criteria**. **Logical Derivation:** - The consumer identifies a **consideration set** (also called *evoked set*) — typically 3–5 brands being seriously considered. - Evaluates against attributes: **price, quality, taste, brand reputation, nutritional value, packaging.** - Uses a **multi-attribute attitude model**: $$ \text{Attitude toward Brand} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i \times b_i) $$ Where: - $w_i$ = importance weight of attribute $i$ - $b_i$ = consumer's belief about brand's performance on attribute $i$ - $n$ = total number of attributes considered **Example:** While evaluating breakfast cereals: - Attribute 1: Taste (weight 0.4, belief 8) → 0.4 × 8 = 3.2 - Attribute 2: Price (weight 0.3, belief 7) → 0.3 × 7 = 2.1 - Attribute 3: Health (weight 0.3, belief 9) → 0.3 × 9 = 2.7 - Total attitude score = 3.2 + 2.1 + 2.7 = **8.0** The brand with the **highest attitude score** is most preferred. --- #### **Stage 4: Purchase Decision** This is the *moment of truth.* However, two factors can intervene between evaluation and actual purchase: $$ \text{Purchase Decision} = f(\text{Intent}, \text{Situational Factors}, \text{Others' Attitudes}) $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Purchase Intent:** Consumer forms an intention to buy brand X. 2. **Intervening Variables:** - **Attitude of others:** Family disapproval may switch choice. - **Situational factors:** Store out of stock, budget constraints. - **Unexpected events:** Emergency expense, change in plan. 3. **Actual Purchase:** Final transaction occurs. **Sub-decisions within purchase:** - **Product choice** (which variant?) - **Brand choice** (which company?) - **Quantity** (how much to buy?) - **Timing** (when to buy?) - **Payment method** (cash, UPI, credit card?) - **Dealer choice** (which store/website?) --- #### **Stage 5: Post-Purchase Behavior** After purchase, the consumer experiences either **satisfaction** or **dissonance.** $$ \text{Post-Purchase Outcome} = \text{Cognitive Dissonance OR Satisfaction OR Dissatisfaction} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Post-purchase dissonance (Buyer's Remorse):** Common in high-involvement purchases — *"Did I make the right choice?"* 2. **Satisfaction:** Product meets or exceeds expectations → **brand loyalty, repeat purchase, word-of-mouth.** 3. **Dissatisfaction:** Product falls short → **returns, complaints, negative reviews, brand switching.** **Marketing Action Implications:** - Follow-up communication to reassure buyer. - Easy return policies. - Customer service responsiveness. - Loyalty programs to retain satisfied customers. --- ### 3.2 The Black Box Model (Stimulus-Response Model of CB) This is the **foundational analytical framework** that every KTU student must master. It is the basis for almost every marketing strategy formulation. $$ \boxed{ \text{Marketing Stimuli} + \text{Environmental Stimuli} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Black Box} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Response} } $$ **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1 — Stimuli (Input):** The consumer is exposed to two broad categories of stimuli: $$ S_{total} = S_{marketing} + S_{environmental} $$ **Step 1a — Marketing Stimuli (4Ps):** $$ S_{marketing} = \underbrace{P}_{\text{Product}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Price}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Place}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Promotion}} $$ **Step 1b — Environmental Stimuli (Forces):** $$ S_{environmental} = E_{economic} + E_{tech} + E_{political} + E_{cultural} + E_{social} $$ **Step 2 — Buyer's Black Box (Internal Processing):** This is the "invisible" cognitive and emotional processing inside the consumer's mind. It consists of two parts: $$ BB_{contents} = C_{buyer} + DP_{decision process} $$ Where: - $C_{buyer}$ = Buyer's characteristics (cultural, social, personal, psychological) - $DP_{decision process}$ = The 5-stage decision process (covered in Section 3.1) **Step 3 — Buyer Responses (Output):** $$ R_{buyer} = \{ R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5 \} $$ Where: - $R_1$ = Product choice (what is bought) - $R_2$ = Brand choice (which brand) - $R_3$ = Dealer choice (where to buy) - $R_4$ = Purchase timing (when to buy) - $R_5$ = Purchase quantity (how much to buy) **Complete Equation:** $$ S_{marketing} + S_{environmental} \xrightarrow{BB_{contents}} R_{buyer} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often forget that **Buyer's Black Box = Buyer's Characteristics + Decision Process**, not just one of them. Always list **both** components. --- ### 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix | **Stage** | **Input** | **Processing** | **Output** | **Food Marketing Example** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Need Recognition | Hunger, ad trigger | Gap identification | "I need breakfast" | McDonald's "Good Morning" campaign | | 2. Information Search | Google, friends, reviews | Internal + External recall | Consideration set of 3–5 brands | Reading Zomato reviews for a new restaurant | | 3. Evaluation of Alternatives | Brand attributes | Multi-attribute scoring | Ranked preference list | Comparing Nestle vs. Amul vs. local dairy | | 4. Purchase Decision | Choice + Intervening factors | Final selection | Actual transaction | Paying via UPI at the billing counter | | 5. Post-Purchase | Product experience | Satisfaction/dissonance evaluation | Loyalty/switching behavior | Posting a positive Instagram food review | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Consumer Decision-Making Process — Detailed Flowchart > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** Node IDs are alphanumeric only (no reserved keywords). Labels are clean uppercase text without bold/italic markdown. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([START: Consumer Triggered]) --> n1["STAGE 1: NEED RECOGNITION

Consideration Set Identified
Multi-attribute Model Applied"] n3 --> n4["STAGE 4: PURCHASE DECISION
Intent Formed

Product Used and Evaluated"] n5 --> n6{Outcome} n6 -- SATISFIED --> n7["Brand Loyalty

No reserved keywords used as node IDs. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph inputs["STIMULI INPUT SIDE"] stimA["MARKETING STIMULI

Buying Decision"] --> B B["FACTORS INFLUENCING CB"] --> C["CULTURAL FACTORS

Motivation

Beliefs and Attitudes"] C --> A D --> A E --> A F --> A style A fill:#E74C3C,color:#FFFFFF style B fill:#F39C12,color:#FFFFFF style C fill:#3498DB,color:#FFFFFF style D fill:#2ECC71,color:#FFFFFF style E fill:#9B59B6,color:#FFFFFF style F fill:#1ABC9C,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- #### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Consumer Behaviour. State any two definitions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Consumer Behaviour is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy their needs and wants. **Two Definitions (2 Marks):** 1. **Leon G. Schiffman & Leslie L. Kanuk:** *"Consumer behavior is the behavior that consumers display in searching for, purchasing, using, evaluating, and disposing of products and services that they expect will satisfy their needs."* 2. **Philip Kotler:** *"Consumer behavior is the study of how people make decisions about what they buy, what they want, what they need, or how they act toward a product, service, or organization."* > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Note:** Examiners award **1 mark** for a clear conceptual definition and **1 mark each** for every author definition quoted. Always mention the scholar's name. --- #### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **List and briefly explain any three importance of studying consumer behaviour.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Importance to Marketers (1 Mark):** Studying CB helps marketers understand consumer needs, design appropriate marketing mixes, and predict responses to product launches — thereby reducing product failure rates. 2. **Importance to Consumers (1 Mark):** It empowers consumers to make informed, rational, and value-driven purchase decisions while protecting them from deceptive marketing. 3. **Importance to Public Policy (1 Mark):** It aids governments in framing consumer protection laws, food safety regulations (e.g., FSSAI), and fair-trade practices. > [!NOTE] > Any three of the four (marketer, consumer, policy, society) are acceptable. --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)** *Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.* --- ## ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss in detail the various definitions of Consumer Behaviour as given by different authors. Explain its nature and characteristics. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the importance of studying Consumer Behaviour. Why is it particularly significant in Food Marketing? **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks** **1. Introduction to CB (1 Mark):** Consumer Behaviour is a multi-disciplinary field studying how consumers make decisions about selecting, purchasing, using, and disposing of products to satisfy their needs. **2. Definitions by Various Authors (4 Marks):** - **Schiffman & Kanuk:** Defined CB as the behavior displayed in searching for, purchasing, using, evaluating, and disposing of products and services expected to satisfy needs. - **Michael Solomon:** Described CB as the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. - **Philip Kotler:** Defined CB as the study of how people decide what to buy, want, need, or how they act toward products or organizations. - **Engel, Blackwell & Kollat:** Defined CB as the acts of individuals directly involved in obtaining and using economic goods and services, including the decision processes preceding and determining these acts. **3. Nature and Characteristics (2 Marks):** - **Dynamic Process:** CB evolves with time, culture, and technology. - **Multi-disciplinary Roots:** Drawn from psychology, sociology, economics, anthropology. - **Multiple Roles:** Consumer plays initiator, influencer, decider, buyer, and user. - **Diverse Across Individuals:** Behavior varies widely among consumers. --- ### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks** **1. Importance of Studying CB (4 Marks):** - **To Marketers:** Helps in segmentation, targeting, positioning (STP), and designing the marketing mix (4Ps). Reduces product failure and builds brand loyalty. - **To Consumers:** Empowers informed decision-making, ensures value-for-money, and protects from deceptive practices. - **To Public Policy / Government:** Helps frame consumer protection laws (e.g., Consumer Protection Act 2019), food safety regulations, and fair trade practices. - **To Society:** Promotes sustainable consumption, ethical practices, and improved quality of life. **2. Significance in Food Marketing (3 Marks):** - Food is bought **frequently**, providing rich behavioral data. - Food purchases are influenced by **culture** (regional preferences), **emotion** (nostalgia, comfort), and **health concerns** (organic, low-sugar). - Modern consumers demand **transparency in labeling**, **sustainable packaging**, and **ethical sourcing**, all of which require deep CB insights. - The food industry's **perishability and safety risks** make consumer trust a critical marketing asset. --- ## ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) #### `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the Black Box Model of Consumer Behaviour with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe in detail the five stages of the Consumer Decision-Making Process with suitable food marketing examples. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks** **1. Introduction (1 Mark):** The Black Box Model, also called the **Stimulus-Response Model**, is the foundational framework of CB developed within Kotler's marketing system. It treats the consumer's mind as a "black box" whose internal processes can be inferred from inputs and outputs. **2. Model Components (5 Marks):** - **Stimuli (Input):** - **Marketing Stimuli:** The 4Ps — Product, Price, Place, Promotion. - **Environmental Stimuli:** Economic, technological, political, cultural, and social forces. - **Buyer's Black Box (Processing):** - **Buyer's Characteristics:** Cultural, social, personal, and psychological factors. - **Decision Process:** The 5-stage process (need recognition → post-purchase). - **Buyer Responses (Output):** - Product choice, brand choice, dealer choice, purchase timing, and quantity purchased. **3. Diagram (1 Mark):** *(Student is expected to draw the Stimulus → Black Box → Response flowchart.)* **Equation Form:** $$ \boxed{ \text{Stimuli} \xrightarrow{\text{Black Box}} \text{Response} } $$ --- ### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks** **1. Stage 1 — Need Recognition (1.5 Marks):** Triggered when a gap arises between the desired and actual state. - *Food Example:* Hunger in the morning + seeing a breakfast cereal ad = need recognition. **2. Stage 2 — Information Search (1.5 Marks):** Consumer recalls from memory and seeks external information. - *Food Example:* Searching Google for "best sugar-free chocolate brands in India." **3. Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives (1.5 Marks):** Consumer compares brands on attributes like price, taste, brand, and health value using the multi-attribute attitude model. - *Food Example:* Comparing Britannia vs. McVitie's vs. local brands on crunchiness, sugar level, and price. **4. Stage 4 — Purchase Decision (1.5 Marks):** Final decision is influenced by intervening variables — others' attitudes and situational factors. - *Food Example:* Deciding to order via Swiggy instead of dining out due to rain. **5. Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behavior (1 Mark):** Consumer experiences satisfaction, dissatisfaction, or cognitive dissonance, leading to loyalty or switching. - *Food Example:* Posting a positive Instagram review after loving a new protein bar. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT skip stages** of the 5-stage model — each carries ~1.5 marks; missing even one costs up to 2 marks. > 2. **Do NOT confuse** "Customer" with "Consumer" — examiners test this distinction explicitly. > 3. **Always draw diagrams** for Black Box and Decision-Making Process — even a rough sketch earns 1 mark and improves presentation. > 4. **Cite author names** when providing definitions — unsourced definitions lose credibility and marks. > 5. **For Food Marketing examples**, give **specific Indian brand references** (Britannia, Amul, Nestlé India, ITC Foods) — generic "brand X" answers are penalized. > 6. **Use equations/boxed formulas** wherever possible — they signal depth and earn "extra credit" in KTU valuation. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Definition and Importance of CB** - ✅ Consumer Behaviour is the **study of the entire consumption journey** — not just the purchase act. - ✅ Always remember the **multi-disciplinary nature** — psychology, sociology, anthropology, economics, demography. - ✅ The **5W + 1H framework** is the simplest analytical scaffold for any CB question. - ✅ Key scholars to remember: **Schiffman, Kotler, Solomon, Engel-Blackwell-Kollat.** - ✅ The **5-stage Consumer Decision-Making Process**: Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behavior. - ✅ The **Black Box Model** = Stimulus (Marketing + Environmental) → Black Box (Buyer Characteristics + Decision Process) → Response. - ✅ **Customer ≠ Consumer**: Customer pays; Consumer uses. - ✅ CB is **important to four stakeholders**: Marketers, Consumers, Public Policy, and Society. - ✅ In **Food Marketing**, CB is especially crucial due to **perishability, cultural sensitivity, emotional attachment, health concerns, and sustainability demands.** - ✅ **Buyer Responses** in Black Box: Product choice, Brand choice, Dealer choice, Purchase timing, Purchase quantity. - ✅ **Maslow's Hierarchy** can be directly applied to food needs — from physiological (hunger) to self-actualization (ethical eating). - ✅ **Multi-attribute attitude model**: $\text{Attitude} = \sum (w_i \times b_i)$. - ✅ Always end answers with a **food-specific example** for higher marks in KTU food-marketing courses.

topics.title: Key Theories and Models of Consumer Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Key Theories and Models of Consumer Behaviour # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Consumer Behaviour** is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. In the context of food marketing, it specifically examines the decision-making processes, physical, emotional, and social actions of consumers before, during, and after the purchase of food products. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Consumer behaviour is a field of study that investigates the mental, emotional, and physical activities that people engage in when they select, purchase, use, and dispose of products and services to satisfy needs and desires." ### Key Theories and Models — The Conceptual Framework Theories and models of consumer behaviour are systematic constructs that **explain, predict, and influence** the buying behaviour of consumers. They integrate insights from **psychology, sociology, economics, and anthropology** to map the journey from need recognition to post-purchase evaluation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Decision** Imagine you walk past a bakery. Why did you stop? Why did you choose the chocolate croissant over the plain one? Why did you pay ₹120 instead of going elsewhere? - Was it **hunger**? (Physiological need — Maslow) - Was it the **aroma**? (Sensory stimulus — Stimulus-Response Model) - Was your friend eating one? (Social influence — Reference Groups, Veblen) - Did an advertisement pull you in? (Marketing-mix influence) - Or did you just have a bad morning and needed comfort? (Emotional driver — Freudian Theory) > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** > Every consumer decision is a *convergence* of multiple internal (psychological) and external (environmental) forces. A theory is a "lens" that highlights one specific set of these forces. ## 1.3 Major Categories of Theories The theories of consumer behaviour are broadly classified into four foundational schools of thought: | **S.No.** | **School of Thought** | **Disciplinary Origin** | **Primary Focus** | |-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Economic Theory** | Economics | Price, income, utility maximization | | 2 | **Psychoanalytic Theory** | Psychology (Freud) | Unconscious motives, hidden desires | | 3 | **Behavioral / Learning Theory** | Psychology (Pavlov, Skinner) | Stimulus-Response, conditioning | | 4 | **Cognitive / Motivational Theory** | Psychology (Maslow, Herzberg) | Needs, hierarchy, satisfaction | | 5 | **Sociological / Cultural Theory** | Sociology/Anthropology | Groups, culture, social class | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** Questions on the *Economic Man Theory*, *Marshall's Theory*, *Veblen's Theory*, *Freud's Theory*, *Maslow's Hierarchy*, and the **Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model** appear frequently in ESE. ## 1.4 Core Constructs and Standard Metrics - **Consumer Decision Process (CDP):** 5-stage standardized model (N-IB-PI-AP). - **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** Predicts the net profit attributed to the entire future relationship with a customer. - **Buyer Readiness Stage:** Awareness → Knowledge → Liking → Preference → Conviction → Purchase (AIDA-adjacent model). > [!NOTE] > The metric **Purchase Intention Score (PIS)** is widely used in food marketing research to gauge pre-buying sentiment. It typically ranges from **1 to 7** on a Likert scale, with **>4.0** indicating strong buy likelihood. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Economic Theory of Consumer Behaviour ### 2.1.1 The "Economic Man" Theory (Classical Economist Theory) The earliest and most foundational theory assumes the consumer is a **rational economic agent**. **Core Assumptions:** - Consumer has **perfect information** about all alternatives. - Consumer seeks to **maximize utility** (satisfaction) at the **lowest cost**. - Decisions are based on **price and income** only. - Taste and preferences are **constant**. **Limitation:** Ignores psychological, social, and emotional factors. ### 2.1.2 Marshall's Economic Theory (Law of Equi-Marginal Utility) Alfred Marshall refined the Economic Man model by introducing the concept of **marginal utility** — the additional satisfaction derived from consuming one extra unit of a good. **Core Principle:** A rational consumer allocates income so that the **marginal utility per rupee spent is equal across all products**. ### 2.1.3 Veblen's Theory (Conspicuous Consumption) Thorstein Veblen challenged the economic model, arguing that consumers often buy goods to **display wealth and social status**, not to maximize utility. **Key Idea:** In some food purchases (e.g., premium imported cheese, branded chocolates), the *visible price tag* itself becomes a desirable attribute. ## 2.2 Psychological Theories ### 2.2.1 Freudian Psychoanalytic Theory (Sigmund Freud) Freud proposed that human behaviour is driven by **unconscious motives**, often rooted in childhood conflicts. **Three Levels of Consciousness:** - **Id:** Primitive, pleasure-seeking (e.g., craving junk food when stressed). - **Ego:** Mediator, reality-based (choosing a healthier option). - **Superego:** Moral conscience (avoiding food waste). **Marketing Implication:** Many food cravings (e.g., chocolate during exam stress) are *subliminal* and cannot be elicited by direct questioning — hence qualitative research (focus groups, projective techniques) is used. ### 2.2.2 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (Abraham Maslow) A **5-tier pyramid** explaining the progression of human needs: > **Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization** In food marketing: - **Physiological need** → Hunger → Basic groceries. - **Safety need** → Food safety, FSSAI certification, organic labels. - **Social need** → Family meals, sharing food on social media. - **Esteem need** → Premium dining, exotic cuisines. - **Self-actualization** → Sustainable, ethical food choices (e.g., veganism). ### 2.2.3 Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (Frederick Herzberg) Applied to consumer behaviour, this theory distinguishes: - **Hygiene Factors (Dissatisfiers):** Absence causes dissatisfaction but presence does not motivate purchase (e.g., packaging integrity, expiry date, basic food safety). - **Motivational Factors (Satisfiers):** Drive active purchase and delight (e.g., unique flavor, brand story, nutritional value). > [!NOTE] > **Food Marketing Application:** A hygienic-packaged food product will not be *preferred* over competitors, but a **non-hygienic** product will be *rejected outright*. ### 2.2.4 Pavlovian / Behavioral Learning Theory (Classical Conditioning) Ivan Pavlov demonstrated that **neutral stimuli** can elicit a response when paired with an unconditioned stimulus. **Food Marketing Application:** The aroma of coffee, a fast-food jingle, or the golden-brown color of fried food acts as a **conditioned stimulus** that triggers hunger or cravings. ## 2.3 Sociological Theories ### 2.3.1 The Theory of Social Class (Veblen, Warner) Consumers in the **upper class** tend to purchase products for **status signaling**, while the **lower class** buys for **utility and value**. In food, this manifests in the choice between local kirana stores (value) and premium supermarkets (status). ### 2.3.2 Reference Group Theory A **reference group** is a social group that influences an individual's attitudes, values, and behaviours. In food marketing, family, peers, social media influencers, and celebrities all act as reference groups. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Application in Food Marketing** | |--------------|--------------------------|------------------|-------------------------------------| | **Equi-Marginal Utility (Marshall)** | $\frac{MU_A}{P_A} = \frac{MU_B}{P_B} = \lambda$ | Optimal allocation of budget across food items | Pricing strategies across product lines | | **Engel Curve** | $E = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot I$ | Expenditure (E) as a linear function of Income (I) | Identifying income-elastic vs. inelastic food items | | **Income Elasticity of Demand** | $E_I = \frac{\%\ \Delta Q}{\%\ \Delta I}$ | Measures responsiveness of demand to income changes | Predicting demand for premium vs. staple foods | | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$ | Present value of future customer cash flows | Loyalty programs, retention marketing | | **Purchase Intention Score (PIS)** | $PIS = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i}{n \cdot s_{max}}$ | Normalized score (0 to 1) of buying likelihood | Product launch testing | | **Reference Group Influence Index** | $RGI = \alpha \cdot Normative + \beta \cdot Informational$ | Weighted influence of group on decision | Influencer marketing, peer recommendations | | **Maslow's Need Satisfaction Score** | $NSS = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot L_i$ | Weighted score across 5 need levels | Segmentation by motivational profile | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Always specify the **unit** of elasticity — *Income Elasticity is unitless*, while *Marginal Utility is measured in utils per unit consumed*. Examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Marketing These theories and formulas are actively used by: - **FMCG giants** (HUL, Nestlé, ITC) for product positioning. - **Food delivery platforms** (Zomato, Swiggy) for personalization algorithms. - **D2C food brands** for influencer marketing and pricing. - **Government** (FSSAI, Ministry of Food Processing) for policy design and subsidy targeting. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Key Models ## 3.1 The Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model of Consumer Decision Making The EKB Model (1968) is one of the most comprehensive and widely tested models of consumer behaviour. It views consumer decision-making as a **four-stage process** integrated with **information processing**. ### 3.1.1 The Model Equation (Conceptual Form) The decision output (Choice) is a function of inputs, information processing, and decision process stages: $$C = f(I, P, E, V, L)$$ Where: - $C$ = Final Consumer Choice - $I$ = Information inputs (marketing stimuli, environmental) - $P$ = Information processing - $E$ = Evaluation of alternatives - $V$ = Values and goals - $L$ = Learning and memory ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Flow of the EKB Model **Stage 1: Need Recognition (Problem Identification)** - The consumer perceives a gap between their **actual state** and **desired state**. - Triggered by internal (hunger) or external (advertisement) stimuli. - *Output:* A clear, activated need. **Stage 2: Information Search** - Two types: **Internal search** (memory recall) and **External search** (online reviews, peer advice, store visits). - The depth of search depends on **perceived risk** and **involvement level**. **Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives** - Consumer evaluates available products against **attribute criteria**: price, taste, brand, nutrition, packaging. - A common technique is the **multi-attribute attitude model**: $$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot b_{ijk}$$ Where: - $A_{ij}$ = Attitude of consumer $i$ toward brand $j$ - $w_k$ = Importance weight of attribute $k$ - $b_{ijk}$ = Belief of consumer $i$ that brand $j$ has attribute $k$ **Step-by-step expansion for a food product evaluation:** $$A_{brand} = w_{taste} \cdot b_{taste} + w_{price} \cdot b_{price} + w_{nutrition} \cdot b_{nutrition}$$ Substituting example values: - $w_{taste} = 0.5$, $b_{taste} = 8$ - $w_{price} = 0.3$, $b_{price} = 6$ - $w_{nutrition} = 0.2$, $b_{nutrition} = 7$ $$A_{brand} = (0.5 \cdot 8) + (0.3 \cdot 6) + (0.2 \cdot 7)$$ $$A_{brand} = 4.0 + 1.8 + 1.4 = 7.2$$ A score of **7.2 / 10** indicates a moderately favourable attitude. **Stage 4: Purchase Decision and Outcome** - The consumer acts on the most favourable attitude, subject to **situational factors** (availability, time, peer pressure). - Followed by **post-purchase behaviour**: satisfaction, cognitive dissonance, repurchase intention. ## 3.2 The Howard-Sheth Model (Buyer Behaviour Model) The Howard-Sheth Model (1969) emphasizes the **learned problem-solving** nature of consumer decisions. **Key Constructs:** 1. **Perceptual Variables** — Input stimuli (price, quality, brand). 2. **Learning Variables** — Motive, Cues, Response, Reinforcement. 3. **Output Variables** — Attention, Comprehension, Attitude, Intention, Purchase. **The fundamental flow equation:** $$B = f(M, C, R, Re, O)$$ Where: - $B$ = Buyer behaviour - $M$ = Motives - $C$ = Cues - $R$ = Responses - $Re$ = Reinforcement - $O$ = Outputs **In food marketing application:** A consumer's *motive* (e.g., fitness goal) interacts with *cues* (a "low-sugar" label on a yogurt), producing a *response* (trial purchase), and upon satisfaction, *reinforcement* (repeat purchase) occurs. ## 3.3 The Nicosia Model (1966) Francesco Nicosia's model focuses on the **interaction between the firm and the consumer**. It has four fields: **Field 1:** Consumer attitude formation (from firm's message). **Field 2:** Information search and evaluation. **Field 3:** Act of purchase. **Field 4:** Feedback (post-purchase experience → stored attitude). **Mathematical representation of attitude change in Field 1:** $$\Delta A_1 = k \cdot M \cdot (I - A_0)$$ Where: - $\Delta A_1$ = Change in attitude - $k$ = Sensitivity constant - $M$ = Marketing message intensity - $I$ = Ideal point of consumer - $A_0$ = Initial attitude **Interpretation:** The change in attitude is **proportional to the gap** between the consumer's ideal point and their current attitude, scaled by the message intensity. ## 3.4 The Stimulus-Response (S-R) Model (Black Box Model) The S-R Model treats the consumer's mind as a **black box** — marketers only observe inputs and outputs. $$\text{Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Black Box (Consumer Mind)} \rightarrow \text{Response}$$ **Stimuli Components:** $$S = S_{Marketing} + S_{Environmental} + S_{Situational}$$ **Marketing Stimuli** $S_{Marketing} = f(\text{4Ps} = \text{Product, Price, Place, Promotion})$ **Environmental Stimuli** $S_{Environmental} = f(\text{Economic, Technological, Political, Cultural})$ **Response Components:** $$R = f(\text{Choice, Brand, Quantity, Timing, Spender})$$ **The Behavioural Equation (Black Box Form):** $$R = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n + \epsilon$$ Where $X_i$ are internal psychological variables (perception, attitude, motivation) and $\epsilon$ is the random error term. The coefficients $\beta_i$ represent the relative influence of each variable on the purchase response. ## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix of Models | **Model** | **Year** | **Proponent** | **Core Construct** | **Stage Count** | **Key Strength** | **Key Limitation** | |-----------|----------|----------------|----------------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------| | Economic Man | 1800s | Classical Economists | Rational utility | 1 | Simplicity | Ignores emotions | | Marshall | 1890 | Alfred Marshall | Marginal utility | 1 | Quantifiable | Unrealistic rationality | | Veblen | 1899 | Thorstein Veblen | Conspicuous consumption | 1 | Social dimension | Limited to luxury goods | | Pavlov | 1903 | Ivan Pavlov | Classical conditioning | 2 | Explains habits | Cannot explain complex decisions | | Freud | 1920 | Sigmund Freud | Unconscious motives | 3 | Deep insight | Difficult to research | | Maslow | 1943 | Abraham Maslow | Hierarchy of needs | 5 | Universal framework | Rigid hierarchy | | Herzberg | 1959 | Frederick Herzberg | Two-factor | 2 | Hygiene vs. motivator | Originally workplace-based | | Howard-Sheth | 1969 | Howard & Sheth | Learning-based | 4 | Comprehensive | Complex to apply | | Nicosia | 1966 | Francesco Nicosia | Firm-consumer interaction | 4 | Marketing-actionable | Less individual-focused | | EKB | 1968 | Engel, Kollat, Blackwell | Information processing | 4 | Most empirical | Sequential assumption | | S-R (Black Box) | 1950s | Behavioural School | Stimulus → Response | 2 | Marketing-utility | Black box nature | # 4.
