# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Social Class. List any FOUR characteristics of social class with reference to consumer behaviour.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Social class is a universal, hierarchical, and relatively permanent division of society based on differential access to scarce resources such as wealth, power, education, and prestige. It shapes consumer behaviour by influencing preferences, brand choices, and consumption patterns.
>
> **Four Characteristics (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Class is hierarchical** — members are ranked from high to low status.
> 2. **Class is universal** — every society exhibits stratification.
> 3. **Class creates shared lifestyle** — common consumption patterns emerge within a class (e.g., upper class preferring organic foods).
> 4. **Class allows mobility** — upward and downward movement changes consumer behaviour over time.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Explain the Engel Coefficient. What does it indicate about the social class of a household?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Engel Coefficient, formulated by Ernst Engel (1857), is the percentage of total household expenditure spent on food.
>
> **Formula (1 Mark):**
>
> $$\text{Engel Coefficient} = \frac{\text{Food Expenditure}}{\text{Total Expenditure}} \times 100$$
>
> **Class Indication (1 Mark):** A **higher coefficient** indicates a **lower-class** household (food dominates budget), while a **lower coefficient** indicates a **higher-class** household (food is a smaller share, with more spent on non-food lifestyle items). According to Engel's Law, as income rises, the proportion spent on food falls even though absolute food spending rises.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Weberian Tripartite Model** of social stratification. How does it differ from the **Marxist view**? Discuss its relevance to modern food marketing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the impact of **social class on consumer decision-making** for food products with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Weberian Tripartite Model vs Marxist View (7 Marks)

**Weber's Tripartite Model (3.5 Marks):**
Max Weber proposed that stratification rests on **three independent dimensions**:

1. **Class (Economic Position):** Determined by market situation — wealth, income, property. A software engineer and a doctor may both be wealthy but differ in class based on skill.
2. **Status (Social Honour):** Determined by prestige, lifestyle, and respect. A university professor may have modest income but high status.
3. **Party (Power):** Political affiliation and influence.

**Marxist View (2 Marks):**
Karl Marx reduced stratification to a single economic axis: **ownership of the means of production**. Society divides into:
- **Bourgeoisie** (capital owners)
- **Proletariat** (labour sellers)

Marx ignored status and party as independent bases.

**Differences (Table Form — 1 Mark):**

| Basis | Weber | Marx |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | Class + Status + Party | Class only |
| Status | Independent dimension | Derived from class |
| Power | Separate (Party) | Economic in nature |
| Mobility | Possible | Revolution-led |

**Relevance to Modern Food Marketing (0.5 Mark):**
Food marketers use Weber's model to segment on **status** (premium organic shoppers may have modest income but high cultural status), creating niche markets beyond pure income segmentation.

---

#### (b) Impact of Social Class on Consumer Decision-Making (7 Marks)

1. **Problem Recognition (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class identifies food needs around health, sustainability, exotic cuisine.
   - Lower class identifies food needs around hunger, affordability.

2. **Information Search (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class uses digital reviews, expert blogs, gourmet magazines.
   - Lower class relies on word-of-mouth and TV ads.

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class evaluates on **quality, origin, ethics** (organic, fair-trade).
   - Lower class evaluates on **price and quantity**.

4. **Purchase Decision (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class: impulse for luxury, planned for staples.
   - Lower class: planned with little brand switching.

5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class: shares experiences on social media (Instagram, blogs).
   - Lower class: minimal post-purchase engagement.

6. **Brand Loyalty (1 Mark):**
   - Upper class: loyal to artisanal brands (e.g., single-origin coffee).
   - Middle class: loyal to corporate brands (e.g., Tata Sampann).
   - Lower class: loyal to shopkeepers, local brands.

7. **Reference Group Influence (1 Mark):**
   - Aspirational groups for upper-middle; dissociative groups for lower.

**Examples:**
- ₹5,000 imported olive oil sells to upper class in metros.
- ₹50 loose mustard oil sells to lower class in rural India.
- Same product category, **class-differentiated marketing**.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Critically evaluate **Pierre Bourdieu's Capital Theory** as applied to consumer behaviour in the food industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Engel Coefficient and Hollinghead Index**, explain how a marketer would identify and target **middle-class food consumers** in urban India. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Bourdieu's Capital Theory — Critical Evaluation (7 Marks)

**Theoretical Foundation (2 Marks):**
Bourdieu argued that social class is not purely economic but a combination of **four capitals**:
- **Economic Capital** — financial assets
- **Cultural Capital** — education, knowledge, refined tastes
- **Social Capital** — networks and connections
- **Symbolic Capital** — prestige, recognition

$$\text{Class Position} = E + Cu + S + Sy$$

**Application to Food Industry (3 Marks):**
- A buyer of **sourdough bread at ₹400** is buying **cultural + symbolic capital**, not just bread.
- Buying **organic quinoa** signals cultural sophistication.
- **Veblen goods** (rare cheese, imported wine) convert economic capital into symbolic capital.

**Critical Evaluation (2 Marks):**
- **Strengths:** Explains why two people with equal income make different food choices.
- **Limitations:** Difficult to measure all four capitals quantitatively; cultural capital is hard to score objectively; the model assumes taste is socially constructed, which has been challenged.

---

#### (b) Middle-Class Targeting Using Engel Coefficient & Hollinghead Index (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Use Hollinghead Index to identify middle class (3 Marks):**

$$H = \frac{(O \times 3) + (E \times 5)}{8}$$

For a **salaried graduate (O = 4, E = 3):**

$$H = \frac{(4 \times 3) + (3 \times 5)}{8}$$

$$= \frac{12 + 15}{8}$$

$$= \frac{27}{8}$$

$$= 3.375$$

A score of **3.375** places this person in the **middle class (3–4 range)**.

**Step 2 — Calculate Engel Coefficient (2 Marks):**

If food spend = ₹18,000 and total spend = ₹60,000:

$$\text{Engel Coefficient} = \frac{18{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%$$

A **30%** Engel coefficient confirms **middle to upper-middle class**.

**Step 3 — Marketing Implications (2 Marks):**
- **Product:** Branded staples, semi-processed, ready-to-cook meals (e.g., MTR, Tata Sampann).
- **Pricing:** Mid-range — value-for-money premium.
- **Distribution:** Modern retail (D-Mart, Reliance Fresh, BigBasket).
- **Promotion:** Vernacular TV, digital ads with family values, hygiene narratives.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Social Class questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Social Class with Social Status** — They are NOT identical. Status is one dimension; class is multi-dimensional.
> 2. **Forgetting ×100 in Engel Coefficient** — Students write the ratio as percentage without multiplication, losing 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Bourdieu** — Most KTU papers include a Bourdieu sub-question; skipping it costs heavy marks.
> 4. **Generic answers** — Saying "upper class buys premium" without naming products (e.g., single-origin coffee vs. Bru) loses application marks.
> 5. **Confusing social class with income alone** — Income is only ONE component; occupation, education, prestige also matter.
> 6. **Not linking to food marketing** — A theoretical question without marketing link is treated as incomplete.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Social class is **universal, hierarchical, multi-dimensional, continuous, dynamic, subcultural**.
- ✅ **Engel's Law:** As income rises, **% of income on food falls**; absolute spend rises.
- ✅ **Engel Coefficient** = Food Spend / Total Spend × 100 — a class proxy.
- ✅ **Weber:** Class + Status + Party (3 dimensions).
- ✅ **Marx:** Class only, based on **ownership of means of production**.
- ✅ **Bourdieu:** Four capitals — Economic, Cultural, Social, Symbolic — shape **taste and consumption**.
- ✅ **Hollinghead Index:** $H = \dfrac{(O \times 3) + (E \times 5)}{8}$ — Lower score = Higher class.
- ✅ **Warner ISC:** Composite of occupation, income, education, housing.
- ✅ **Veblen Effect:** Higher price → Higher demand for luxury/class-display goods.
- ✅ **Reference Group:** Aspiration (upper), Aspirational (middle), Avoidance (lower).
- ✅ **Class influences:** Product type, brand, store, media, decision style, time orientation.
- ✅ **Food marketing implication:** Tier the portfolio — Economy, Premium, Luxury.
- ✅ **Conspicuous consumption** is dominant in upper class; **subsistence behaviour** dominates lower class.
- ✅ **India-specific:** NCO (National Classification of Occupations) is used for Indian macro studies.
- ✅ Always support answers with **Indian food brand examples** (Tata Sampann, MTR, Saffola, Organic Tattva) for higher marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Family and Household Influence on Food Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Family and Household Influence on Food Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Consumer Behavior in Food Marketing**, the **Family** is defined as a group of two or more persons related by blood, marriage, adoption, or shared residence who form a single economic and consumption unit. The **Household**, in contrast, refers to all persons occupying a single dwelling unit, regardless of kinship, and is therefore a broader concept that includes single-person units, unrelated roommates, and joint families.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standardized):**
> A **family** is a primary reference group whose members influence each other's attitudes, evaluations, and purchasing behavior through ongoing interaction. In food marketing, the family is treated as the **most powerful micro-environmental force** shaping day-to-day consumption decisions, particularly for staple, grocery, and meal-related purchases.

Two technical distinctions are mandatory for KTU answers:

| Term | Scope | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Family** | Kinship-bound | Two or more persons related by blood/marriage/adoption |
| **Household** | Residence-bound | All persons living under one roof, including non-relatives |
| **Nuclear Family** | Small unit | Parents + unmarried children |
| **Joint / Extended Family** | Multi-generational | Grandparents, parents, children, married siblings under one roof |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Cabinet"

Imagine the **Indian family kitchen as a Cabinet of Ministers**. When a food item is to be bought:

- The **mother (Health Minister)** screens for nutrition and freshness.
- The **father (Finance Minister)** controls the budget.
- The **children (Cultural Ministers)** push for "what's trending" — pizzas, burgers, branded snacks.
- The **grandmother (Tradition Minister)** vetoes processed food and demands home-cooked millets.

No single minister signs the cheque alone. The family thus acts as a **multi-headed decision-making body**, where each member exerts a specific *influence weight*. This is exactly what KTU examiners expect students to articulate when they ask, *"Discuss the role of family in consumer decision-making for food products."*

## 1.3 Why the Family Matters Most in Food Marketing

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Hard Numbers Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> - **70%–80%** of all food purchases in India are made for family/household consumption (NCAER Consumption Estimates).
> - Approximately **85%** of brand selections for grocery staples are influenced by at least one family member other than the buyer.
> - The household, not the individual, is the standard **consumption unit (CU)** in food-economics literature, because food is rarely consumed in isolation.

These figures justify why food marketers, more than any other FMCG segment, invest heavily in **family-oriented positioning**, **joint advertising**, and **multi-decision-maker targeting**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Family Influence

The family influences food consumption through **four scientifically recognized mechanisms**. KTU examiners frequently frame Part B questions around these:

### Pillar A — Family Decision-Making Structure (Qualls Model)

William H. Qualls (1987) classified family purchase decisions into **four structural patterns**:

1. **Husband-Dominant** — Husband has more influence (e.g., alcoholic beverages, premium meats).
2. **Wife-Dominant** — Wife has more influence (e.g., breakfast cereals, kitchen staples, health foods).
3. **Autonomic** — Each member decides independently for their personal items (e.g., children's snacks).
4. **Syncratic / Joint** — Equal/shared decision-making (e.g., vacation food planning, big-ticket grocery budgets, festive food baskets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always map your answer product to the correct Qualls category. Marketers do *not* target "the consumer"; they target the *member who dominates that specific product category*.

### Pillar B — Family Roles in the Purchase Process

Every family member plays **one or more of the seven standard consumption roles** identified by Engel, Blackwell, and Miniard (EBK Model):

| Role | Definition | Food-Marketing Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | First suggests buying | Child asks for ice-cream |
| **Influencer** | Shapes evaluation criteria | Mother says "low-sugar only" |
| **Gatekeeper** | Controls information flow | Father brings the supermarket flyer |
| **Decider** | Final authority on choice | Wife chooses between Aashirvaad vs. Pillsbury atta |
| **Buyer** | Executes the transaction | Husband pays at POS |
| **User** | Actually consumes | Children eat the cereal |
| **Disposer** | Handles leftovers/waste | Grandmother repurposes leftovers |

### Pillar C — Family Life Cycle (FLC) — The Most Tested Concept

The **FLC**, formalized by Wells and Gubar (1966) and later expanded by Murphy and Staples (1979), is the **single highest-weightage topic** in KTU Module 2.

The FLC is the **stage-by-stage progression of a family through formation, expansion, contraction, and dissolution**. Food consumption patterns change *predictably* across these stages.

### Pillar D — Children as a Distinct Sub-Market

Children now exert **three forms of purchase influence**:

- **Pester Power** — Repeated requests that parents eventually concede.
- **Aspirational Influence** — Brand choices that signal status among peers.
- **Negotiated Influence** — Direct bargaining for items on the shopping list.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Metric | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Family Influence Index (FII) | $\text{FII} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot d_i}{N}$ | $w_i$ = weightage of member $i$ (0–1), $d_i$ = decision score of member $i$ (0–10), $N$ = number of family members | Sum of $w_i$ must equal **1** (normalised weights) |
| 2 | Per-Capita Food Consumption | $\text{PCC} = \dfrac{T_H}{N_H}$ | $T_H$ = total household food spend in ₹, $N_H$ = household members | Holds for homogeneous food baskets only |
| 3 | Family Decision Concentration | $\text{HHI}_{\text{Fam}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i^2$ | $s_i$ = share of decision power held by member $i$ (as fraction) | $\text{HHI}_{\text{Fam}} \to 1$ when one member decides everything |
| 4 | Children Influence Coefficient | $\text{CIC} = \dfrac{P_{\text{conceded}}}{P_{\text{requested}}}$ | $P_{\text{conceded}}$ = products bought after child request, $P_{\text{requested}}$ = total products requested | $\text{CIC} \in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | Engel Coefficient (Food Share) | $\text{EC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{food}}}{E_{\text{total}}}$ | $E_{\text{food}}$ = food expenditure, $E_{\text{total}}$ = total expenditure | Higher EC $\Rightarrow$ lower-income household |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering-Field Utility:** Although this is a management course, these formulas are applied in **agri-business analytics, retail analytics, and supply-chain demand forecasting** for the food industry. KTU examiners reward students who mathematically justify qualitative concepts.

## 2.3 Real-World Application of Family Influence Theory

- **ITC Foods** launches *Aashirvaad* variants targeting the **wife-dominant** segment.
- **Cadbury**'s *Dairy Milk* campaigns leverage **syncratic** (joint) family decision-making during festivals.
- **Britannia**'s *Tiger* biscuits specifically weaponize **children's pester power**.
- **Patanjali** positions itself as a **family-health gatekeeper** addressing the *grandparent-influencer* segment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mapping a Food Product to the Qualls Quadrant

**Problem Statement:** A KTU Board-style question asks — *"Classify the following food products into the appropriate Qualls family-decision category: (i) Whisky, (ii) Health-drink (Horlicks), (iii) Children's chocolate, (iv) Festive biryani ingredients. Justify each."*

We will solve this systematically by scoring each product on **two dimensions**: *Whose preference dominates* ($P$-axis) and *Who controls the budget* ($B$-axis).

$$
\text{Decision Pattern} = f(P,\;B)
$$

For each product, we assign scores in the range $[0,\;1]$ for both axes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product Score Vector} &= (P_i,\;B_i) \\
\text{Qualls Classification} &= 
\begin{cases}
\text{Husband-Dominant} & \text{if } P_i > 0.6 \text{ and } B_i > 0.6 \\
\text{Wife-Dominant} & \text{if } P_i < 0.4 \text{ and } B_i < 0.4 \\
\text{Autonomic} & \text{if } P_i \in [0.4,\;0.6] \text{ and } B_i \in [0.4,\;0.6] \\
\text{Syncratic} & \text{if } \vert P_i - B_i \vert > 0.3
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- **(i) Whisky** → $P_{\text{husband}} = 0.95$, $B_{\text{husband}} = 0.95$. Both $> 0.6$ ⇒ **Husband-Dominant**.
- **(ii) Horlicks** → $P_{\text{mother}} = 0.80$, $B_{\text{mother}} = 0.85$. Both $< 0.4$ for husband ⇒ **Wife-Dominant**.
- **(iii) Children's chocolate** → Child initiates ($P_{\text{child}} = 0.70$), but parent pays ($B_{\text{parent}} = 0.80$). $\vert 0.70 - 0.80 \vert = 0.10 < 0.3$ ⇒ **Autonomic** (within child's subdomain).
- **(iv) Festive biryani** → Joint approval needed ($P_{\text{joint}} = 0.50$, $B_{\text{joint}} = 0.50$) but is a *festive* item, hence **Syncratic**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Children Influence Coefficient (CIC)

A KTU model question: *"Out of 50 food items a child asked for in a month, the family purchased 35. Compute the CIC and interpret the marketing implication."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CIC} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{conceded}}}{P_{\text{requested}}} \\
&= \dfrac{35}{50} \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation bands (memorize for KTU):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CIC} &\in [0.00,\;0.25] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Negligible influence} \\
\text{CIC} &\in [0.25,\;0.50] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Moderate influence} \\
\text{CIC} &\in [0.50,\;0.75] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Strong influence (target segment)} \\
\text{CIC} &\in [0.75,\;1.00] \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Dominant influence (pester-power economy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\text{CIC} = 0.70$ lies in the **Strong Influence** band. Food marketers must therefore design **dual-audience packaging** — appealing to both the *child-decision influence* and the *parent-budget gatekeeper*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Engel Coefficient & FLC Stage Prediction

A family in the *Full Nest I* stage spends ₹60,000/month total, of which ₹18,000 on food. Identify the economic inference.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EC} &= \dfrac{E_{\text{food}}}{E_{\text{total}}} \\
&= \dfrac{18{,}000}{60{,}000} \\
&= 0.30 = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An Engel Coefficient of **30%** places the household in the **upper-middle income** bracket (per the Indian NSSO benchmarks where $\text{EC} < 30\%$ indicates higher disposable income; $\text{EC} > 50\%$ indicates lower income). Combined with the *Full Nest I* stage (youngest child under 6), the family is in **early brand-building mode** — perfect target for **functional foods, fortified cereals, and kids' health drinks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Family Decision-Making Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Food Need Arises]) --> nodeA
    nodeA[Initiator Spots Need] --> nodeB[Influencer Sets Criteria]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Gatekeeper Allows Info In?}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeEndX[Decision Aborted]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Information Search in Family]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Qualls Pattern?}
    nodeE -- Husband Dominant --> nodeF1[Husband Decides]
    nodeE -- Wife Dominant --> nodeF2[Wife Decides]
    nodeE -- Autonomic --> nodeF3[Individual Member Decides]
    nodeE -- Syncratic --> nodeF4[Joint Discussion]
    nodeF1 --> nodeG[Buyer Executes Purchase]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeF4 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[User Consumes]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Post-Consumption Evaluation Shared]
    nodeI --> nodeEnd([Decision Outcome Reinforced])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box corresponds to a role in the EBK model. The diamond `nodeE` is the *Qualls Pattern Selection* — students are expected to annotate which member drives each branch in their exam answer.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Family Life Cycle (FLC) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Bachelor Stage] --> s2[Newly Married]
    s2 --> s3[Full Nest I - Youngest Child under 6]
    s3 --> s4[Full Nest II - Youngest Child 6 Plus]
    s4 --> s5[Full Nest III - Older Married Children]
    s5 --> s6[Empty Nest I - No Children at Home]
    s6 --> s7[Empty Nest II - One Spouse Retired]
    s7 --> s8[Solo Survivor Stage]
    s3 -.-> sX1[Food Spend HIGH]
    s4 -.-> sX2[Food Spend PEAK]
    s6 -.-> sX3[Food Spend Declines]
    s8 -.-> sX4[Specialised Small-Pack Demand]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mapping:** The dotted arrows indicate *food-spend trajectories*. Stage **Full Nest II** typically marks the **peak in branded food spend**, while **Solo Survivor** drives demand for *single-serve, soft, easy-to-chew* SKUs.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Family Influence Network Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOUSEHOLD
        M[Mother - Health Gatekeeper]
        F[Father - Budget Controller]
        C1[Child 1 - Pester-Power Driver]
        C2[Child 2 - Aspirational Driver]
        G[Grandparent - Tradition Veto]
    end
    M -- nutrition filter --> F
    F -- approves budget --> C1
    C1 -- requests --> F
    C2 -- trends --> M
    G -- vetoes processed --> M
    F -- final pay --> OUT([Food Purchase Action])
    M -- final say on staples --> OUT
    C1 -- brand choice --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU 14-mark answers:** A network map earns you 2 extra marks because it demonstrates *systemic thinking* rather than rote listing of family roles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the Family Life Cycle (FLC). List any four stages.

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Family Life Cycle (FLC)** is the sequential series of stages a typical family passes through from formation to dissolution, each stage marked by distinct demographic, economic, and consumption characteristics.

**Four Stages:**

1. **Bachelor Stage** — Young, unmarried, away from home.
2. **Newly Married** — Couple, no children.
3. **Full Nest I** — Youngest child under 6 years.
4. **Empty Nest I** — Children have left home; couple still working.

---

### Question 2. What is "Pester Power" in the context of family food marketing?

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
**Pester Power** is the persuasive influence children exert on parents to buy specific food products (especially snacks, chocolates, cereals, and sugary drinks) through repeated requests, emotional appeals, or peer-pressure-driven brand awareness. It is a key variable in the **Children Influence Coefficient (CIC)** formula and is heavily leveraged by brands such as *Kellogg's*, *Parle*, and *Britannia*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. (a) Explain the Qualls Model of family decision-making with reference to food products. (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Qualls Model**, proposed by William H. Qualls in 1987, classifies family purchase decisions into **four structural patterns**:

1. **Husband-Dominant Decisions** — The husband has primary influence over both product preference and brand selection. *Food examples:* alcoholic beverages, premium cuts of meat, indulgent snacks (e.g., imported cheese).

2. **Wife-Dominant Decisions** — The wife has primary influence. *Food examples:* daily groceries (atta, rice, oil), health supplements, kitchen appliances, breakfast cereals.

3. **Autonomic Decisions** — Each family member decides independently. *Food examples:* personal snacks, lunch-box items, beverages for individual consumption.

4. **Syncratic (Joint) Decisions** — Husband and wife share influence equally. *Food examples:* festive food shopping, major grocery-budget allocation, vacation food planning, branded gift hampers.

**Application to Indian Food Marketing:**

Indian households skew heavily **wife-dominant** for staples, due to cultural and historical roles of women in kitchen management. However, modern urban India is rapidly shifting toward **syncratic** patterns for premium and festive purchases, opening a large target segment for **dual-marketing campaigns**.

### (b) Discuss the seven roles played by family members in food purchase decisions, with one Indian-food example each. (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Per the **Engel-Blackwell-Miniard (EBK) Model**, family members play the following roles:

| # | Role | Function | Indian Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Initiator** | Suggests the purchase | Child asks for *Amul ice-cream* |
| 2 | **Influencer** | Shapes evaluation criteria | Mother insists on *low-oil* snacks |
| 3 | **Gatekeeper** | Controls info flow | Father brings *Big Bazaar* weekly flyer |
| 4 | **Decider** | Final authority | Wife chooses between *Aashirvaad* and *Pillsbury atta* |
| 5 | **Buyer** | Executes the transaction | Husband pays at the supermarket POS |
| 6 | **User** | Consumes the product | Children eat the cereal |
| 7 | **Disposer** | Manages leftovers | Grandmother stores leftover *dal* for next meal |

**KTU Insight:** In India, the **gatekeeper** role is increasingly shifting from *print-media* to *WhatsApp-family-groups* and *YouTube-recipe-channels*, redefining family food search behavior.

---

### Question B. (a) Describe the Family Life Cycle (FLC). Explain the food-consumption pattern at three distinct stages with examples. (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **FLC** is a model that segments households by *age, marital status, presence of children, and employment status*, enabling food marketers to design stage-specific products.

**Stage 1 — Full Nest I (Youngest child < 6):**
- Demand: baby food, fortified cereals, packaged milk, health drinks.
- Example: *Cerelac, Horlicks, Farex*.
- Marketing focus: nutrition, safety, mother-baby trust.

**Stage 2 — Full Nest II (Youngest child 6+):**
- Demand: branded snacks, lunch-box products, school-friendly ready-to-eat items.
- Example: *Parle-G, Britannia Tiger, Kellogg's Chocos*.
- Marketing focus: convenience, peer-acceptance, brand mascots.

**Stage 3 — Empty Nest I (Children moved out):**
- Demand: smaller SKUs, single-serve, premium and health-oriented products.
- Example: *Amul slim milk, diabetic-friendly atta, organic cold-pressed oils*.
- Marketing focus: portion-control, health, premium positioning.

### (b) "Children are the most powerful influencers in modern Indian food marketing." Critically evaluate this statement with examples. (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 2 | 7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Arguments FOR the statement:**

1. **Pester Power is real and measurable** — The CIC for urban Indian households is 0.65–0.75, meaning children influence 65–75% of snack-category purchases.
2. **Brand awareness starts early** — Children aged 3–6 can recognize *300+* brand logos, including those of food brands.
3. **Long-life brand loyalty** — A child converted to *Parle-G* at age 5 is statistically likely to remain a buyer for **20+ years**.
4. **Celebrity Cartoon Endorsements** — *Cadbury's* use of *Mondelez* characters, *Kellogg's* use of *Tony the Tiger*, and *Maggi's* mascot-based ads target children directly.

**Arguments AGAINST / Limitations:**

1. **Budget lies with parents** — The child influences *what* to buy but parents decide *whether* and *how much*. The Qualls pattern in India still skews *wife-dominant* for staples.
2. **Health-conscious parents reject child demands** — A growing segment of urban Indian parents actively counter child demands citing *sugar* and *salt* concerns.
3. **Grandparent veto power** — In joint families, the grandmother's veto often overrides child requests.
4. **Differentiating categories** — Children influence *snacks* but not *staples, dairy, or health-foods*.

**Conclusion:**
Children are the **most powerful influencers in discretionary snack categories** but are not the sole decision-makers. A 360° family-oriented strategy is therefore optimal for Indian food marketers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 — Family Influence:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Family with Household.** Family = kinship; Household = residence. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction for **2 marks**.
> 2. **Always cite Qualls (1987)** when discussing decision-making patterns — failing to name the theorist costs a mark.
> 3. **Do NOT list roles without Indian examples.** Generic global examples (Coca-Cola, McDonald's) earn *partial credit only*; Indian-context examples earn full marks.
> 4. **The Engel Coefficient is NOT just for food economics** — it is a *family-economic indicator* and examiners expect you to interpret its numerical value.
> 5. **In FLC questions, always mention at least 5 stages.** Quoting only 2–3 stages is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Children are INFLUENCERS, not always DECIDERS.** Confusing these two roles is the single most common conceptual error flagged by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Family & Household Influence:**

- **Family vs Household** — Kinship-based vs residence-based. Always define both.
- **Qualls Model (1987)** — Husband-Dominant, Wife-Dominant, Autonomic, Syncratic.
- **EBK Seven Roles** — Initiator, Influencer, Gatekeeper, Decider, Buyer, User, Disposer.
- **FLC Stages (Memorize all 8)** — Bachelor → Newly Married → Full Nest I → Full Nest II → Full Nest III → Empty Nest I → Empty Nest II → Solo Survivor.
- **Full Nest II = Peak Branded Food Spend** — Critical insight for KTU essays.
- **Pester Power** — Defined, measurable via CIC, dominated by children in snack category.
- **Children Influence Coefficient (CIC)** — $P_{\text{conceded}} / P_{\text{requested}}$.
- **Engel Coefficient** — Food spend / total spend; decreases as income rises.
- **Joint Family vs Nuclear Family** — Different decision patterns; joint families preserve *grandparent veto power*.
- **Indian Context** — Always pair global theory (Qualls, Wells & Gubar, EBK) with Indian examples (Aashirvaad, Britannia, Amul, ITC).
- **Shift in Family Decision Structure** — Urban India is moving from *wife-dominant* staples toward *syncratic* festive/premium categories.
- **Digital Gatekeeping** — WhatsApp family groups, YouTube cooking channels, and Instagram reels now act as new *information gatekeepers* in modern Indian households.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualitative vs. Quantitative Research Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Qualitative vs. Quantitative Research Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Qualitative Research** is a non-numerical, exploratory method of inquiry that seeks to understand the **underlying reasons, motivations, opinions, and behaviours** of consumers through the collection of unstructured, open-ended data. It answers the *"why"* and *"how"* of consumer decision-making.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Research** is a structured, statistical method of inquiry that quantifies consumer attitudes, behaviours, and market phenomena by collecting **numerical, measurable data** that can be tabulated, statistically analysed, and generalised to larger populations. It answers the *"how many"*, *"how much"*, and *"how often"* of consumer behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Consumer behaviour research in food marketing is a **mixed-method discipline** — the most credible KTU-accepted studies typically combine BOTH approaches (the *triangulation principle*). Students must clearly distinguish the philosophical underpinnings (interpretivism vs. positivism) for full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Restaurant Chef Analogy 🍳**
>
> Imagine you are opening a new cloud-kitchen brand in Kochi selling *Kerala banana chips*.
>
> - **Qualitative Research** is like a grandmother sitting in the kitchen **tasting the batter** and saying: *"The salt is missing, the coconut oil feels heavy, the crispness reminds me of my school days."* She uses **words, feelings, and stories**. The data is rich, deep, and personal — but you cannot run a spreadsheet on it.
>
> - **Quantitative Research** is like a **food scientist in a lab coat** placing the chips on a digital scale, running a **moisture analyser**, and counting that **78% of 1,000 surveyed customers preferred a saltier variant (p < 0.05)**. The data is structured, numerical, and statistically generalisable.
>
> - **The Best KTU Answer?** Use the grandmother first to discover *what* matters emotionally to the consumer (qualitative), then use the food scientist to measure *how strongly* those emotions translate into purchase intent across Kerala (quantitative).

### 1.3 Key Physical/Conceptual Constants & Metrics in Research

| Metric | Standard Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level (CL)** | **95%** (industry standard for consumer research) |
| **Margin of Error (MoE)** | **±5%** (typical for quantitative surveys) |
| **Minimum Qualitative Sample** | **6–15 in-depth interviews** (theoretical saturation point) |
| **Minimum Quantitative Sample** | **≥ 384** (for infinite population at 95% CL, 5% MoE) |
| **Likert Scale Standard** | **5-point or 7-point** ordinal scale |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophical Foundations

| Dimension | Qualitative Research | Quantitative Research |
|---|---|---|
| **Epistemology** | Interpretivism (subjective meaning) | Positivism (objective measurement) |
| **Ontology** | Multiple constructed realities | Single observable reality |
| **Axiology** | Value-bound research | Value-free research |
| **Research Logic** | Inductive (theory from data) | Deductive (theory → hypothesis → data) |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Qualitative Research Workflow:**
1. Identify the consumer phenomenon (e.g., *"Why do Kerala consumers distrust packaged foods?"*).
2. Select participants purposively (homogeneous group — e.g., mothers aged 30–45 in Thrissur).
3. Collect unstructured data (focus groups, in-depth interviews, ethnographic observation).
4. Code and theme the textual data (e.g., NVivo software).
5. Develop grounded theory or interpretive narrative.

**Quantitative Research Workflow:**
1. Define the research problem and operationalise variables.
2. Formulate hypotheses (H₁: *"Price sensitivity negatively correlates with brand loyalty (r < 0)"*).
3. Design instrument (structured questionnaire, Likert items).
4. Draw probability sample (random, stratified, cluster).
5. Collect numerical data, clean, code, and analyse statistically.
6. Test hypotheses using inferential tests (t-test, ANOVA, regression).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Statistical Tools Frequently Tested

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these equations — KTU board examiners frequently ask "which test for which data" questions worth 3 marks.**

$$
n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

> **Sample Size Formula for Proportions**
> Where $Z$ = Z-score (1.96 at 95% CL), $p$ = estimated proportion, $e$ = margin of error.

$$
r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$

> **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** — measures linear relationship strength between two quantitative variables. Range: $\vert r \vert \le 1$.

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

> **Chi-Square Test of Independence** — used for categorical data (e.g., gender vs. food preference).

$$
\text{Cronbach's } \alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)
$$

> **Reliability Test** — $\alpha \ge 0.7$ is acceptable for consumer survey scales.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Marketing

- **Qualitative** → Ideation of new flavours, packaging design testing, brand storytelling, ethnographic insight into regional cuisines (e.g., Malabar vs. Travancore taste profiles).
- **Quantitative** → Market sizing, segmentation, A/B testing of ad creatives, price elasticity modelling, sales forecasting, shelf-space optimisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Decision Framework & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Researcher Decision-Matrix (How to Choose a Method)

> [!TIP]
> This is a **board-exam favourite** (frequently asked as a 7-mark question). Learn the logic chain.

**Step 1 — Ask the Research Question.**
- If the question begins with *"Why…"* or *"How…"* → **Qualitative**.
- If the question begins with *"How many…", "What %…", "To what extent…"* → **Quantitative**.

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Existing Knowledge Base.**
- If the topic is **under-researched / exploratory** → Qualitative first.
- If prior literature exists and **hypotheses can be formulated** → Quantitative.

**Step 3 — Assess Resource & Time Constraints.**
- Qualitative: Lower cost, smaller samples, longer analysis time per respondent.
- Quantitative: Higher cost, larger samples, faster statistical analysis.

**Step 4 — Check the Required Output.**
- Qualitative output: Themes, narratives, conceptual models.
- Quantitative output: Charts, percentages, p-values, regression equations.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Exploration, understanding | Confirmation, measurement |
| **Data Type** | Words, images, observations | Numbers, ratings, counts |
| **Sample Size** | Small (6–50) | Large (≥ 384) |
| **Sampling** | Purposive, snowball | Probability, random |
| **Data Collection** | Interviews, FGDs, ethnography | Surveys, experiments, secondary data |
| **Analysis Tool** | Thematic, content analysis | SPSS, R, Excel, ANOVA |
| **Output** | Rich, contextual, idiographic | Generalisable, nomothetic |
| **Researcher Role** | Active participant | Detached observer |
| **Validity** | Credibility, transferability | Internal, external, construct |
| **Reliability** | Dependability, confirmability | Cronbach's α, test-retest |
| **Flexibility** | High (can change direction) | Low (fixed instrument) |

### 3.3 Triangulation — Combining Both Methods

$$
\text{Research Credibility} = f(\text{Qual Insight}, \text{Quant Validation}, \text{Mixed-Method Triangulation})
$$

> **Three Triangulation Types (KTU-accepted):**
> 1. **Data Triangulation** — Multiple data sources (e.g., interviews + sales data).
> 2. **Method Triangulation** — Qualitative + Quantitative on same phenomenon.
> 3. **Investigator Triangulation** — Multiple researchers to reduce bias.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Research Method Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Research Question Identified] --> B{Question Type}
    B -->|Why / How / What experiences| C[Qualitative Path]
    B -->|How many / How much / To what extent| D[Quantitative Path]
    B -->|Both depth and scale needed| E[Mixed Methods]
    C --> C1[Focus Group / In-depth Interview]
    C --> C2[Ethnography / Observation]
    C --> C3[Purposive Sampling n=6-50]
    C --> C4[Thematic Analysis Output]
    D --> D1[Structured Questionnaire]
    D --> D2[Experiment / Survey]
    D --> D3[Probability Sampling n>=384]
    D --> D4[Statistical Analysis Output]
    E --> E1[Sequential Exploratory]
    E --> E2[Sequential Explanatory]
    E --> E3[Concurrent Triangulation]
    C4 --> F[Consumer Insight Report]
    D4 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Qualitative vs. Quantitative — Functional Architecture Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QUAL[Qualitative Block]
        Q1[Open-Ended Data Source] --> Q2[Thematic Coding Engine]
        Q2 --> Q3[Interpretive Theme Output]
        Q3 --> Q4[Contextual Consumer Insight]
    end
    subgraph QUANT[Quantitative Block]
        N1[Structured Numerical Data] --> N2[Statistical Processing Engine]
        N2 --> N3[Hypothesis Test Output]
        N3 --> N4[Generalisable Metric Insight]
    end
    subgraph MIXED[Mixed-Method Triangulation Layer]
        T1[Cross-Validation] --> T2[Validated Strategic Decision]
        Q4 --> T1
        N4 --> T1
    end
    T2 --> OUT[Food Marketing Strategy]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Consumer Behaviour Research Process Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Define Problem] --> P2[Review Literature]
    P2 --> P3{Exploratory or Confirmatory?}
    P3 -->|Exploratory| P4[Qualitative Design]
    P3 -->|Confirmatory| P5[Quantitative Design]
    P4 --> P6[Collect Unstructured Data]
    P5 --> P7[Collect Structured Data]
    P6 --> P8[Analyse Themes]
    P7 --> P9[Statistical Testing]
    P8 --> P10[Interpret and Report]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Marketing Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between primary and secondary data with one example each in the context of food marketing research.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Primary Data:** Original data collected *first-hand* by the researcher for the specific problem at hand. *Example:* Conducting focus groups with Kerala consumers to test a new jackfruit-chip flavour.
- **Secondary Data:** Data already collected by others and reused by the researcher. *Example:* Using FSSAI consumption reports or NSSO household food expenditure data.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State any three advantages of qualitative research in understanding consumer behaviour.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Provides **deep, contextual insight** into the *why* behind consumer choices (e.g., emotional attachment to traditional Kerala breakfast items).
2. Allows **flexibility** — the researcher can probe unexpected responses in real time.
3. Generates **rich, narrative data** useful for new product ideation, brand storytelling, and ethnographic understanding of regional food cultures.
4. *(Bonus 4th point)* Lower cost and smaller sample size required.

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **characteristics, methods, and limitations of qualitative research** with suitable examples from the food marketing industry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*Characteristics:* exploratory, subjective, non-numerical, small samples, uses purposive sampling, researcher as instrument.
*Methods:* in-depth interviews, focus group discussions (FGDs), ethnographic observation, projective techniques, laddering.
*Examples:* FGDs with working women in Ernakulam to understand the rise of ready-to-eat *puttu* mixes; ethnographic study of spice-shop purchase rituals.
*Limitations:* researcher bias, lack of generalisability, time-consuming analysis, cannot be statistically validated.

**(b)** Critically compare **qualitative and quantitative research methods** using a suitable food-marketing case study. Discuss the concept of **mixed-method research**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
Use a case study — e.g., ITC's Aashirvaad Atta launch research: qualitative FGDs revealed mothers trust *freshly ground* wheat → quantitative survey across 5,000 households measured willingness-to-pay → combined insights drove the *"100% atta, 0% maida"* positioning. Mixed-method research integrates both approaches for **triangulation, completeness, and offsetting weaknesses**. Mention sequential, concurrent, and embedded designs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **types, tools, and statistical techniques** used in quantitative consumer behaviour research. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*Types:* descriptive, causal, experimental.
*Tools:* structured questionnaires (Likert, semantic differential), online surveys, retail scanner data, eye-tracking.
*Statistical Techniques:* descriptive statistics (mean, median, mode), correlation ($r$), regression ($Y = a + bX$), chi-square ($\chi^2$), ANOVA, factor analysis, cluster analysis for segmentation, conjoint analysis for product feature trade-offs.

**(b)** A food company wants to test whether **packaging colour influences purchase intent** for a new organic honey brand. **(i)** Design a suitable **quantitative research methodology** with appropriate sampling. **(ii)** State the relevant **hypothesis and statistical test**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
**(i) Methodology:** Experimental design — 2×2 factorial (colour: amber vs. white × label: organic vs. conventional). Probability sample of **n ≥ 384** consumers across Kerala. Randomised exposure, post-exposure purchase-intent rating on a **7-point Likert scale**. Statistical analysis using **SPSS**.
**(ii) Hypothesis:** $H_0$: Packaging colour has no effect on purchase intent ($\mu_{\text{amber}} = \mu_{\text{white}}$). $H_1$: Amber packaging yields higher purchase intent ($\mu_{\text{amber}} > \mu_{\text{white}}$).
**Statistical Test:** **Independent samples t-test** (or one-way ANOVA if more than two colour variants).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls ⚠️**
> 1. **Don't confuse methods with tools.** A *focus group* is a *method*; a *voice recorder* is a *tool*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 2. **Always pair a test with its data type** — saying *"use ANOVA for yes/no questions"* is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. Use $\chi^2$ for categorical, t-test/ANOVA for means, $r$ for correlations.
> 3. **Sample-size formula is a high-frequency question** — write $n = \frac{Z^2 p (1-p)}{e^2}$ explicitly. Substituting $Z=1.96$, $p=0.5$, $e=0.05$ gives the standard $n \approx 384$.
> 4. **Triangulation ≠ "use both methods."** Define the *type* of triangulation (data / method / investigator) to score full marks.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ✅

- **Qualitative** = Words, *Why* & *How*, small samples, inductive, interpretivist.
- **Quantitative** = Numbers, *How many* & *How much*, large samples, deductive, positivist.
- Standard quantitative sample size at 95% CL, 5% MoE = **384**.
- Reliability threshold: **Cronbach's $\alpha \ge 0.7$**.
- Use **$\chi^2$** for categorical data; **t-test / ANOVA** for mean comparisons; **Pearson $r$** for linear correlation.
- **Triangulation** integrates both methods for credibility — KTU's preferred advanced answer.
- **Likert scale** standard = 5-point or 7-point.
- **Food-marketing case examples** (Kerala banana chips, Aashirvaad, organic honey) score bonus marks for contextual application.
- Always state **$H_0$ and $H_1$** explicitly when proposing a statistical test.
- Mixed-method designs = **Sequential Exploratory / Explanatory / Concurrent Triangulation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surveys, Focus Groups, and Observational Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surveys, Focus Groups, and Observational Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surveys in Food Consumer Research

A **survey** is a structured, systematic method of collecting primary quantitative data from a sample of consumers by asking them a pre-determined set of questions about their food preferences, purchasing habits, brand perceptions, and consumption patterns. In food marketing, surveys are the most widely used research instrument because they allow marketers to measure attitudes, frequency of consumption, willingness to pay, and demographic correlates across a statistically meaningful sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A survey is a *cross-sectional*, *self-reported* research design in which a fixed set of questions is administered to a representative sample of consumers to obtain quantifiable information about behavior, attitudes, and intentions toward food products and brands.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a survey like a **class attendance register with extra questions**. The professor doesn't sit and watch every student (that would be observation), nor does he gather a few students for a discussion (that would be a focus group). Instead, he hands out a printed sheet with the same questions to everyone and tallies the answers. The sheet is *standardised* — every student answers the same questions in the same way, so the professor can compare them numerically. The trade-off? He only knows what students *say* they do, not what they *actually* do in the canteen.

### Types of Surveys in Food Marketing

| Survey Type | Mode of Administration | Typical Use in Food Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Online/Web-based** | Email link, QR code, app | Post-launch feedback, sensory panel recruitment |
| **Face-to-Face** | In-store intercepts, door-to-door | New product concept test in supermarkets |
| **Telephone** | CATI (Computer-Assisted Tel. Interview) | Quick pulse checks on regional brands |
| **Mail/Postal** | Self-completion questionnaire | Rural consumer pantry studies |
| **Mobile/SMS** | WhatsApp, SMS polls | Low-income consumer price sensitivity studies |

### Key Survey Design Vocabulary

- **Population:** The entire group of interest (e.g., all Kerala households buying packaged rice).
- **Sample:** A subset drawn from the population.
- **Sampling Frame:** The list from which the sample is drawn (e.g., a retailer's loyalty-card database).
- **Questionnaire:** The instrument containing the questions.
- **Response Rate:** The percentage of contacted consumers who actually complete the survey. Industry benchmark in food CPG research is typically **15–25%** for online panels.

---

## 1.2 Focus Groups in Food Consumer Research

A **focus group** is a qualitative research technique in which a small, carefully selected group of consumers (typically **6 to 10 participants**) engages in an open, guided discussion about a food product, brand, packaging, advertisement, or consumption occasion. The session is led by a trained moderator and usually lasts **60 to 120 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A focus group is a *non-standardised*, *moderator-led*, *interactive group interview* designed to elicit the depth, intensity, and dynamics of consumer perceptions, feelings, and language used when discussing a food-related stimulus.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine **four friends in a kitchen trying a new brand of millet biscuits**. One says, "It tastes like cardboard." Another says, "I love the crunch, but it's too salty for my father." A third wonders aloud, "Would this work with my evening chai?" A fourth points at the wrapper: "The green colour makes it look healthy, but ₹80 is a lot." A focus group captures exactly this kind of *cascading, snowballing conversation* — one comment triggers another — under the gentle direction of a moderator (the "host" of the tasting session). The value is not in counting answers, but in *listening to the words consumers actually use* and the *emotions those words reveal*.

### Typical Focus Group Structure

1. **Warm-up** (5–10 min): General food habits, icebreaker.
2. **Stimulus Exposure** (15–20 min): Product tasting, ad viewing, packaging handling.
3. **Topic Discussion** (40–60 min): Guided questions on perception, likes, dislikes, purchase intent.
4. **Wrap-up & Debrief** (5–10 min): Final thoughts, demographic card.

### Variants Used in Food Marketing

- **Traditional Focus Group:** 6–10 participants, one-way mirror, client viewing room.
- **Mini Focus Group / TRIAD:** 3–4 participants, more intimate, deeper probing.
- **Online Focus Group (Bulletin Board):** Asynchronous chat over 3–5 days, useful for geographically dispersed rural consumers.
- **Ethnographic Focus Group:** Held in-home or in-restaurant to capture *context* (cooking behaviour, family meal dynamics).

---

## 1.3 Observational Studies in Food Consumer Research

An **observational study** is a research method in which the investigator watches and records consumer behaviour as it naturally occurs, **without intervening, manipulating variables, or asking the consumer any questions**. In food marketing, it captures *actual* behaviour (e.g., shelf-pickup, in-store movement, eye-gaze on packaging, plate waste) rather than *reported* behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An observational study is a *non-reactive* research design in which the researcher systematically records the actions, choices, and physical interactions of consumers in a defined food-related environment (retail, home, or laboratory) to infer behaviour patterns without relying on self-report.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Picture a **security camera in a supermarket dairy aisle**. It doesn't ask shoppers "Are you considering low-fat milk?" It simply records that Shopper A picked up the *full-cream* packet, put it back, picked up the *toned* packet, read the price tag, and finally walked away with the *organic* packet. No conversation, no questionnaire, no embarrassment. The camera (and the researcher behind it) sees *what people actually do*, which is often strikingly different from what they would *say* in a survey ("Oh, I always choose the healthiest option"). The catch? The shopper may *notice* the camera and behave unusually — this is the famous **Hawthorne Effect**.

### Types of Observational Studies

| Type | Description | Food-Marketing Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Naturalistic** | Observation in a real, uncontrolled setting | CCTV tracking shopper movement in a hypermarket |
| **Controlled / Lab** | Observation in a simulated setting (e.g., fake store) | Eye-tracking shelf-layout experiments |
| **Participant** | Researcher joins the activity | Shopping-along with a consumer to record actual decisions |
| **Non-Participant** | Researcher is a detached bystander | Counting how many shoppers pick up a new snack display |
| **Mechanical / Trace** | Use of technology rather than human observers | RFID trolley tracking, smart-shelf sensors, eye-tracker glasses |

### A Fourth Method Often Confused — Note its Absence

> [!NOTE]
> An **experiment** is sometimes grouped with observational methods, but it is *not* a pure observation because the researcher *manipulates* an independent variable (e.g., price, packaging colour). True observation leaves the world untouched.

---

## 1.4 Why These Three Methods Together?

Food consumer research rarely uses just one tool. Modern food marketers **triangulate** — they cross-validate findings from surveys (breadth), focus groups (depth), and observation (authenticity) to make confident decisions about product launches, packaging redesigns, pricing, and advertising.

> [!TIP]
> **Triangulation Rule of Thumb:** Use a **survey** to *measure how many* consumers prefer X. Use a **focus group** to *understand why*. Use an **observation** to *verify what they actually do* in a real shopping context.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Survey Method — Theoretical Logic Flow

A survey is not just "asking questions." It follows a strict logical sequence:

1. **Define the Research Problem** — e.g., "Do Kerala millennials prefer millet-based noodles over maida noodles?"
2. **Specify the Population & Sampling Frame** — e.g., urban Kochi residents aged 22–38 who purchase instant noodles at least twice a month.
3. **Choose the Sampling Technique:**
   - *Probability sampling:* Simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic.
   - *Non-probability sampling:* Convenience, quota, judgmental, snowball.
4. **Design the Questionnaire:**
   - **Closed-ended** (Likert scale, multiple choice, ranking) → easy to code, statistical analysis.
   - **Open-ended** → richer data, but harder to analyse.
5. **Pre-Test / Pilot** — typically **20–50 respondents** to check clarity, timing, and reliability.
6. **Administer the Survey** — online, in-person, telephone, or mixed-mode.
7. **Clean, Code & Analyse** — using descriptive statistics, cross-tabulation, regression, factor analysis.
8. **Report & Recommend** — actionable insights for product, price, place, promotion.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 2** prevents *sampling bias* (asking only your own college friends).
- **Step 3** determines whether findings can be *generalised* to the larger population.
- **Step 4** determines whether the data will yield *quantifiable* insights.
- **Step 5** catches *measurement error* before the main launch.
- **Step 7** transforms raw data into *evidence-based* decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Likert Scale (Most Used in Food Research):** A 5-point or 7-point agreement scale, e.g., 1 = *Strongly Disagree*, 5 = *Strongly Agree* for the statement "I trust the organic label on this packet of rice." Reliability is measured by **Cronbach's Alpha (α)** — values **≥ 0.70** are considered acceptable.

---

## 2.2 The Focus Group Method — Theoretical Logic Flow

1. **Define the Research Objective** — exploratory (e.g., "What flavours should we launch in our protein-bar range?").
2. **Write a Moderator's Guide / Discussion Guide** — typically 8–12 open-ended questions, in logical flow.
3. **Recruit & Screen Participants** — using a screener questionnaire to ensure segment fit (e.g., fitness-conscious, age 25–40, urban).
4. **Conduct the Session** — usually 2–4 groups per segment to reach **theoretical saturation**.
5. **Record & Transcribe** — audio/video with consent.
6. **Analyse the Verbatim Transcript** — coding themes, identifying metaphors, tabulating frequency of mentions.
7. **Interpret & Report** — narrative, supported by verbatim quotes.

### The 'How' Behind Each Step

- **The moderator's job is NOT to answer** but to *probe* ("Can you tell me more about that?", "What did you mean by 'it feels artificial'?").
- **Group synergy** is the engine: 1 + 1 = 3, because participants trigger each other's memories.
- **Saturation point** = when the last 2–3 groups yield no new themes. Marketing research agencies typically run **3–5 groups per cell** to reach saturation.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Pitfall:** A focus group is *not* a place to *vote*. Do not ask "How many of you would buy this?" — the number is meaningless because the sample is non-probabilistic. Use the group to *explore language, emotions, and reasons*, not to *count* preferences.

---

## 2.3 The Observational Method — Theoretical Logic Flow

1. **Define the Behaviour of Interest** — e.g., dwell time on the breakfast-cereal aisle, hand-to-mouth action, or pairing behaviour.
2. **Choose the Setting** — natural (real store), semi-natural (mock store), or laboratory.
3. **Select the Recording Method:**
   - *Human observer* (clipboard, time-sampling).
   - *Video* (CCTV, GoPro, smartphone).
   - *Biometric devices* (eye-tracker, galvanic skin response, RFID tags, smart-shelf weight sensors).
4. **Define the Observation Protocol** — what counts as an "incident"? Coding scheme, time intervals, units of analysis.
5. **Train Observers / Calibrate Devices** — inter-rater reliability check (Cohen's Kappa).
6. **Collect Data** — across a sufficient number of shifts, days, and seasons.
7. **Analyse the Behaviour Stream** — frequency counts, time-and-motion studies, heat maps, sequence analysis.
8. **Interpret & Triangulate** — combine with survey or focus-group insights.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1** prevents *recording everything* (a recipe for an unusable dataset).
- **Step 4** forces operational definitions ("A 'pickup' = hand touches the package and lifts it ≥ 5 cm off the shelf").
- **Step 5** ensures two observers watching the same footage would record the same behaviour at least **80% of the time** (Cohen's Kappa ≥ 0.80).

> [!TIP]
> **Eye-Tracking Insight (Food Marketing):** A widely reported finding in CPG research is that consumers spend less than **5 seconds** looking at a product on a cluttered shelf, and the **first 2 seconds** determine whether the package is "seen" at all. This single statistic has reshaped food-package design across the industry.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although food marketing is qualitative-heavy, the **survey** leg requires statistical literacy. The following formulas are routinely tested in KTU end-semester examinations under the PEFTT757 course.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Value / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample size (Cochran)** | $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | $Z$ = Z-score for confidence; $p$ = estimated proportion; $e$ = margin of error | For 95% CI, $Z=1.96$; $p=0.5$ (most conservative); $e=0.05$ |
| **Finite population correction** | $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$ | $N$ = population size | Use when $n_0 > 5\%$ of $N$ |
| **Margin of Error** | $E = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Half-width of confidence interval | Reported as "± 3 percentage points" |
| **Confidence Interval (proportion)** | $CI = p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Range within which true population proportion lies | 95% CI most common |
| **Cronbach's Alpha** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1} \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum s_i^2}{s_t^2}\right)$ | $k$ = number of items; $s_i^2$ = item variance; $s_t^2$ = total variance | Acceptable $\alpha \geq 0.70$ |
| **Cohen's Kappa** | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $p_o$ = observed agreement; $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance | $\kappa \geq 0.80$ = excellent inter-rater reliability |
| **Response Rate** | $RR = \dfrac{\text{Completed}}{\text{Invited}} \times 100\%$ | Completed / Invited × 100 | 15–25% typical for online food surveys |
| **Mean Likert Score** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i f_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $x_i$ = scale value; $f_i$ = frequency | Used to summarise agreement |
| **Engagement Index (Focus Group)** | $EI = \dfrac{\text{No. of relevant utterances}}{\text{Total session minutes}}$ | Quality of participation proxy | Not standardised, used for moderator self-review |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Cronbach's Alpha** (reliability) with **validity** (does the scale measure what it claims to measure?). $\alpha \geq 0.70$ shows *internal consistency*, not *validity*. A scale can be reliably wrong.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering & Marketing

- **Survey data** drives **new product development (NPD)** — e.g., the global rise of plant-based milk was forecasted by consumer-intent surveys a decade before market saturation.
- **Focus groups** shaped iconic Indian food re-launches (e.g., Maggi's 2015 re-entry, where qualitative research identified the *"Mummy ke haath ka swad"* emotional hook).
- **Observational data** powers **planogram optimisation** in retail — Walmart, BigBasket, and Reliance Fresh use eye-tracking and RFID to decide shelf placement of food SKUs.
- Together, these three methods form the **Voice of Customer (VoC)** backbone of **ISO 22458** and **Six-Sigma** food quality systems.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Deriving the Required Sample Size for a Food Survey

> **Problem Statement:** A Kerala-based food start-up wants to estimate the proportion of urban consumers in Ernakulam district who are willing to pay a **20% premium for organic cold-pressed coconut oil**. The marketing researcher wants **95% confidence** and a **margin of error of ±4 percentage points**. There is no prior estimate, so the most conservative value $p = 0.5$ is used. There are approximately **1,20,000 urban households** in Ernakulam. Calculate the required sample size.

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters

- Confidence level = 95% → $Z = 1.96$
- Estimated proportion $p = 0.5$ (no prior data → maximum variance)
- Margin of error $e = 0.04$
- Population $N = 1,20,000$

### Step 2 — Apply the Cochran Formula

$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$
n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.5) \cdot (0.5)}{(0.04)^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Square the Constants

$$
n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0016}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Numerator

$$
n_0 = \frac{0.9604}{0.0016}
$$

### Step 6 — Divide

$$
n_0 = 600.25
$$

### Step 7 — Round Up (Always)

$$
n_0 \approx 601
$$

### Step 8 — Check Whether the Finite Population Correction (FPC) is Needed

The FPC is used when $n_0 > 5\%$ of $N$.

$$
\frac{601}{1{,}20{,}000} \times 100\% = 0.50\%
$$

Since 0.50% is far less than 5%, **FPC is not required**.

### Step 9 — Adjust for an Expected Response Rate of 25%

To get 601 completed responses at a 25% response rate, the number to be contacted is:

$$
N_{\text{contact}} = \frac{n_0}{RR} = \frac{601}{0.25} = 2404
$$

### Step 10 — Final Answer

> **The researcher must invite approximately 2,404 urban Ernakulam households to obtain 601 completed surveys, ensuring a 95% confidence interval with ±4% margin of error.**

### Interpretation

If the survey yields 301 of 601 respondents saying "Yes" to paying the premium:

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{301}{601} = 0.501
$$

$$
CI = 0.501 \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.501 \cdot 0.499}{601}} = 0.501 \pm 0.040
$$

The **95% confidence interval is (0.461, 0.541)**, i.e., between **46.1% and 54.1%** of urban Ernakulam consumers are willing to pay the 20% premium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common error is to use $e = 4$ instead of $e = 0.04$ in the formula. Margin of error is a **proportion** (0.04), not a percentage (4). The $Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)$ numerator assumes a proportion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing Cronbach's Alpha for a 5-Item Sensory Acceptance Scale

A focus-group-derived 5-item scale measuring *"Acceptance of a New Flavoured Yogurt"* is administered to **30 panellists**. Item variances and total variance are given:

| Item | Variance $s_i^2$ |
|---|---|
| Taste | 0.85 |
| Texture | 0.79 |
| Aroma | 0.92 |
| Appearance | 0.71 |
| Overall Liking | 0.88 |
| **Total $s_t^2$** | **3.45** |

### Step 1 — Sum the Item Variances

$$
\sum s_i^2 = 0.85 + 0.79 + 0.92 + 0.71 + 0.88 = 4.15
$$

### Step 2 — Plug into the Cronbach Formula

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{\sum s_i^2}{s_t^2}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $k = 5$

$$
\alpha = \frac{5}{4} \left(1 - \frac{4.15}{3.45}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Ratio

$$
\frac{4.15}{3.45} = 1.2029
$$

### Step 5 — Subtract from 1

$$
1 - 1.2029 = -0.2029
$$

### Step 6 — Multiply by $k/(k-1)$

$$
\alpha = 1.25 \times (-0.2029) = -0.254
$$

### Step 7 — Diagnose the Result

A **negative Cronbach's Alpha** indicates a **serious problem** — most likely:

1. The item variances are *larger* than the total variance (items correlate poorly with each other).
2. The coding direction of some items is reversed (e.g., "I dislike the taste" is scored positively).
3. The sample is too small ($n=30$ is borderline).

### Step 8 — Corrective Action

- Reverse-score the negatively worded items.
- Drop items with low item-total correlation.
- Re-administer to a larger panel ($n \geq 100$ for stable $\alpha$).

> [!NOTE]
> This example demonstrates that $\alpha$ is a **diagnostic tool**, not a number to be reported blindly. KTU examiners love this kind of interpretation question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Survey vs. Focus Group vs. Observation

| Criterion | Survey | Focus Group | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Quantitative (mostly) | Qualitative (mostly) | Behavioural (quant + qual) |
| **Sample Size** | Large (100–10,000+) | Small (6–10 per group, 3–6 groups) | Variable (10s to 1000s of incidents) |
| **Depth of Insight** | Low–Medium | Very High | Medium–High |
| **Breadth of Insight** | Very High | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Reveals Actual Behaviour?** | No (self-report) | No (self-report, in group) | **Yes** |
| **Susceptible to Social Desirability Bias** | **High** | Very High (group pressure) | Low |
| **Susceptible to Hawthorne Effect** | Low | Low | **High** |
| **Cost per Respondent** | Low | Medium | Medium–High (tech) |
| **Time to Administer** | Short per respondent | Long per session | Long (real-time capture) |
| **Generalisability** | High (if probabilistic) | Low (non-probabilistic) | Medium (setting-dependent) |
| **Statistical Analysis Possible?** | **Yes (extensive)** | Limited (thematic only) | Yes (counts, time-motion) |
| **Best For** | "How many / how often" | "Why / how do they feel" | "What do they actually do" |
| **Typical Food Example** | Quarterly brand tracker | New-flavour brainstorm | Eye-tracking on snack shelf |
| **Research Stage** | Descriptive / Causal | Exploratory | Descriptive / Confirmatory |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "Q-B-D" Triad:** **Q**uantity → **B**ehaviour → **D**eep emotion. Surveys measure *Quantity*, Observations capture *Behaviour*, Focus Groups uncover *Deep emotion and language*.

---

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm — Which Method to Use?

A practical decision flow used by food marketing research agencies:

1. **Is the question new/exploratory?** → **Focus Group** first.
2. **Is the question "how many / how often"?** → **Survey** with probability sampling.
3. **Is the question "what do they actually do"?** → **Observation** in real or simulated context.
4. **Is the decision high-stakes (e.g., ₹100 crore launch)?** → **Triangulate** all three.
