# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define a focus group. List any two advantages of using focus groups in food marketing research.**

**Model Answer:**
A focus group is a qualitative research method in which **6 to 10 carefully selected consumers** participate in a **moderator-led, open discussion** about a food product, brand, or consumption experience, typically lasting **60 to 120 minutes**.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Depth of Insight:** It uncovers the *why* behind consumer choices — emotions, language, memories — that surveys cannot capture.
2. **Group Synergy:** Participants react to each other's comments, producing ideas and reactions that would not emerge in a one-to-one interview, making it especially useful for **new flavour or packaging concept testing**.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Differentiate between naturalistic and controlled observation in food consumer research with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Naturalistic Observation | Controlled Observation |
|---|---|---|
| Setting | Real, natural environment | Simulated or laboratory environment |
| Researcher intervention | None | Some control over stimuli (e.g., shelf layout) |
| Example | CCTV tracking shopper movement in a BigBasket store | Eye-tracking consumers in a mock grocery aisle to test two shelf arrangements |

In **naturalistic** observation, the researcher is a *passive* recorder of real behaviour. In **controlled** observation, the researcher manipulates one variable (e.g., packaging colour, price tag) and observes the effect, but still without asking the consumer questions.

*(1.5 marks for differentiation + 1.5 marks for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question from the choice provided. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **different types of surveys** used in food marketing research. Discuss the merits and limitations of **online surveys** compared with **face-to-face intercept surveys** in the context of Indian urban consumers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A leading South-Indian breakfast-mix brand wants to estimate the proportion of Kerala households that consume ready-to-eat (RTE) idli mixes at least once a month. The marketing team wants a **95% confidence level** and a **margin of error of ±3%**. Assume $p = 0.5$ and the urban-household population of Kerala is approximately **8,00,000**. Calculate the required sample size, decide whether finite population correction is needed, and recommend the number of households to be contacted assuming an expected response rate of **20%**.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Types of Surveys in Food Marketing (3 marks):**

1. **Online/Web Surveys:** Distributed via email, QR code, or app. Ideal for tech-savvy urban consumers and quick turnaround.
2. **Face-to-Face (Intercept) Surveys:** Conducted in-store, at exhibitions, or door-to-door. Useful where literacy or internet access is uneven.
3. **Telephone (CATI) Surveys:** Computer-assisted live interviews. Cost-effective for quick pulse checks.
4. **Mail/Postal Surveys:** Self-completion questionnaires sent by post. Slow but useful for rural and panellist studies.
5. **Mobile/SMS-based Surveys:** WhatsApp and SMS polls for low-income or hard-to-reach segments.

**Online vs. Face-to-Face Intercept Surveys (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Online Survey | Face-to-Face Intercept |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per response** | Very low (₹10–30) | High (₹150–400) |
| **Reach in urban India** | High among 18–45 age | High across all ages |
| **Visual stimuli (product images, ads)** | Easy to embed | Easy to physically show |
| **Response rate** | 10–25% typical | 60–80% typical |
| **Social desirability bias** | Higher (anonymous) | Lower (interviewer present) |
| **Sample bias** | Excludes non-internet users | More representative |
| **Speed** | Days | Weeks |
| **Best suited for** | Concept test, ad recall, post-purchase feedback | In-store trial evaluation, sensory panel, low-literacy segments |

**Conclusion:** For Indian urban food marketing, a **mixed-mode** (online + intercept) design is recommended to balance speed, cost, and representativeness.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $p = 0.5$
- $e = 0.03$
- $N = 8,00,000$

**Step 1 — Apply Cochran's Formula (2 marks):**

$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.03)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
1.96^2 = 3.8416
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.8416 \times 0.25 = 0.9604
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (1 mark):**

$$
0.03^2 = 0.0009
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n_0$ (1 mark):**

$$
n_0 = \frac{0.9604}{0.0009} = 1067.11 \approx 1068
$$

**Step 5 — Finite Population Correction Check (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{1068}{8,00,000} \times 100\% = 0.1335\%
$$

Since 0.13% < 5%, **FPC is not required**.

**Step 6 — Adjust for 20% Response Rate (1 mark):**

$$
N_{\text{contact}} = \frac{1068}{0.20} = 5340
$$

**Final Recommendation:** The brand should contact approximately **5,340 urban Kerala households** to obtain 1,068 completed surveys, ensuring a 95% confidence interval with a ±3% margin of error.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **focus group method** as a qualitative research tool in food marketing. Explain the **role of the moderator** and outline the **structure of a typical 90-minute focus group session on a new flavoured yogurt**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hawthorne Effect** in the context of observational studies in food marketing. Describe **two technological tools** (e.g., eye-tracking, RFID, smart-shelf sensors, CCTV analytics) that minimise this effect and how each contributes to a better understanding of consumer behaviour.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Focus Group Method (3 marks):**
A focus group is a moderated, interactive group discussion of **6 to 10 participants**, used in food marketing primarily for **exploratory research** — understanding consumer feelings, language, and mental models around food products. It is *non-probabilistic*, so it cannot be used to project percentages to a population, but it excels at uncovering the *why* behind behaviour.

**Role of the Moderator (2 marks):**
The moderator is the *trained facilitator* who:
- **Guides** discussion using a pre-tested discussion guide.
- **Probes** for depth ("Can you say more about that?").
- **Manages group dynamics** — ensuring quieter voices are heard, dominant voices are balanced.
- **Stays neutral** — does not reveal personal opinions or react to participant comments.
- **Uses projective techniques** — e.g., "If this yogurt were a person, how would you describe them?" — to bypass rational filters.

**Structure of a 90-Minute Session on a New Flavoured Yogurt (2 marks):**

| Time | Segment | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 0–10 min | **Welcome & Warm-up** | Introduction, ground rules, icebreaker on general snacking habits. |
| 10–25 min | **Stimulus Exposure** | Blind taste of 3 flavours; then branded tasting; package handling. |
| 25–70 min | **Guided Discussion** | Perception of taste, texture, sweetness, healthiness, price perception, brand fit, occasions of consumption. |
| 70–85 min | **Probing & Projectives** | "If this yogurt were a celebrity…" / "Where would you see yourself eating this?" |
| 85–90 min | **Wrap-up & Demographic Card** | Final thoughts, thank-you, demographic capture. |

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**The Hawthorne Effect (3 marks):**
The **Hawthorne Effect** is the phenomenon whereby consumers **modify their behaviour** when they know they are being observed. In a food-marketing context, a shopper who knows a researcher is watching may pick up the "healthy" cereal, even though their habitual choice is the sugary one. This distorts the validity of observational findings.

**Technological Tools that Minimise the Hawthorne Effect (4 marks):**

1. **CCTV with AI Video Analytics (1.5 marks):**
   Small, ceiling-mounted cameras record the entire aisle without the shopper's awareness. Modern AI algorithms count pickups, dwell time, gaze direction, and even demographic estimation. The shopper behaves naturally, so the Hawthorne Effect is virtually eliminated. Output: heat maps, sequence diagrams, and SKU-level interaction counts.

2. **RFID-Enabled Smart Shelves and Trolleys (1.5 marks):**
   Each product carries an RFID tag. The smart shelf logs every *lift*, *replace*, and *time on shelf* automatically. The shopper does not see any human observer and the tags are invisible, so behaviour is natural. Output: pick-up-to-purchase ratio, decision time, and substitution patterns.

3. **Bonus: Mobile Eye-Tracker Glasses (1 mark — optional mention):**
   Wearable eye-trackers record gaze as the shopper walks the store. Although the device is *visible*, the shopper quickly forgets it, and the technology captures **fixation maps** showing exactly which part of a package attracts attention. This is a partially-minimised Hawthorne Effect.

**Conclusion:** Combining invisible sensors (CCTV + RFID) with wearable biometrics (eye-tracking) gives food marketers a **multi-layered, low-bias** picture of actual consumer behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the Hawthorne Effect (behaviour change due to observation) with the *Placebo Effect* (behaviour change due to expectation of benefit). They are different concepts.
> 2. **Do NOT claim** observation proves causation. Observation reveals *patterns*, not *cause-effect* relationships. Only an **experiment** (with manipulated IV) can establish causation.
> 3. **In sample-size problems**, students frequently forget to **round up** the calculated $n_0$. Always round *up* — you cannot survey 0.25 of a person.
> 4. **Cronbach's Alpha ≠ Validity.** A high $\alpha$ only proves internal consistency, not that the scale measures the *correct* construct.
> 5. **Focus groups are NOT for voting.** Mentioning "X% of focus-group participants said yes" is a classic deduction in Part B answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Surveys** → **Structured**, **quantitative**, **large sample**, useful for "how many / how often" questions. Use **Cochran's formula** for sample size.
- **Focus Groups** → **Unstructured**, **qualitative**, **6–10 per group**, useful for "why / how do they feel" questions. Moderator is the engine. **Not for voting.**
- **Observational Studies** → **Behavioural**, **non-reactive** (ideally), useful for "what do they actually do" questions. Vulnerable to **Hawthorne Effect**. Use **Cohen's Kappa** for inter-rater reliability.
- **Triangulation** = combining all three to obtain a **360°** view of the food consumer.
- **Cronbach's Alpha ≥ 0.70** = acceptable scale reliability (not validity).
- **Z = 1.96** for 95% confidence, **Z = 2.58** for 99% confidence, **Z = 1.65** for 90% confidence.
- **Always round up** the sample size, never down.
- **Margin of error is a proportion** (0.05), not a percentage (5) in the formula.
- **Likert scale** is the most common survey measurement in food-acceptance studies.
- **Focus group saturation** is usually reached after **3–5 groups per cell**.
- **Eye-tracking** reveals that consumers spend **< 5 seconds** viewing a product on a cluttered shelf.
- **Smart shelves + RFID + CCTV** are the modern triad for unobtrusive food-consumer observation.
- **Triangulation rule:** Survey for *breadth*, Focus Group for *depth*, Observation for *authenticity*.
- **Final KTU exam mnemonic:** **"S-F-O = Say-Feel-Do"** → Survey = Say, Focus Group = Feel, Observation = Do.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demographic, Psychographic, and Behavioural Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Demographic, Psychographic, and Behavioural Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Market Segmentation** is the strategic process of dividing a broad consumer market into distinct subsets of buyers who share common needs, characteristics, or behaviours, and who might require separate marketing mixes or food-product positioning strategies. Within Module 3 of Consumer Behavior in Food Marketing, segmentation is operationalised through three interlocking lenses:

1. **Demographic Segmentation** — grouping consumers on the basis of *objective, measurable population descriptors* such as **age**, **gender**, **income**, **education**, **occupation**, **family life cycle (FLC)**, **religion**, and **ethnicity/nationality**.
2. **Psychographic Segmentation** — grouping consumers on the basis of *psychological attributes* such as **lifestyle**, **values**, **attitudes**, **interests**, **personality**, and **social class (A, B, C1, C2, D, E in the Indian SEC grid)**.
3. **Behavioural Segmentation** — grouping consumers on the basis of *actual consumption-related behaviours* such as **occasions**, **benefits sought**, **user status**, **usage rate**, **loyalty status**, **readiness stage**, and **attitude toward the food product**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In food marketing, behavioural segmentation is considered the *most actionable* of the three, because food purchases are highly situational (festivals, fasting days, time-of-day) and benefit-driven (taste, health, convenience, indulgence).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a busy **Kerala tea-stall that also sells juice, packaged snacks, and diet shakes**. The owner cannot sell everything to everyone the same way. He mentally groups his morning crowd into:

- **Demographic view:** "Office-going men aged 25–40, middle income" vs. "college students aged 18–22."
- **Psychographic view:** "Health-conscious yoga attendees" vs. "Indulgence-seeking weekend snackers."
- **Behavioural view:** "Daily loyal chai regulars" vs. "Occasional festive buyers of *payasam* mix."

That mental sorting — done formally — *is* segmentation. The three lenses differ only in **what attribute** we use to draw the boundary around the customer.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Food-Segmentation Research

| Metric | Standard Value / Scale | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **NRSS (New Readership Survey) – SEC grid** | A, B, C1, C2, D, E | Indian socio-economic classification |
| **VALS™ (Values & Lifestyles)** | 8 types (Innovators, Thinkers, Achievers, Experiencers, Believers, Strivers, Makers, Survivors) | Psychographic typing |
| **FLC Stage** | Bachelor → Newly Married → Full Nest I/II/III → Empty Nest → Solitary Survivor | Demographic life-stage |
| **Usage Rate Index (URI)** | $\text{URI} = \dfrac{\text{Consumer's brand usage}}{\text{Average category usage}}$ | Behavioural heaviness |

> [!NOTE]
> All three segmentation bases are *complementary*, not alternatives. KTU valuation expects students to state which base is appropriate for *which* food-product category (e.g., demographic for baby food, psychographic for premium chocolate, behavioural for instant noodles).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Demographic Segmentation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the demographic variables relevant to the food product.**
Food consumption is biologically and economically tied to demographics. For example, *infant formula* → age & parental education; *organic quinoa* → income & education.

**Step 2 — Build a demographic profile of the target segment.**
This converts raw census data into a *persona*: "Urban Indian female, 28–35, postgraduate, household income ≥ ₹12 lakh p.a., nuclear family with one child."

**Step 3 — Size the segment using a population pyramid or census grid.**
$$\text{Segment Size} = \text{Total Population} \times \text{Proportion satisfying demographic filters}$$

**Step 4 — Decide accessibility (can we reach them via supermarkets, e-commerce, kirana?).**

**Step 5 — Match the 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) to the demographic profile.**

> **Why it works:** Demographics are *observable, measurable, and stable*. They form the base layer on which psychographic and behavioural overlays are built.

## 2.2 Psychographic Segmentation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Choose a psychographic typology** (VALS, AIO model — Activities/Interests/Opinions, or Indian-specific frameworks like the *Lovals* and *Ethicals* typology).

**Step 2 — Conduct primary research** using Likert-scale questionnaires, focus groups, or projective techniques (e.g., "What does breakfast mean to you?").

**Step 3 — Apply factor analysis or cluster analysis** to group consumers with similar psychological profiles.

**Step 4 — Translate each cluster into a marketing-relevant archetype** (e.g., *Conscious Eater*, *Convenience Seeker*, *Traditional Indulger*).

**Step 5 — Position the food product around the *dominant psychographic driver* of the chosen cluster.**

> **Why it matters for food:** Two consumers of identical demographics can have *vastly different* food choices — a vegan vs. a keto follower both aged 30, both earning ₹10 lakh, are demographically identical but psychographically polar.

## 2.3 Behavioural Segmentation — Step-by-Step Logic

Behavioural segmentation is built on **what consumers do**, not who they are.

**Step 1 — Choose the behavioural variable** (occasion, benefit, user status, usage rate, loyalty, readiness, attitude).

**Step 2 — Operationalise the variable into measurable categories.**
Example for *occasion* in Kerala: Onam, Vishu, Ramadan Iftar, weekday breakfast, Sunday family lunch.

**Step 3 — Measure the variable** through loyalty-card data, sales records, or survey questions such as "How often do you buy *X*?"

**Step 4 — Quantify segment profitability** using the *80/20 principle* (Pareto) — typically 20% of heavy users drive 80% of category sales in FMCG food.

**Step 5 — Design segment-specific marketing actions** (loyalty programme for heavy users, sampling for non-users, festive packs for occasion-buyers).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Model | Key Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)** | $\text{SAS} = w_1 \cdot S + w_2 \cdot P + w_3 \cdot A + w_4 \cdot C$ | $S$ = size, $P$ = profit margin, $A$ = accessibility, $C$ = competition level; $w_i$ weights with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ |
| 2 | **Usage Rate Index (URI)** | $\text{URI} = \dfrac{\text{Consumer's brand usage}}{\text{Average category usage}}$ | URI > 100 = heavy user; URI < 100 = light user |
| 3 | **Brand Loyalty Index (BLI)** | $\text{BLI} = \dfrac{\text{Purchases of focal brand}}{\text{Total category purchases}} \times 100$ | BLI ≥ 70 = loyal; 40–69 = split; < 40 = switcher |
| 4 | **Segment Profitability** | $\text{SP} = \text{Segment Size} \times \text{Avg. Revenue/Buyer} \times \text{Contribution Margin \%}$ | Used in KTU numericals on food-FMCG cases |
| 5 | **AIO Composite Score (Psychographic)** | $\text{AIO Score} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Likert ratings on A, I, O items}}{n}$ | Scale 1–5; cluster analysis follows |
| 6 | **Pareto Ratio for Heavy Users** | $\text{Pareto Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{\% Sales from heavy users}}{\text{\% Heavy users in population}}$ | > 1 = heavy users over-contribute |
| 7 | **Family Life Cycle (FLC) Stage Index** | $\text{FLC Stage} = f(\text{Age}, \text{Marital Status}, \text{No. of Children}, \text{Employment of spouse})$ | Each stage = distinct food consumption pattern |
| 8 | **VALS / SEC Grid** | Categorical (no formula) | Indian SEC: A, B, C1, C2, D, E |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always define the variable, write the formula with units/meaning, and then apply it to a food-marketing example**. The valuation key allocates 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, and the remaining marks for application.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food-Marketing Engineering

- **Product Development:** Demographic + psychographic overlays drive new SKU launches (e.g., low-sodium ready-meals for hypertensive seniors in SEC A).
- **Pricing Strategy:** Income-based demographic pricing tiers for organic produce; benefit-based behavioural pricing for "protein-rich" or "sugar-free" claims.
- **Distribution (Place):** Behavioural occasion-based distribution (cold coffee in malls, payasam mix near Onam).
- **Promotion:** Psychographic appeal via influencer marketing (e.g., keto-influencer for low-carb snacks).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Application Derivations

> Since food-marketing segmentation is a behavioural science application rather than a closed-form physics derivation, the "derivation" below is presented as an **analytical decision matrix** showing the full logical flow from raw consumer data to actionable marketing decision — the same depth expected in a 14-mark KTU Part B answer.

## 3.1 Master Analytical Matrix — Variable → Measurement → Action

### 3.1.1 DEMOGRAPHIC SEGMENTATION — Detailed Variable Matrix

| Variable | Measurement Scale | Food-Marketing Decision Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| **Age** | Numeric (years) / Cohort bands (Gen Z, Millennial, Gen X, Boomer) | Baby food, school snacks, geriatric nutrition, soft-textured foods |
| **Gender** | Nominal (M / F / Other) | Iron-fortified products for women; protein shakes for men |
| **Income** | Monthly/Annual ₹; SEC grid A-E | Premium vs. mass-market pricing; pack-size optimisation |
| **Education** | Years of schooling / Degree tier | Health-claim communication complexity (educated buyers accept technical claims) |
| **Occupation** | White-collar / Blue-collar / Self-employed / Student | Convenience foods for working women; on-the-go packs for students |
| **Family Size** | Number of members | Bulk packs (5 kg Atta) vs. single-serve (30 g oats) |
| **Family Life Cycle (FLC)** | Bachelor / Newly Married / Full Nest I-III / Empty Nest / Solitary | Cookies for Full Nest I; diet foods for Empty Nest; comfort foods for Solitary |
| **Religion / Ethnicity** | Categorical | Halal certification, Jain (no onion-garlic), Christian (Christmas cakes), Hindu (sattvic) |
| **Geography / Nationality** | Urban-Semiurban-Rural; State/Country | Regional flavours (Kerala banana chips vs. Tamil Nadu murukku) |

**Logical Transition (textual derivation step):**

Let the demographic vector for consumer $i$ be:

$$
D_i = [a_i, g_i, y_i, e_i, o_i, n_i, \text{FLC}_i, r_i, l_i]
$$

where $a$ = age, $g$ = gender, $y$ = income, $e$ = education, $o$ = occupation, $n$ = family size, FLC = life-cycle stage, $r$ = religion, $l$ = location.

Step 1: For a chosen product *P* (e.g., infant cereal), we filter the population:

$$
S_{\text{demo}} = \{i \in \text{Population} \mid a_i \in [0,3], \; y_i > y_{\min}, \; e_i > e_{\min}, \; n_i \geq 2\}
$$

Step 2: The size of the demographic target segment is:

$$
\vert S_{\text{demo}} \vert = N \cdot P(a \in [0,3]) \cdot P(y > y_{\min}) \cdot P(n \geq 2)
$$

assuming independence (or we use census joint-distribution tables in practice).

Step 3: Revenue potential estimate:

$$
R_{\text{demo}} = \vert S_{\text{demo}} \vert \cdot \text{ASP} \cdot \text{Purchase Frequency}
$$

where ASP = average selling price.

This is the standard KTU-style demographic-targeting derivation.

### 3.1.2 PSYCHOGRAPHIC SEGMENTATION — Detailed Variable Matrix

| Variable | Measurement Instrument | Food-Marketing Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifestyle** (AIO model) | Likert-scale AIO statements | On-the-go bars for active lifestyles; gourmet kits for home-chef lifestyle |
| **Values** (e.g., Schwartz values) | Schwartz Value Survey (SVS) | Sustainability → eco-packaging; tradition → heritage food brands |
| **Attitudes** | Semantic-differential scales | Attitude toward "health", "indulgence", "purity" drives claim positioning |
| **Personality (Big Five)** | NEO-PI or BFI-44 | Openness → novel flavours; Conscientiousness → nutrition tracking |
| **Social Class (SEC)** | Occupation + Education of Chief Wage Earner | Premium imported cheese for A/B; value brand rice for D/E |
| **VALS / LOVALS type** | SRI VALS questionnaire | Innovators/Achievers → exotic superfoods; Believers → traditional foods |

**Logical Transition (textual derivation step):**

A consumer's psychographic profile is constructed as:

$$
P_i = [L_i, V_i, A_i, \text{BigFive}_i, \text{SEC}_i, \text{VALS}_i]
$$

Step 1: Apply **factor analysis** to reduce many AIO items into 4–6 latent factors (e.g., *Health-Concern*, *Indulgence-Seeking*, *Price-Sensitivity*, *Tradition-Anchored*).

Step 2: Apply **k-means cluster analysis** to group consumers into $k$ psychographic segments (typically 4–7 in food-marketing practice).

Step 3: Label each cluster with a descriptive archetype.

Step 4: The chosen archetype becomes the **target psychographic segment** for positioning.

**Example derivation — Identifying a "Health-Conscious Urban Millennial" segment:**

$$
S_{\text{psycho}} = \{i \mid \text{Health-Concern score}_i > \mu_H + \sigma_H, \; \text{Indulgence score}_i < \mu_I, \; \text{VALS type}_i = \text{Achiever/Experiencier}\}
$$

This psychographic slice is the basis for marketing *Greek yogurt with probiotics, low-sugar muesli, kombucha*, etc.

### 3.1.3 BEHAVIOURAL SEGMENTATION — Detailed Variable Matrix

| Variable | Measurement | Food-Marketing Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **Occasions** | Time, event, ritual | Onam sadya pack, Ramadan iftar thali, back-to-school lunchbox |
| **Benefits Sought** | Conjoint analysis or attribute ranking | Taste, health, convenience, freshness, price |
| **User Status** | Non-user / Ex-user / Potential / First-time / Regular | Sampling for non-users; reactivation offers for ex-users |
| **Usage Rate** | Heavy / Medium / Light / Non-user | Loyalty rewards for heavy users |
| **Loyalty Status** | Hard-core loyal / Split loyal / Shifting / Switcher | Retention programme for hard-core; win-back for switchers |
| **Readiness Stage** | Unaware / Aware / Interested / Intending / Ready to buy | Awareness ads → trial coupons funnel |
| **Attitude Toward Product** | Enthusiastic / Positive / Indifferent / Negative / Hostile | Enthusiasts = brand ambassadors; Hostile = crisis PR |

**Logical Transition (textual derivation step):**

Behavioural segment of consumer $i$:

$$
B_i = [\text{Occ}_i, \text{Benefit}_i, \text{UserStatus}_i, \text{UsageRate}_i, \text{Loyalty}_i, \text{Readiness}_i, \text{Attitude}_i]
$$

Step 1: Pareto-based heavy-user identification:

$$
H = \{i \mid \text{URI}_i > 100\}
$$

Step 2: The "80/20 rule" states:

$$
\sum_{i \in H} \text{Sales}_i \approx 0.8 \cdot \text{Total Category Sales}
$$

Step 3: ROI of loyalty programme for heavy users:

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Revenue from } H - \text{Programme Cost}}{\text{Programme Cost}}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
If a dairy brand has 10,000 customers, 20% are heavy users (URI > 100), contributing ₹80 lakh of the ₹1 crore annual sales, and a loyalty programme costs ₹5 lakh but increases heavy-user sales by 12%:

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(80 \text{ lakh} \times 0.12) - 5 \text{ lakh}}{5 \text{ lakh}} = \dfrac{9.6 - 5}{5} = 0.92 = 92\%
$$

This is a positive ROI → programme justified.

## 3.2 Integrated Three-Layer Segmentation (Synthesis)

In a real food-marketing plan, the three bases are applied **hierarchically** (demographic → psychographic → behavioural), as the following structured flow shows:

1. **Layer 1 (Demographic):** Filter population to a sizeable, accessible cohort (e.g., urban women, 25–40, SEC A/B).
2. **Layer 2 (Psychographic):** Within that cohort, identify the lifestyle/values cluster most receptive (e.g., "Wellness-Driven Achievers").
3. **Layer 3 (Behavioural):** Within that cluster, find the heaviest users or the most profitable occasion (e.g., weekday-evening smoothie buyers).

The **targeting decision** is then made on Layer 3 outputs, but *positioning* and *creative messaging* use Layer 2 insights, and *media planning* uses Layer 1 data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Bases of Segmentation — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Consumer Market] --> B[Demographic Segmentation]
    A --> C[Psychographic Segmentation]
    A --> D[Behavioural Segmentation]
    B --> B1[Age]
    B --> B2[Gender]
    B --> B3[Income]
    B --> B4[Education]
    B --> B5[FLC Stage]
    B --> B6[Religion]
    C --> C1[Lifestyle - AIO]
    C --> C2[Values]
    C --> C3[Attitudes]
    C --> C4[Personality]
    C --> C5[SOCIAL CLASS - SEC]
    D --> D1[Occasions]
    D --> D2[Benefits Sought]
    D --> D3[User Status]
    D --> D4[Usage Rate]
    D --> D5[Loyalty Status]
    D --> D6[Attitude]
    B1 --> Z[Food Marketing Mix 4Ps]
    B2 --> Z
    B3 --> Z
    B4 --> Z
    B5 --> Z
    B6 --> Z
    C1 --> Z
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    C4 --> Z
    C5 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    D2 --> Z
    D3 --> Z
    D4 --> Z
    D5 --> Z
    D6 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Layered Segmentation Flow (Demographic → Psychographic → Behavioural)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Total Food Market Population]) --> L1[Layer 1: Demographic Filter]
    L1 --> L1a[Age and Gender]
    L1 --> L1b[Income and SEC]
    L1 --> L1c[Geography and FLC]
    L1 --> L1out[Demographic Cohort]
    L1out --> L2[Layer 2: Psychographic Filter]
    L2 --> L2a[AIO and Lifestyle]
    L2 --> L2b[Values and Attitudes]
    L2 --> L2c[VALS Type]
    L2 --> L2out[Psychographic Archetype]
    L2out --> L3[Layer 3: Behavioural Filter]
    L3 --> L3a[Occasion and Benefit]
    L3 --> L3b[Usage Rate and Loyalty]
    L3 --> L3c[Readiness Stage]
    L3 --> L3out[Target Food Marketing Segment]
    L3out --> Plan[4Ps Mix and Positioning]
```

## 4.3 Behavioural Segmentation — Decision Tree for Food Brands

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B[Behavioural Variable] --> O[Occasion]
    B --> Ben[Benefit Sought]
    B --> U[User Status]
    B --> UR[Usage Rate]
    B --> L[Loyalty]
    B --> R[Readiness]
    B --> Att[Attitude]
    O --> O1[Daily Use Item]
    O --> O2[Festive or Seasonal Pack]
    O --> O3[Special Day Gifting]
    Ben --> Be1[Taste Indulgence]
    Ben --> Be2[Health and Nutrition]
    Ben --> Be3[Convenience and Speed]
    U --> U1[Non-User]
    U --> U2[Ex-User]
    U --> U3[Potential]
    U --> U4[First-Time]
    U --> U5[Regular]
    UR --> UR1[Heavy - URI over 100]
    UR --> UR2[Medium - URI 50 to 100]
    UR --> UR3[Light - URI under 50]
    L --> L1[Hard-Core Loyal - BLI over 70]
    L --> L2[Split Loyal - BLI 40 to 70]
    L --> L3[Switcher - BLI under 40]
    R --> R1[Aware but Not Interested]
    R --> R2[Aware and Interested]

    R --> R4[Ready to Purchase]

```

    V1[Innovators] --> F1[Exotic Superfoods, Global Cuisine]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**"Differentiate between demographic and psychographic segmentation with two food-product examples for each."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Demographic segmentation** groups consumers on the basis of *objective, measurable* population descriptors — age, gender, income, education, occupation, family life cycle, religion, nationality. *(1 mark)*
- **Psychographic segmentation** groups consumers on the basis of *psychological attributes* — lifestyle, values, attitudes, personality, and social class. *(1 mark)*
- **Food examples:** *Demographic* → Infant formula (target: parents 25–35, SEC A/B); geriatric nutrition drinks (target: 60+, low-sodium). *Psychographic* → Keto snacks (target: health-driven, low-carb value orientation); artisanal cheese (target: lifestyle-aspirational Achievers). *(1 mark for the paired examples)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**"List any six variables used in behavioural segmentation of food consumers and state one marketing action for each."**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
1. **Occasion** → Launch Onam-only payasam pack *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Benefits Sought** → Position oats as "heart-healthy" benefit cluster *(0.5 mark)*
3. **User Status** → Free sample to first-time buyers *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Usage Rate** → Loyalty points to heavy users of ketchup *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Loyalty Status** → Win-back discount for switchers from brand A to brand B *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Readiness Stage** → Awareness ads for "unaware" of new plant-protein milk *(0.5 mark)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Past Paper Style]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **demographic segmentation** in food marketing. Discuss the role of **age, income, and family life cycle (FLC)** in designing a food product portfolio, with suitable Indian food examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **psychographic segmentation** using the **VALS framework**. Illustrate how a food company would target two different VALS groups with contrasting positioning strategies.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Demographic segmentation is the division of a market into groups based on demographic variables — *age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size, family life cycle, religion, and ethnicity*. These variables are observable, quantifiable, and directly related to demand for food products.

**Role of Age (1.5 marks):**
Age determines nutritional need, taste preference, and consumption occasion. *Examples:* Complan/Boost target children 6–14 (growth drinks); Horlicks targets adolescents; geriatric food (low-sodium, easy-to-chew) targets 60+. Marketers design *age-specific packaging, flavour profiles, and media plans* (cartoon mascots for kids, health-claim ads for elders).

**Role of Income (1.5 marks):**
Income — operationalised as the **SEC grid (A, B, C1, C2, D, E)** in India — decides the **price tier** and **pack size**. *Examples:* SEC A/B (₹>₹25k/month household income) is targeted by imported olive oil, exotic cheese, and organic quinoa. SEC C/D (₹5k–₹20k) is targeted by mass-market Atta, refined oil, and biscuits. Marketers use *price discrimination by pack size* (e.g., ₹5, ₹10, ₹20 SKU ladder).

**Role of FLC (2 marks):**
The Family Life Cycle stages (Bachelor, Newly Married, Full Nest I/II/III, Empty Nest, Solitary Survivor) correspond to *distinct food baskets*.

- *Bachelor:* single-serve noodles, ready-to-eat meals.
- *Newly Married:* premium cooking ingredients, gourmet cooking kits.
- *Full Nest I (child < 6):* baby food, toddler snacks, fortified Atta.
- *Full Nest II/III (school-going children):* lunchbox items, milk, breakfast cereals.
- *Empty Nest:* smaller pack sizes, diet/health food.
- *Solitary Survivor:* easy-to-cook, single-serve, soft-textured foods.

**Conclusion (0 mark reserved):** Marketers combine the three variables into a *demographic persona* to size the addressable market and design the 4 Ps.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **— Question A(b)**

**VALS Framework Recap (2 marks):**
VALS (Values and Lifestyles), developed by SRI International, classifies consumers into 8 types based on resources and self-orientation: *Innovators, Thinkers, Achievers, Experiencers, Believers, Strivers, Makers, Survivors*.

**VALS Target 1 — Achievers (2.5 marks):**
- *Profile:* Goal-oriented, status-conscious, brand-loyal, high disposable income.
- *Food positioning strategy:* Launch a **premium range of imported olive oil, exotic cheese, and wine-pairing chocolates**.
- *Marketing mix:* Price = premium tier; Place = upscale supermarkets (Foodhall, Nature's Basket); Promotion = lifestyle magazine ads showing aspirational dinner-party imagery; Product = sleek glass-bottle packaging, country-of-origin certifications.

**VALS Target 2 — Survivors (2.5 marks):**
- *Profile:* Resource-constrained, brand-loyal to low-cost staples, focused on basic needs.
- *Food positioning strategy:* Launch a **value-tier atta-rice-dal combo pack at ₹99 with a free salt sachet**.
- *Marketing mix:* Price = sub-₹100; Place = kirana + PDS; Promotion = wall-painting and regional-language TV; Product = large family pack, no-frills packaging, fortified with iron (public-health benefit).

**Conclusion (held):** The same food company can target both VALS extremes with *different SKUs, different price points, and different emotional appeals* — proving that psychographic segmentation unlocks *premiumisation and value-for-money* strategies simultaneously.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Past Paper Style]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **behavioural segmentation**. Critically analyse the **"80/20 rule"** as applied to heavy users in food marketing, with a numerical illustration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** "Food purchase decisions are occasion-driven." Discuss with reference to **festivals (Onam, Ramadan, Christmas), time-of-day, and location-based occasions** in the Indian food market.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Behavioural segmentation is the division of a market into groups based on **consumers' actual behaviours** toward a product — occasions of use, benefits sought, user status, usage rate, loyalty, readiness stage, and attitude.

**The 80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle) (2 marks):**
In food marketing, it has been consistently observed that approximately **20% of consumers account for 80% of category sales**. These 20% are the *heavy users* (URI > 100). They are the most profitable, the most brand-loyal, and the most cost-efficient to retain (acquisition cost > retention cost by 5–7×).

**Numerical Illustration (3 marks):**
A south-Indian breakfast-mix brand has 50,000 active buyers. Market research gives:

- Heavy users (URI > 100): 10,000 buyers (20% of base) → contribute 80% of sales.
- Annual sales = ₹10 crore → heavy users = ₹8 crore; light/medium users = ₹2 crore.

If a loyalty programme costs ₹50 lakh/year and is offered *only* to heavy users, and it lifts heavy-user sales by 10%:

$$
\text{Incremental Revenue} = 8 \text{ crore} \times 0.10 = ₹80 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
\text{Net Gain} = 80 \text{ lakh} - 50 \text{ lakh} = ₹30 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

→ Programme is **financially justified** to retain the heavy-user base.

**Critical Caveat (0.5 mark):**
The 80/20 split is *empirical*, not a law. In some categories it is 70/30, in others 90/10. Marketers must re-measure per category.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **— Question B(b)**

**Premise (1 mark):**
Food, unlike durables, is consumed multiple times a day, and purchase is *triggered by a specific occasion* — a meal-time, a festival, a location, or a social context. Hence behavioural segmentation by **occasion** is uniquely powerful in food marketing.

**Festive Occasions (2 marks):**
- *Onam (Kerala):* Sales of *payasam mix, banana chips, traditional pickles* spike 4–6× in the 2 weeks pre-Onam. Marketers launch *Onam Sadya Combo Packs* with limited-edition packaging.
- *Ramadan:* Iftar-focused foods — *dates, chana, fruit-based drinks, haleem mix* — are heavily advertised post-sunset. Halal-certified, pre-dusk consumption-marked SKUs.
- *Christmas / Easter:* Plum cakes, marzipan, wine, cold cuts — concentrated in Christian-majority belts (Kerala, Goa, NE India).

**Time-of-Day Occasions (2 marks):**
- *Breakfast (6–9 AM):* Cereal, oats, bread, eggs — convenience-led.
- *Lunch (12–2 PM):* Office lunchbox, ready-to-eat, home-cooked.
- *Snack (4–6 PM):* Namkeen, biscuits, tea-time snacks.
- *Dinner (7–10 PM):* Ready-meals, family-pack curries.
- *Late-night (10 PM+):* Indulgent items (ice-cream, instant noodles).

**Location-Based Occasions (1.5 marks):**
- *Travel occasion:* Packaged water, chips, sandwiches at railway stations/airports.
- *Workplace occasion:* Subscription-tiffin, vending-machine coffee.
- *Home-leisure occasion:* Frozen food, popcorn for movie night.

**Strategic Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Occasion-based marketing lets brands *own* a specific consumption moment, increasing mental availability and justifying a price premium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. **Confusing "demographic" with "psychographic"** — students often write "lifestyle" as a demographic variable. KTU values *correct taxonomy* strictly; lose 1–2 marks per such error.
> 2. **Listing variables without food-marketing examples** — the KTU key wants *applied* answers, not textbook definitions. Always pair a variable with a *named Indian food category*.
> 3. **In 14-mark answers, omitting the "80/20" numerical illustration** in behavioural questions — examiners allocate ~3 marks specifically for the worked example; skipping it costs heavily.
> 4. **Writing VALS as "8 segments" but only naming 2–3** — you must list all 8 (Innovators, Thinkers, Achievers, Experiencers, Believers, Strivers, Makers, Survivors) for full credit.
> 5. **No internal choice practised** — in KTU 2024 ESE, Part B has internal choice. Always present **two** full alternative questions if you are an examiner, and **attempt one completely** as a student.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three bases of segmentation** — Demographic (who they *are*), Psychographic (how they *think*), Behavioural (what they *do*). All three are complementary.
- **Demographic variables to memorise for KTU:** age, gender, income (SEC grid), education, occupation, family size, FLC stage, religion, geography.
- **Psychographic variables to memorise:** lifestyle (AIO), values, attitudes, personality (Big Five), social class, VALS type.
- **Behavioural variables to memorise:** occasions, benefits sought, user status, usage rate, loyalty, readiness stage, attitude toward product.
- **Formulas to keep ready:** $\text{SAS}$, $\text{URI}$, $\text{BLI}$, $\text{AIO Score}$, $\text{Segment Profitability} = \vert S \vert \times \text{ARPU} \times \text{CM\%}$.
- **The 80/20 Pareto rule** — ~20% of consumers (heavy users, URI > 100) generate ~80% of food-category sales; this is the *core commercial logic* of behavioural segmentation.
- **VALS has 8 types** — be able to map each to a *food-product example* and to a *positioning strategy*.
- **FLC stages and food baskets** — bachelor, newly married, full nest I/II/III, empty nest, solitary survivor; each demands a different portfolio.
- **Hierarchical segmentation** — demographic filters the *population*, psychographic filters the *mindset*, behavioural filters the *purchase*; in practice, always use all three in that order.
- **Indian context to weave in:** SEC grid, AIO scale adapted for Indian food culture, Onam/Ramadan/Christmas occasion examples, urban-rural divide.
- **Numericals to expect:** A SAS computation, a URI / BLI calculation, an 80/20 loyalty programme ROI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying and Analyzing Target Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identifying and Analyzing Target Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Target Market Identification and Analysis** is the strategic marketing process of dividing a heterogeneous consumer market into distinct, homogeneous subgroups (segmentation), evaluating these groups against organizational objectives and resources (targeting), and ultimately selecting one or more segments to serve with a tailored marketing mix (positioning). In food marketing, this process is critical because consumer food preferences are influenced by a unique blend of biological, psychological, cultural, and situational factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Target market analysis in food marketing involves the systematic identification, evaluation, and selection of specific consumer groups whose needs, preferences, and buying behavior align with a food organization's product offerings, value proposition, and strategic capabilities.

The complete process is governed by the **STP Framework**:
- **S** – Segmentation
- **T** – Targeting
- **P** – Positioning

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fisherman's Approach:** Imagine a fisherman in Kerala who wants to catch fish. Instead of throwing a single net into the entire backwater, the fisherman observes: "Where do Karimeen (Pearl Spot) gather? Near the aquatic plants in shallow regions. Where do Ayala (Mackerel) swim? In deeper, cooler waters." The fisherman then **identifies** the specific fish (target market), **analyzes** their behavior (feeding time, water conditions, bait preference), and **positions** the right bait at the right depth. This is exactly how a food company like *Nandini* or *Milma* identifies and analyzes its target markets.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standard Metrics

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market):** The total revenue opportunity available if 100% market share were achieved.
- **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market):** The segment of TAM that is realistically reachable.
- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market):** The portion of SAM that the company plans to capture.
- **STP (Segmentation-Targeting-Positioning):** The standard **3-step** strategic marketing framework.
- **B2C vs B2B:** Consumer-facing markets versus institutional buyers (e.g., hotels, hospitals).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Target Market Analysis

**Pillar 1: Market Segmentation**
Segmentation is the decomposition of a broad market into sub-markets based on shared characteristics. In food marketing, segmentation variables are categorized as follows:

- **Geographic Variables:** Region (Kerala, Tamil Nadu), climate, population density, urban vs rural.
- **Demographic Variables:** Age (infants, children, adults, seniors), gender, income, family size, life cycle stage.
- **Psychographic Variables:** Lifestyle (health-conscious, traditional), values, personality, social class.
- **Behavioral Variables:** Occasions (festivals like Onam, daily use, gifting), benefits sought (convenience, nutrition, taste), loyalty status, usage rate.

**Pillar 2: Target Market Evaluation**
Each identified segment must be evaluated using the **5 Cs Criteria**:
1. **Customer Needs:** Does the segment have distinct, identifiable needs?
2. **Company Objectives:** Does the segment align with the firm's mission?
3. **Competition:** Is the segment competitively attractive?
4. **Cost:** Can the company profitably serve the segment?
5. **Compatibility:** Is the segment compatible with company resources and capabilities?

**Pillar 3: Positioning**
Positioning is the act of designing the firm's offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the target consumer's mind. The **Perceptual Mapping** technique is used to visualize positioning.

### 2.2 Target Market Selection Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Food Marketing Example |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Undifferentiated** | Single marketing mix for the whole market | Basic iodized salt |
| **Differentiated** | Separate marketing mix for multiple segments | Amul — Butter, Cheese, Paneer, Ice Cream |
| **Concentrated (Niche)** | Focus on a single, narrow segment | Organic baby food for urban mothers |
| **Micromarketing** | Tailored to individuals/local areas | Custom diet meal kits in Kochi |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following mathematical formulations are standard KTU 2024 exam-ready formulas for market sizing and attractiveness scoring.**

$$
\text{Market Size (Units)} = \text{Number of Buyers} \times \text{Average Quantity Purchased per Buyer}
$$

$$
\text{Market Size (Value)} = \text{Number of Buyers} \times \text{Average Price per Unit} \times \text{Average Quantity}
$$

$$
\text{Market Share (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)} = w_1 R + w_2 G + w_3 C - w_4 T
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Segment size & growth rate
- $G$ = Competitive intensity (lower is better)
- $C$ = Compatibility with company resources
- $T$ = Threat from substitutes
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Strategic weights (must sum to **1.00**)

$$
\text{Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \times r_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

Where $R_t$ is revenue at time $t$, $C_t$ is cost, $r_t$ is retention probability, and $d$ is the discount rate.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Marketing

- **Product Development:** Identifying a niche (e.g., gluten-free snacks) guides R&D.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Premium segments (organic foods) command higher prices.
- **Distribution Decisions:** Rural segments need different channels than urban segments.
- **Promotional Strategy:** Festival segments (Onam Sadhya ingredients) require seasonal campaigns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Market Size and Segment Sizing

**Problem Setup:** A Kerala-based organic food startup wants to estimate the market size for organic rice in Ernakulam district.

**Given Data:**
- Population of Ernakulam district: **3,500,000**
- Percentage of households buying rice: **85%**
- Average household size: **4.1 members**
- Percentage of households preferring organic rice: **12%**
- Average monthly consumption per household: **8 kg**
- Average price of organic rice per kg: **₹120**

**Step 1: Calculate the Number of Households**

$$
N_{\text{HH}} = \frac{\text{Total Population}}{\text{Household Size}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{HH}} = \frac{3{,}500{,}000}{4.1} = 853{,}658.54
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Households Buying Rice**

$$
N_{\text{Rice}} = N_{\text{HH}} \times 0.85 = 853{,}658.54 \times 0.85 = 725{,}609.76
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Households Preferring Organic Rice (TAM in units)**

$$
N_{\text{Organic}} = 725{,}609.76 \times 0.12 = 87{,}073.17 \text{ households}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Monthly Volume Demand (TAM Units)**

$$
\text{TAM}_{\text{units}} = 87{,}073.17 \times 8 = 696{,}585.36 \text{ kg/month}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Monthly Value Demand (TAM in Rupees)**

$$
\text{TAM}_{\text{value}} = 696{,}585.36 \times 120 = \text{₹}\,83{,}590{,}243.20/\text{month}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate Annual TAM**

$$
\text{TAM}_{\text{annual}} = 83{,}590{,}243.20 \times 12 = \text{₹}\,1{,}003{,}082{,}918.40/\text{year}
$$

**Result:** The annual TAM is approximately **₹10.03 Crores** for organic rice in Ernakulam.

### 3.2 Derivation: Segment Attractiveness Scoring Matrix

**Problem:** A dairy company evaluates **4 potential target segments** for a new probiotic yogurt. Strategic weights are: $w_1 = 0.30$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.25$, $w_4 = 0.20$.

| Segment | Size & Growth (R) | Competition (G) | Compatibility (C) | Substitutes Threat (T) |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Urban health-conscious adults | 9 | 8 | 9 | 6 |
| Children (aged 5–12) | 8 | 5 | 7 | 4 |
| Senior citizens | 6 | 3 | 6 | 5 |
| Gym & fitness enthusiasts | 7 | 6 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1: Calculate SAS for Urban Health-Conscious Adults**

$$
\text{SAS}_1 = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) - (0.20 \times 6)
$$

$$
\text{SAS}_1 = 2.70 + 2.00 + 2.25 - 1.20 = 5.75
$$

**Step 2: Calculate SAS for Children**

$$
\text{SAS}_2 = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 7) - (0.20 \times 4)
$$

$$
\text{SAS}_2 = 2.40 + 1.25 + 1.75 - 0.80 = 4.60
$$

**Step 3: Calculate SAS for Senior Citizens**

$$
\text{SAS}_3 = (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 6) - (0.20 \times 5)
$$

$$
\text{SAS}_3 = 1.80 + 0.75 + 1.50 - 1.00 = 3.05
$$

**Step 4: Calculate SAS for Gym & Fitness Enthusiasts**

$$
\text{SAS}_4 = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 8) - (0.20 \times 7)
$$

$$
\text{SAS}_4 = 2.10 + 1.50 + 2.00 - 1.40 = 4.20
$$

**Step 5: Rank the Segments**

$$
\text{Ranking: } \text{SAS}_1 (5.75) > \text{SAS}_2 (4.60) > \text{SAS}_4 (4.20) > \text{SAS}_3 (3.05)
$$

**Conclusion:** Urban health-conscious adults is the **most attractive target segment** with the highest composite score.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Segmentation Variable Summary

| Variable Type | Example Variables | Best Suited For | Food Industry Application |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Geographic | Region, climate, density | Localized products | Coconut oil brands (Kerala) |
| Demographic | Age, income, gender | Mass-market FMCG | Infant formula (0–2 years) |
| Psychographic | Lifestyle, values | Premium/positioned brands | Organic, vegan, keto foods |
| Behavioral | Occasion, loyalty, usage | Retention-driven firms | Festival sweets, ready-to-eat |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The STP Process in Food Marketing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Conduct Market Research] --> B[Step 2: Identify Segmentation Variables]
    B --> C[Step 3: Develop Segment Profiles]
    C --> D[Step 4: Evaluate Segment Attractiveness]
    D --> E[Step 5: Select Target Segments]
    E --> F[Step 6: Develop Positioning Strategy]
    F --> G[Step 7: Design Marketing Mix 4Ps]
    G --> H[Step 8: Launch and Monitor]

    A1[Consumer Surveys] --> A
    A2[Focus Groups] --> A
    A3[Sales Data Analysis] --> A

    B1[Geographic] --> B
    B2[Demographic] --> B
    B3[Psychographic] --> B
    B4[Behavioral] --> B

    D1[5Cs Criteria] --> D
    D2[SAS Calculation] --> D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Target Market Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Consumer Data]
        I2[Market Trends]
        I3[Competitor Data]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[ANALYSIS LAYER]
        P1[Segmentation]
        P2[Targeting]
        P3[Positioning]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[TAM Calculation]
        O2[SAM Calculation]
        O3[SOM Calculation]
        O4[Perceptual Map]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[DECISION LAYER]
        D1[Target Segment Selection]
        D2[Marketing Mix Design]
        D3[Resource Allocation]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P3 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
    O1 --> D1
    O2 --> D1
    O3 --> D1
    O4 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Perceptual Map Concept

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Perceptual Map of Kerala Tea Brands
    x-axis Low Price --> High Price
    y-axis Traditional --> Premium Quality
    quadrant-1 Premium Segment
    quadrant-2 Value Premium
    quadrant-3 Mass Market
    quadrant-4 Affordable Quality
    Brand A: [0.85, 0.90]
    Brand B: [0.20, 0.30]
    Brand C: [0.60, 0.75]
    Brand D: [0.35, 0.20]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Target Market" and explain the concept of STP with reference to the food industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **target market** is a specific group of consumers at whom a company directs its marketing efforts and resources. It consists of consumers with similar needs, characteristics, or behaviors that the company aims to serve with a tailored marketing mix.

The **STP framework** stands for:
- **S – Segmentation:** Dividing the broad food market into smaller, homogeneous groups based on variables like demographics, psychographics, geography, and behavior.
- **T – Targeting:** Evaluating each segment and selecting one or more segments to enter.
- **P – Positioning:** Designing the food product's image and offering to occupy a distinct place in the target consumer's mind.

**Example:** Britannia segments the biscuit market into kids, adults, and health-conscious consumers, targets the premium health segment with *NutriChoice*, and positions it as a "wholesome snack." **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between TAM, SAM, and SOM with a suitable food marketing example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Metric | Definition | Example: Organic Honey Market in India |
|--------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| **TAM** | Total revenue opportunity if 100% share achieved | All honey buyers in India (~₹3,000 Cr) |
| **SAM** | Segment of TAM reachable by firm's product | Online + premium retail channels (~₹600 Cr) |
| **SOM** | Realistic share the firm can capture | ~5% of SAM = ₹30 Cr in 3 years |

The relationship is always: **TAM ≥ SAM ≥ SOM**. Marketers use this hierarchy to size opportunities and set realistic sales forecasts. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplemented Model]**

**(a)** Explain the major bases of market segmentation with **specific examples from the food industry**. Discuss how behavioral segmentation is more useful than demographic segmentation for premium food brands. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Kerala-based startup wants to launch a new range of millet-based breakfast products. Outline a step-by-step process to identify and analyze the target market for this product. Use the **5Cs framework** in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Market Segmentation Bases (7 marks):**

1. **Geographic Segmentation (1 mark):** Dividing the market by region, climate, or location. *Example:* Sales of hot *sambar powder* are higher in South India; cold beverages sell more in tropical Kerala summers.

2. **Demographic Segmentation (1 mark):** Age, income, gender, family size. *Example:* Infant formula targets mothers aged 25–35 with infants aged 0–2 years.

3. **Psychographic Segmentation (1 mark):** Lifestyle, values, social class. *Example:* Vegan, keto, and organic food buyers belong to the health-conscious lifestyle segment.

4. **Behavioral Segmentation (1.5 marks):** Occasions, benefits sought, loyalty, usage rate. *Example:* MTR Ready-to-Eat products target working professionals seeking convenience during lunch hours; loyalty programs at *Chai Point* retain habitual customers.

5. **Why Behavioral > Demographic for Premium Food Brands (2.5 marks):**
   - Demographics only tell "who" the customer is, not "why" they buy.
   - Behavioral data reveals **occasions, motivations, and benefits** sought — critical for premium positioning.
   - *Example:* Two 30-year-old urban professionals (same demographic) may have entirely different food preferences — one seeks organic, the other seeks indulgent. Behavioral segmentation captures this difference, while demographic segmentation does not.
   - Behavioral targeting also enables **dynamic pricing, personalized promotions, and loyalty rewards** for premium brands.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Target Market Analysis for Millet Breakfast Products (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Define the Broad Market (1 mark):** The Indian breakfast market, valued at ~₹30,000 Cr, including cereals, traditional items, and ready-to-eat options.

**Step 2: Choose Segmentation Variables (1.5 marks):**
- *Demographic:* Urban, middle-income, ages 25–50.
- *Psychographic:* Health-conscious, diabetic-friendly seekers.
- *Behavioral:* Daily breakfast users, convenience seekers.

**Step 3: Develop Segment Profiles (1 mark):** Create personas like "Working Professional Riya (28, Kochi, health-conscious, time-poor)" and "Diabetic Retired Mr. Pillai (62, Thrissur)."

**Step 4: Apply the 5Cs Framework (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Analysis |
|-----------|----------|
| **Customer Needs** | Strong unmet need for healthy, convenient, diabetic-friendly breakfast. |
| **Company Objectives** | Aligns with the startup's mission of "healthy traditional foods." |
| **Competition** | MTR and Kellogg's dominate RTE; millets are an under-served niche. |
| **Cost** | Millet sourcing is affordable in Kerala; processing cost is moderate. |
| **Compatibility** | Fits the startup's existing millet supply chain and brand ethos. |

**Step 5: Select Target Segment (0.5 mark):** Urban, health-conscious adults aged 25–45 in Tier 1 & 2 Kerala cities.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Variant]**

**(a)** What is **Positioning**? Explain any **three positioning strategies** with food industry examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The annual demand for processed idli/dosa batter in a city of 800,000 population is to be estimated. Given that 70% of households consume batter, average household size is 4, monthly per-household consumption is 5 kg, and the price is ₹60/kg, calculate the **annual TAM** in value terms. Also identify **two key behavioral segments** for this product. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Positioning and Three Strategies (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Positioning is the process of creating a distinctive image and identity for a product in the target consumer's mind, relative to competing offerings.

**Three Positioning Strategies (5 marks — 1.67 each):**

1. **Attribute Positioning:** Highlighting a specific product feature. *Example:* "Fortified with Vitamin D" — *Amul Pro* milk.

2. **Benefit Positioning:** Leading with the consumer benefit. *Example:* "Maggi 2-Minute Noodles — *Fast, Tasty, Done!*"

3. **Use/Application Positioning:** Tied to a specific occasion. *Example:* *Cadbury* celebrating festivals like Onam and Diwali with gift packs.

4. *(Bonus)* **User Positioning:** Targeted to a specific user. *Example:* *Horlicks* targeting school-going children for growth.

---

**(b) TAM Calculation and Behavioral Segments (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Number of Households (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{HH}} = \frac{800{,}000}{4} = 200{,}000 \text{ households}
$$

**Step 2: Households Buying Batter (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{Batter}} = 200{,}000 \times 0.70 = 140{,}000 \text{ households}
$$

**Step 3: Monthly Volume (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Volume} = 140{,}000 \times 5 = 700{,}000 \text{ kg/month}
$$

**Step 4: Monthly Value (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Value} = 700{,}000 \times 60 = \text{₹}\,42{,}000{,}000/\text{month}
$$

**Step 5: Annual TAM (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Annual TAM} = 42{,}000{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}\,50{,}40{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}50.4 \text{ Crores}
$$

**Two Behavioral Segments (Bonus — for full 7 marks):**
1. **Occasion-based:** Morning convenience buyers (working professionals, hostels).
2. **Loyalty/Usage-rate:** High-volume traditional South Indian households with daily breakfast rituals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing TAM with SAM:** Students often calculate only one without differentiating. Always show the hierarchy.
> 2. **Skipping units in calculations:** Every step must show units (kg, households, ₹).
> 3. **STP in wrong order:** The correct order is **S → T → P**, not T → S → P.
> 4. **Vague positioning answers:** "Good quality" is NOT positioning. Use specific, customer-relevant statements.
> 5. **Weight sum check:** In SAS formula, students forget that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$. Examiners **deduct marks** if the sum is not 1.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Identifying and Analyzing Target Markets:**

- **Core Framework:** STP — Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning. Memorize the order.
- **Segmentation Bases:** 4 major — Geographic, Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioral.
- **5Cs of Target Evaluation:** Customer, Company, Competition, Cost, Compatibility.
- **Market Hierarchy:** TAM > SAM > SOM (always).
- **TAM Formula:** Number of buyers × Quantity × Price (annualized).
- **SAS Formula:** $w_1 R + w_2 G + w_3 C - w_4 T$, where weights must sum to **1**.
- **CLV Formula:** Discounted future cash flows from a customer segment.
- **Selection Strategies:** Undifferentiated, Differentiated, Concentrated, Micromarketing.
- **Behavioral > Demographic** for premium/niche food brands because it captures *why* people buy, not just *who* they are.
- **Positioning Types:** Attribute, Benefit, Use/Application, User, Competitor-based.
- **Kerala-Specific Examples:** Use *Milma, Amul, MTR, Maggi, Britannia, Nandini, Eastern* to score well in KTU exams.
- **Always state units** in market sizing problems — a top-3 valuation tip.
- **Avoid generic answers:** Examiners reward **food-specific, data-driven, regionally-relevant** responses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brand Equity and Brand Loyalty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Brand Equity and Brand Loyalty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Brand Equity — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand Equity (David A. Aaker, 1991):** *"A set of brand assets and liabilities linked to a brand — its name and symbol — that add to or subtract from the value provided by a product or service to a firm and/or to that firm's customers."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer-Based Brand Equity (Kevin Lane Keller, 1993):** *"The differential effect that brand knowledge has on consumer response to the marketing of that brand."*

**Brand Knowledge** is the **controlling node** of brand equity and consists of two components:
- **Brand Awareness** (recognition + recall)
- **Brand Image** (perceptions and associations stored in consumer memory)

**Core Brand Equity Assets (Aaker's Five-Asset Model):**
1. **Brand Loyalty**
2. **Brand Awareness**
3. **Perceived Quality**
4. **Brand Associations**
5. **Other Proprietary Brand Assets** (patents, trademarks, channel relationships)

---

## 1.2 Brand Loyalty — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand Loyalty (Richard L. Oliver, 1999):** *"A deeply held commitment to rebuy or repatronize a preferred product or service consistently in the future, causing repetitive same-brand or same brand-set purchasing, despite situational influences and marketing efforts having the potential to cause switching behavior."*

**Two Foundational Pillars of Brand Loyalty:**
| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Behavioral Loyalty | Repeated purchase pattern (action) |
| Attitudinal Loyalty | Psychological commitment + emotional attachment (belief) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Brand Equity as a "Reputation Bank Account."**
> Every advertisement, quality check, customer complaint handled, or innovation adds **deposits**. Every product failure, misleading label, or service lapse creates **withdrawals**. The balance in this account is **Brand Equity** — it allows the firm to charge a *price premium*, enjoy *distribution leverage*, and resist *competitor attacks*.

**Food Marketing Analogy — Maggi Noodles vs. Local Instant Noodles:**
- Both are *technically* wheat-and-masala noodles.
- **Maggi** has decades of brand equity: mothers trust it, kids recognize the yellow pack, it survives crises (recall episodes).
- **Local brand** may be cheaper and taste similar, but lacks the *equity premium*.
- A loyal Maggi consumer will pay **15–20 % more** and walk past 5 substitute brands — this *premium* is the **monetization of brand equity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Brand Loyalty Analogy — The Daily Chai Routine:**
> A South Indian household buying **Bru** coffee powder for 20 years is not "shopping" — it is exhibiting **conative-action loyalty** (Oliver's final stage). The brand has become part of the consumption ritual. Switching cost is now psychological, not financial.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Food Marketing

- **Brand Equity Premium =** $\Delta P = P_{\text{branded}} - P_{\text{generic}}$
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — used to track loyalty
- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** — industry benchmark: **60–80 %** for mature food FMCG brands
- **Brand Loyalty Index (BLI)** — composite score
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — central to monetizing equity

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Scheme, the question paper typically tests the *distinction* between **equity** and **loyalty**, the **Aaker vs. Keller** model, and the **loyalty pyramid** stages. Memorize the definitions verbatim — 3-mark questions are definition-driven.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aaker's Brand Equity Model — The Tenets

David A. Aaker (1991, *Managing Brand Equity*) groups brand equity into **four perceived dimensions** and **one asset class** as perceived by the consumer:

1. **Brand Loyalty** — the *anchor* asset; reduces marketing costs, attracts new customers, and provides time to respond to competitor threats.
2. **Brand Awareness** — relates to brand recall and recognition under various conditions.
3. **Perceived Quality** — the customer's perception of the overall quality or superiority of a product/service.
4. **Brand Associations** — anything "linked" in memory to the brand (attributes, benefits, attitudes).
5. **Proprietary Brand Assets** — patents, trademarks, channel rights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aaker's Two Brand Equity Types:**
> - **Brand Equity (Customer-side):** The consumer's perception of the brand (focus of this module).
> - **Brand Equity (Financial-side):** The discounted future cash flows attributable to the brand alone — used in M&A and licensing.

---

## 2.2 Keller's CBBE (Customer-Based Brand Equity) Pyramid

Kevin Lane Keller's **Brand Resonance Model** structures brand equity building in **four sequential steps** — often visualized as a pyramid:

| Level | Construct | Strategic Goal | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottom** | Brand Identity (Salience) | Establish category identification & need-satisfaction recall | *"Which noodles come to mind for 2-minute snack?"* → Maggi |
| **Level 2** | Brand Meaning (Performance + Imagery) | Tangible + intangible brand meaning | *"Tastes good, easy to cook, trusted by mother"* |
| **Level 3** | Brand Response (Judgments + Feelings) | Consumer's rational & emotional reactions | *"I trust Maggi, it makes my child happy"* |
| **Top (Resonance)** | Brand Resonance | Psychological bond, intense loyalty, advocacy | Child draws Maggi in school; mother defends brand on social media |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The resonance level is the "Holy Grail" of food marketing** — it converts customers into *advocates* who defend the brand during crisis (recall episodes, controversies).

---

## 2.3 Oliver's Four-Stage Brand Loyalty Model

Richard L. Oliver (1999) proposed loyalty develops through **four sequential cognitive phases**:

$$
\text{Cognitive Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Affective Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Conative Loyalty} \rightarrow \text{Action Loyalty}
$$

| Stage | Type | Consumer State | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Cognitive | Information-based | "I prefer X based on *information*" | "Maggi has 2-min cook time" |
| 2. Affective | Emotion/attitude-based | "I *like* X emotionally" | "I love the taste of Maggi" |
| 3. Conative | Behavioral intention | "I am *committed* to X" | "I will always buy Maggi" |
| 4. Action | Habitual action | "I buy X on *autopilot*" | Auto-replenishment of Maggi in monthly grocery |

> [!WARNING]
> A brand can be at the **cognitive** stage and yet churn — information alone is fragile. Brands must progress toward **action loyalty** to insulate against price wars.

---

## 2.4 Aaker's Brand Loyalty Pyramid (Five-Tier)

$$
\text{Buyer} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Advocate} \rightarrow \text{Partner}
$$

The pyramid shows that loyalty **scales** beyond mere repeat purchase. In food marketing, the top two tiers (Advocate & Partner) are most valuable:
- **Advocate:** Free word-of-mouth marketing.
- **Partner:** Co-creation (e.g., Amul's "Utterly Butterly Girl" campaign crowd-sourcing).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard Unit / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brand Equity Premium | $\Delta P = P_{\text{branded}} - P_{\text{generic}}$ | INR / kg or % | Price premium extraction |
| Customer Retention Rate | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(C_{\text{end}} - C_{\text{new}})}{C_{\text{start}}} \times 100$ | % per period | Loyalty health-check |
| Repeat Purchase Rate | $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{\text{Customers with} \geq 2 \text{ purchases}}{\text{Total customers}} \times 100$ | % | FMCG loyalty tracking |
| Customer Lifetime Value | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{(AP_t \times R_t \times M_t) - AC_t}{(1 + d)^{t}}$ | INR | Long-term equity valuation |
| Share of Category Requirement | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{\text{Brand volume bought by consumers}}{\text{Total category volume bought}} \times 100$ | % | True loyalty measure |
| Net Promoter Score | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Range $\vert -100 \text{ to } +100 \vert$ | Advocacy measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $AP$ = Average Purchase value, $R$ = Retention rate, $M$ = Margin %, $AC$ = Acquisition cost, $d$ = discount rate, $t$ = time period.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Marketing Engineering

- **Product Reformulation Defense:** Strong brand equity allows a firm to reformulate (e.g., reduce sugar, change packaging) without losing shelf demand.
- **Line Extension Leverage:** A brand with high equity can extend into adjacent food categories (e.g., Britannia from biscuits → bread → dairy → croissants).
- **Crisis Insulation:** Maggi's 2015 ban and 2016 re-launch are textbook cases of equity-based survival.
- **Trade Negotiations:** High equity brands secure premium shelf-space and better trade margins with kirana and modern retail.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — Exhaustive Derivation with a Food Brand Worked Example

**Context:** *A premium South-Indian breakfast mix brand "Maa's Tiffin" wants to measure the Customer Lifetime Value of an urban household. Data: Average order = ₹450, orders per year = 6, gross margin = 35 %, acquisition cost = ₹120, annual retention rate = 80 %, discount rate = 12 %, planning horizon = 5 years.*

---

### Step 1: Identify Variables from the Problem

$$
AP = \text{Average Purchase Value} = \text{INR } 450
$$
$$
F = \text{Frequency per year} = 6
$$
$$
M = \text{Gross Margin \%} = 35\% = 0.35
$$
$$
AC = \text{Acquisition Cost} = \text{INR } 120
$$
$$
R = \text{Annual Retention Rate} = 80\% = 0.80
$$
$$
d = \text{Discount Rate} = 12\% = 0.12
$$
$$
n = \text{Planning Horizon} = 5 \text{ years}
$$

**Logic:** Each of these inputs maps to a single "per-customer-per-year" economic unit. A 5-year horizon is industry-standard for FMCG because it captures 2–3 brand-switching cycles.

---

### Step 2: Compute the Yearly Gross Contribution per Customer

$$
GC_{\text{yearly}} = AP \times F \times M
$$

Substituting values:

$$
GC_{\text{yearly}} = 450 \times 6 \times 0.35
$$

$$
GC_{\text{yearly}} = 2700 \times 0.35 = \text{INR } 945
$$

**Logic:** The customer gives the firm ₹2,700 in revenue per year, of which 35 % converts to gross profit → ₹945.

---

### Step 3: Apply Retention Multiplier to Account for Churn

The retention multiplier in year $t$ is $R^{t}$. Customers who churn in earlier years are weighted less.

$$
\text{Adjusted Contribution}_t = GC_{\text{yearly}} \times R^{t} - \frac{AC}{n}
$$

For smoother accounting, we amortize the acquisition cost over the planning horizon:

$$
\frac{AC}{n} = \frac{120}{5} = \text{INR } 24 \text{ per year}
$$

---

### Step 4: Discount Each Year's Cash Flow to Present Value

$$
\text{PV}_t = \frac{GC_{\text{yearly}} \cdot R^{t} - (AC/n)}{(1 + d)^{t}}
$$

**Logic:** Money earned in Year 5 is worth less today — the **discount factor** $\dfrac{1}{(1+d)^{t}}$ accounts for the time-value of money and risk.

---

### Step 5: Year-by-Year Numerical Evaluation

| Year $t$ | $R^{t}$ | Numerator: $945 \times R^{t} - 24$ | Denominator: $(1.12)^{t}$ | Present Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.800 | $756.0 - 24 = 732.0$ | 1.120 | 653.57 |
| 2 | 0.640 | $604.8 - 24 = 580.8$ | 1.254 | 463.16 |
| 3 | 0.512 | $483.8 - 24 = 459.8$ | 1.405 | 327.26 |
| 4 | 0.410 | $387.5 - 24 = 363.5$ | 1.574 | 230.94 |
| 5 | 0.328 | $310.0 - 24 = 286.0$ | 1.762 | 162.32 |

**Logic behind each row:** Retention probability falls geometrically by 20 % each year. Discount factor compounds at 12 %.

---

### Step 6: Sum the Discounted Cash Flows to Compute CLV

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{5} \text{PV}_t
$$

$$
\text{CLV} = 653.57 + 463.16 + 327.26 + 230.94 + 162.32
$$

$$
\text{CLV} = \text{INR } 1837.25
$$

---

### Step 7: Managerial Interpretation (Analytical Matrix)

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Yearly Gross Contribution | ₹945 | Each retained customer yields ₹945 gross profit annually. |
| Cumulative 5-Yr Revenue | ₹13,500 | If no churn — upper-bound scenario. |
| Net CLV (discounted) | ₹1,837.25 | The economic value of an acquired customer to the firm. |
| Acquisition Payback | Year 2 (₹1,116 cumulative) | Acquisition cost ₹120 recovered by mid-Year 1. |
| Recommendation | Retain beyond 2 yrs | Profitability accelerates; invest in CRM. |

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for Food Marketing Managers:** When CLV > 3× Acquisition Cost, customer acquisition is *economically defensible*. Here, $\text{CLV} / AC = 1837 / 120 \approx 15.3$ — extremely healthy.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Aaker vs. Keller

| Parameter | Aaker's Model (1991) | Keller's CBBE (1993) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Brand assets (firm-side) | Brand knowledge (consumer-side) |
| Core Building Blocks | Loyalty, Awareness, Quality, Associations | Awareness + Image (Identity → Meaning → Response → Resonance) |
| End State | Strong brand identity | Brand resonance (loyalty + advocacy) |
| Methodology | Asset-based, financial lens | Cognitive psychology + memory model |
| Food Marketing Use | Brand portfolio management | New food brand launch & positioning |
| Key Strength | Captures proprietary assets | Captures depth of consumer bond |

---

## 3.3 Brand Equity vs. Brand Loyalty — Distinction Matrix

| Parameter | Brand Equity | Brand Loyalty |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broader — all brand assets/liabilities | Narrower — one component of equity |
| **Owner** | Firm + Consumer | Consumer (in the mind) |
| **Direction** | Can be positive or negative | Typically positive (zero or positive) |
| **Measure** | Price premium, financial valuation, awareness | RPR, NPS, SCR, retention |
| **Outcome** | Firm can charge premium, launch extensions | Customer stays, advocates, defends brand |
