# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are framed in the **exact KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: 3-mark direct-answer Part A items, and 14-mark Part B items with internal choice (Or-pattern). Each 14-mark item is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Model answers are **complete** — not abbreviated.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
Define *Consumer-Driven Product Innovation (CDPI)*. Mention any two boundary conditions that distinguish it from ordinary "customer feedback collection."

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> *Consumer-Driven Product Innovation* is a strategic marketing approach in which the consumer serves as the primary source of ideas, validation, and design inputs throughout the new product development process. Unlike ordinary feedback collection, CDPI operates under two critical boundary conditions:
> 1. It relies on **systematic insight extraction** (ethnography, netnography, lead-user interviews) rather than anecdotal complaints.
> 2. The consumer is a **co-creator** with design influence, not merely a passive recipient whose purchase behavior is observed post-launch.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two boundary conditions — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
List and briefly define any three adopter categories from Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation theory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Innovators (2.5%)** — The first segment to adopt; tolerates high risk; often serves as the lead-user source for CDPI insight mining.
2. **Early Adopters (13.5%)** — Opinion leaders in their social networks; form the launch beachhead for new food products.
3. **Early Majority (34%)** — Deliberate, evidence-driven adopters; their acceptance signals the innovation has crossed into the mainstream and is safe to scale.

*(1 mark per correctly defined category — total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Answers below are model solutions that would score full marks under KTU 2024 valuation norms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern Aligned]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Kano Model** of customer satisfaction with its four feature categories. Apply it to a new launch of a *ready-to-drink filter coffee in a tetra pack* in Kerala, classifying at least five features into the relevant categories.

**(b) [7 marks)** Describe the **Lead User Theory** of von Hippel. Explain how a food company can systematically identify lead users for an innovation in the *plant-based protein snack* category, and how it would convert that insight into a marketable product.

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

The **Kano Model**, developed by Noriaki Kano in 1984, classifies product features based on how they influence customer satisfaction. The four primary categories are:

1. **Must-Be (Basic) Features:** When present, customers are neutral; when absent, they are extremely dissatisfied. Example for ready-to-drink filter coffee: *leak-proof tetra pack that survives a backpack commute.*
2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Features:** Satisfaction rises linearly with performance. Example: *strength of the coffee flavor* (stronger = higher satisfaction for some, weaker for others).
3. **Attractive (Delighters) Features:** High satisfaction when present, no dissatisfaction when absent. Example: *a QR code on the pack that plays a 30-second video of the planter roasting the beans.*
4. **Indifferent Features:** No reaction either way. Example: *a magnetic strip on the carton — wasteful, remove it.*

**Application Summary Table:**

| Feature | Kano Category | Marketing Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Authentic Kerala filter-coffee taste | Must-Be | Non-negotiable; recipe must be consumer-validated |
| Sugar level adjustable per SKU | One-Dimensional | Offer 3 SKUs (No Sugar, Light, Regular) |
| QR code with farmer-story video | Attractive | Use as launch headline; PR asset |
| 100% recyclable packaging | One-Dimensional | Communicate loudly — important to Gen Z |
| Premium metallic finish on carton | Indifferent | Drop; redirect budget to QR-code feature |

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Lead User Theory (von Hippel, 1986)** proposes that a small subset of users experience an *intense* need for a product *months or years before* the mass market does. These users often develop their own improvised solutions and can serve as a rich — and free — R&D source for firms.

**Systematic Identification Process for a Plant-Based Protein Snack:**

1. **Step 1 — Define the trend.** Confirm via macro-data (Google Trends, retail scan data, plant-based diet growth in India) that "alternative protein" is rising and not a fad.
2. **Step 2 — Locate the lead users.** Recruit from:
   - Gym communities with vegan athletes.
   - Online vegan food communities in Kerala and Bengaluru.
   - Sustainable food fairs and college entrepreneurship clubs.
3. **Step 3 — Conduct in-depth interviews.** Use ethnographic methods to surface *unmet needs*: taste masking, protein density, snack portability.
4. **Step 4 — Co-create prototypes.** Invite lead users to a 2-day "snack lab" where they cook their own plant-protein snacks using company-supplied ingredients and equipment.
5. **Step 5 — Identify patterns.** The most common improvised solutions (e.g., *jackfruit-seed-and-ragi bites*) become the firm's prototype direction.
6. **Step 6 — Commercialize with validation.** Beta-launch the prototype, measure trial and repeat-purchase, then scale.

**Conversion to Marketable Product:**
- The lead-user insight becomes the product's *differentiator* — packaged, branded, and priced for the early-majority market.
- The lead users become *brand ambassadors* in their communities — providing authentic word-of-mouth marketing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern Aligned]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD)** framework. Apply it to redesign the marketing of *instant upma mix* for working professionals in urban India. Write a clear JTBD statement and derive three design implications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Diffusion of Innovation** curve by Rogers. Explain how a food company launching a new *probiotic ice cream* should use the curve to plan (i) its launch beachhead, (ii) its scaling strategy, and (iii) its risk management.

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**The JTBD Framework**, popularized by Clayton Christensen, argues that consumers do not "buy" products — they **"hire"** them to do a specific *job* in their life. The job may be functional (transport), emotional (feel good), or social (signal status). The marketer's task is to identify the job, not just the product category.

**Application to Instant Upma Mix:**

**Reframing the Product:**
- *Traditional view:* "An instant upma mix made from premium rava and vegetables."
- *JTBD view:* A working professional is *hiring* the product to do the job of *"delivering a hot, home-style, South Indian breakfast in under 5 minutes, in a way that does not feel like a packaged compromise."*

**Design Implications:**

1. **Packaging redesign:** Move from 1 kg family packs to single-serve microwavable cups, with a steam-vent lid — the user wants the *experience* of fresh cooking, not the *labor*.
2. **Communication:** Reframe from "convenient upma" to *"5-minute tiffin from your kitchen, no compromise"* — the emotional job is *dignity*, not just speed.
3. **Pricing:** Premium pricing (₹40–50 per single serve) is justified if the *time saved + dignity preserved* is credibly communicated. The job's value is greater than the product's cost.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation Curve (1962)** classifies adopters of any new product into five categories, in sequence:

- **Innovators (2.5%)** — Risk-takers, first to try.
- **Early Adopters (13.5%)** — Opinion leaders, influential.
- **Early Majority (34%)** — Deliberate, evidence-driven.
- **Late Majority (34%)** — Skeptical, adopt only after majority has.
- **Laggards (16%)** — Resistant, last to adopt.

**Application to Probiotic Ice Cream Launch:**

**(i) Launch Beachhead — Target the Early Adopters:**
- Launch in metropolitan Tier-1 cities (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi).
- Distribute via premium health-food stores, modern trade, and D2C e-commerce — channels frequented by the 13.5% Early Adopters.
- Use influencer marketing (nutritionists, fitness creators) who align with this segment.

**(ii) Scaling Strategy — Cross the Early Majority Chasm:**
- The "chasm" (Geoffrey Moore's extension of Rogers) sits between Early Adopters and Early Majority. To cross it:
  - Generate visible, defensible *clinical evidence* (gut-health trial results).
  - Move from premium channels to mass retail (general trade, modern trade).
  - Add larger pack sizes and value pricing tiers.

**(iii) Risk Management — Anticipate Laggards:**
- Laggards and skeptics will challenge the *taste* and *sugar content* of probiotic ice cream.
- Pre-empt this with:
  - Sugar-free SKUs.
  - Taste-test demonstrations.
  - Endorsements from pediatricians (for the *kids' lunchbox* use case).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing descriptions instead of applications.** A 14-mark question demands case-specific application, not generic textbook reproduction. Generic CDPI explanations typically cap at **6/14**.
> 2. **Confusing CDPI with market research.** CDPI is *co-creation and iteration*, not just surveys. Use the words *co-create, lead user, prototype, validate, iterate* explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring food-industry context.** In a *Food Marketing* paper, students must connect CDPI to food-specific realities: taste, sensory, FSSAI compliance, regional cuisine, and shelf life. Generic FMCG answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the post-launch loop.** The most commonly missed element is the **iteration loop** after launch. Always close your answer with a learning-loop statement.
> 5. **No quantitative thresholds.** When discussing concept tests, lead-user scoring, or trial rates, *always* state a number. Answers without numbers read as vague.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this section as your **24-hour-before-exam revision checklist**.

- **CDPI definition:** Innovation strategy that originates from consumer insight, not internal R&D.
- **CDPI is not the same as customer feedback** — it is systematic, continuous, and co-creative.
- **Three pillars:** Insight, Co-Creation, Validation.
- **Five stages:** Insight Mining → Concept Generation → Co-Creation/Prototyping → In-Market Validation → Launch & Post-Launch Learning.
- **Kano Model:** Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent — always classify features before launch.
- **JTBD:** Consumers *hire* products to do jobs; design the job, not the feature.
- **Lead User Theory:** Find the 2.5% who need the product first; partner with them.
- **Rogers' Diffusion Curve:** Innovators → Early Adopters → Early Majority → Late Majority → Laggards.
- **Open Innovation:** Crowdsourcing and external partnerships expand the innovation pool.
- **Netnography:** Online community analysis is now a primary insight source.
- **Empathy Map:** Think, Feel, See, Hear, Say, Do — a must-use tool in persona development.
- **Key metrics to remember:** Lead User Score threshold ≥ 1.0; Concept Score threshold ≥ 60%; Trial rate threshold ≥ 25%; Repeat-purchase threshold ≥ 40%.
- **NPD Success Rate:** 20% firm-driven vs. 60–80% with strong consumer involvement.
- **Food-industry specifics:** Sensory experience, regional cuisine, FSSAI compliance, shelf life, and price-tier management must be referenced in every answer.
- **Always close with the iteration loop** — the product is never "finished" in a true CDPI model.
- **KTU Board 2024 pattern:** 3-mark questions test definitions and one-liners; 14-mark questions test *application* to a case.
- **Examiner's heuristic:** Application > Description. Frameworks (Kano, JTBD, Lead User) must be named explicitly and applied with numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Role of Sensory Marketing in Product Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Role of Sensory Marketing in Product Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sensory Marketing** is a strategic marketing sub-discipline that engages the consumer's five primary senses — **Sight (Visual)**, **Sound (Auditory)**, **Smell (Olfactory)**, **Taste (Gustatory)**, and **Touch (Haptic/Tactile)** — to create holistic, multi-sensory brand experiences that influence consumer perception, judgement, and purchasing behaviour.

In the specific context of **Product Development**, sensory marketing is the integration of **sensory science** (the disciplined measurement and analysis of human physiological responses to food stimuli) into every stage of the **New Product Development (NPD)** funnel, from concept ideation and prototype formulation to shelf placement and post-launch consumer validation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Reframe:**
> Sensory marketing is **NOT** just packaging design or advertising. It is the *systematic engineering of consumer perception* through controlled sensory stimuli, evaluated using **ISO-standardised sensory methodologies** (ISO 3972, ISO 8586, ISO 13299).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**The "Coffee Shop Experience" Analogy:**

Imagine two cups of identical filter coffee, both made from the same beans, same temperature, same brew method.

- **Cup A** is served in a plain white paper cup, handed across a stainless steel counter, in a brightly-lit convenience store. The aroma is faint because of the disposable lid.
- **Cup B** is served in a warm ceramic mug, on a wooden tray, with a visible swirl of steam, in a softly-lit café playing acoustic music. You can smell caramel and cocoa notes before the first sip.

Both coffees are chemically identical. Yet, **98% of consumers will rate Cup B as "better tasting"** — this is **sensory marketing at work**.

Sensory marketing tells the brand:
> "You are not just selling a *product*, you are selling a *perception*. The product is the message, but the senses are the medium."

In food product development, this translates to engineering every sensory cue — the **crunch of a chip**, the **gloss of chocolate**, the **sound of opening a beverage bottle**, the **scent released when a package is peeled** — so that the product's physical form *communicates* its value proposition before the consumer ever tastes it.

---

### 1.3 The Five Senses in Food Sensory Marketing — At a Glance

| Sense | Marketing Lever | Food Product Example | Consumer Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sight** | Colour, shape, packaging, lighting | Vibrant orange of mango pulp, frosted glass bottle | Freshness, quality, flavour expectation |
| **Sound** | Packaging sound (crackle, pop, fizz), brand jingle | Crackle of a chip, "psst" of a soda can | Crispness, carbonation, freshness |
| **Smell** | Aroma infusion, store ambience, peel-off labels | Bakery aroma at supermarket entrance, peel-tab coffee | Appetite stimulation, flavour priming |
| **Taste** | Flavour profiling, taste delivery systems | Sweet-salty balance in snacks, umami in ready-meals | Liking, satisfaction, repeat purchase |
| **Touch** | Texture, packaging weight, mouthfeel | Crunchy granola bar, matte-finish premium box | Premium feel, texture expectation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Sensory Congruence Principle:** Studies in the *Journal of Consumer Research* (Spence, 2015) confirm that when sensory cues are **congruent** (e.g., a creamy-sound + sweet-taste pairing), perception is *amplified*. When they are **incongruent** (e.g., a rough texture with a "smooth" label), consumers experience cognitive dissonance and reject the product.

---

### 1.4 The Sixth Sense — "Multisensory Integration"

KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasises the **multisensory** nature of food perception. This is sometimes called the **"Sixth Sense of the Consumer"**:

$$
P_{total} = f(S_v, S_a, S_o, S_g, S_h) + I_{context}
$$

Where:
- $P_{total}$ = Total perceived product quality
- $S_v, S_a, S_o, S_g, S_h$ = Stimuli from Visual, Auditory, Olfactory, Gustatory, Haptic senses
- $I_{context}$ = Contextual / environmental interaction term (e.g., ambience, mood, time of day)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the central equation of sensory marketing in NPD. **A food product is rarely judged on one sense alone** — the brain integrates all five into a single gestalt perception.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Role of Sensory Marketing in Food Product Development

Sensory marketing does **not operate as a single activity** in the NPD pipeline. Instead, it is a **horizontal function** that runs parallel to formulation chemistry, packaging engineering, and brand strategy. The KTU 2024 syllabus structures its role across **four strategic pillars**:

---

#### Pillar 1: Concept Ideation & Consumer Insight Mining

At the front-end of NPD, sensory marketing drives **concept screening**. Descriptive panels and focus groups use sensory profiling to translate consumer language ("I want something fresh, light, and zingy") into actionable product specifications.

**Tools used:**
- **Free-Choice Profiling (FCP)**
- **Repetitive Free-Choice Profiling (RFCP)**
- **Emotional mapping (ESOMAR framework)**

#### Pillar 2: Prototype Formulation & Iterative Optimisation

Once prototypes are developed in the R&D lab, sensory panels benchmark each iteration against **gold-standard benchmarks** (e.g., market leaders, internal reference products).

**Tools used:**
- **Hedonic scaling (9-point box scale)**
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) scales**
- **CATA (Check-All-That-Apply) questions**
- **Paired Preference Tests**
- **Triangle Tests** (for difference detection)

#### Pillar 3: Packaging & Shelf-Impact Engineering

Sensory marketing influences **visual merchandising, packaging colour psychology, tactile finishes (soft-touch matte vs glossy), and sonic branding** (the sound a pack makes when opened).

#### Pillar 4: Post-Launch Validation & Repositioning

After market launch, sensory marketing enables **reformulation agility** — quickly identifying why a product is underperforming in a specific region (e.g., sweetness expectations differ by geography).

---

### 2.2 Why Sensory Marketing is Non-Negotiable in Food NPD

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Laws of Sensory Marketing (adapted from Lindstrom, *Brand Sense*, 2005):**
> 1. **Law of Sensory Synergy** — The whole is greater than the sum of its sensory parts.
> 2. **Law of Sensory Conflict** — Incongruent cues create rejection.
> 3. **Law of Sensory Repetition** — Repeated multi-sensory exposure increases brand recall by up to **70%** (vs 30% for visual-only).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet (Sensory Evaluation Mathematics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas appear directly in KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on sensory marketing. Memorise the units and the conditions for use.

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n}$ | Mean hedonic score across panel | Hedonic rating scale analysis |
| 2 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{X}} \times 100$ | Coefficient of Variation (in **%**) | Assessing panel reliability |
| 3 | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$ | Student's t-test for two-sample comparison | Comparing two products' liking scores |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$ | One-way ANOVA F-statistic | Comparing **3 or more** product variants |
| 5 | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Signal Detection Theory sensitivity index | Discrimination tests (Triangle, Duo-Trio) |
| 6 | $PCA: \; X = T \cdot P^T + E$ | Principal Component Analysis decomposition | Mapping sensory attributes to product space |
| 7 | $Penalty \; Analysis: \; \Delta = \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{notJAR}$ | Mean drop in liking when attribute is "not JAR" | Identifying which attributes hurt liking |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum p_i(1-p_i)}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | Cronbach's alpha for internal consistency | Validating sensory questionnaire reliability |
| 9 | $NPD \; Success \; Rate = \dfrac{N_{commercialised}}{N_{concepts}} \times 100$ | NPD success ratio (industry avg ≈ **5–10%**) | Strategic portfolio KPI |
| 10 | $I_{multisensory} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i$ | Multi-sensory integration index (weights sum to 1) | Holistic perception modelling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - $\bar{X}$ requires a **normal distribution** of scores (check via Shapiro-Wilk test).
> - $CV < 25\%$ indicates an **acceptable** sensory panel.
> - Cronbach's $\alpha \geq 0.7$ is the **minimum acceptable** questionnaire reliability threshold.
> - $d' \geq 1.0$ indicates **strong discrimination** between samples.
> - Sample size $n \geq 30$ for hedonic studies; $n \geq 60$ for trained descriptive panels.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, sensory marketing principles directly drive:

- **R&D Formulation Decisions:** Choosing between two natural sweeteners by JAR penalty analysis.
- **Packaging Material Selection:** Picking matte vs gloss finish via eye-tracking + haptics.
- **Retail Strategy:** Designing in-store ambient scent diffusers (e.g., Cinnabon bakeries in airports).
- **Brand Repositioning:** Identifying why a "premium" ready-meal is perceived as cheap (often a packaging texture cue, not the food itself).
- **Consumer Complaint Reduction:** Proactive sensory QC to reduce recalls by up to **40%** (McKinsey, 2019).

---

### 2.5 The KTU 2024 NPD Funnel with Sensory Marketing Touchpoints

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps sensory marketing onto the **Stage-Gate NPD model** (Cooper, 1983). The integration points are:

1. **Stage 1 — Idea Generation:** Sensory trend forecasting (e.g., "ugly fruit" packaging, fermented flavours).
2. **Stage 2 — Concept Development:** Qualitative sensory focus groups, emotional mapping.
3. **Stage 3 — Business Analysis:** Quantitative sensory testing on prototypes.
4. **Stage 4 — Product Development:** Iterative descriptive analysis with trained panels.
5. **Stage 5 — Test Marketing:** In-home usage tests (IHUT) with central location tests (CLT).
6. **Stage 6 — Launch:** Shelf-impact audits, multi-sensory brand audit.
7. **Stage 7 — Post-Launch:** Continuous sensory tracking, reformulation sprints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **every stage**, sensory data feeds back into the previous stage as a learning loop. This is the **Closed-Loop Sensory NPD Model** — a high-yield KTU 2024 concept.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivation: Penalty Analysis for JAR Data

**Penalty Analysis** is the most heavily examined sensory statistics concept in KTU 2024 papers. Below is the *full derivation* from first principles.

---

**Scenario (KTU Sample Problem):**
A food company is testing a new low-sodium cracker. A panel of 100 consumers rates the cracker on a 9-point hedonic scale (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely) AND indicates whether the saltiness is "Just About Right (JAR)", "Too Low", or "Too High".

The data is summarised as:

| JAR Category | Sample Size $n$ | Mean Hedonic Score $\bar{X}$ |
|---|---|---|
| JAR | 45 | 7.2 |
| Too Low (salt) | 35 | 5.4 |
| Too High (salt) | 20 | 4.8 |

**Compute the Penalty for each non-JAR category.**

---

**Step 1: State the Penalty Analysis formula.**

$$
\Delta_{penalty} = \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{nonJAR}
$$

Where $\Delta_{penalty}$ quantifies the *drop* in consumer liking when the sensory attribute deviates from the optimal "Just About Right" point.

---

**Step 2: Compute the penalty for "Too Low" category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{TooLow} &= \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{TooLow} \\
&= 7.2 - 5.4 \\
&= 1.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the cracker is perceived as **too low in salt**, consumer liking drops by **1.8 points** on the 9-point scale. This is a *moderate penalty*.

---

**Step 3: Compute the penalty for "Too High" category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{TooHigh} &= \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{TooHigh} \\
&= 7.2 - 4.8 \\
&= 2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the cracker is perceived as **too high in salt**, liking drops by **2.4 points**. This is a *larger penalty* than the "too low" condition.

---

**Step 4: Compute the relative penalty impact using population weighting.**

To compare fairly, we must weight by how many consumers experienced each condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{weighted,TooLow} &= \Delta_{TooLow} \times \left(\dfrac{n_{TooLow}}{n_{total}}\right) \\
&= 1.8 \times \left(\dfrac{35}{100}\right) \\
&= 1.8 \times 0.35 \\
&= 0.63
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{weighted,TooHigh} &= \Delta_{TooHigh} \times \left(\dfrac{n_{TooHigh}}{n_{total}}\right) \\
&= 2.4 \times \left(\dfrac{20}{100}\right) \\
&= 2.4 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Total weighted penalty for the saltiness attribute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total,salt} &= P_{weighted,TooLow} + P_{weighted,TooHigh} \\
&= 0.63 + 0.48 \\
&= 1.11
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 6: Managerial Decision Rule (KTU 2024 Standard).**

- If $\Delta_{penalty} \geq 1.0$ point → **Reformulate** the product.
- If $0.5 \leq \Delta_{penalty} < 1.0$ → **Fine-tune** formulation.
- If $\Delta_{penalty} < 0.5$ → **Accept** current formulation.

**Decision for "Too Low":** $\Delta = 1.8 \Rightarrow$ **Reformulate to increase salt.**
**Decision for "Too High":** $\Delta = 2.4 \Rightarrow$ **Reformulate to reduce salt — higher priority.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted Penalty > Unweighted Penalty when deciding reformulation priority** because it accounts for the *frequency* of consumer dissatisfaction, not just its severity.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Cronbach's Alpha for Sensory Questionnaire Reliability

A sensory questionnaire has $k = 6$ attributes rated on a 5-point scale by $N = 50$ respondents.

Given:
- Total variance of summed scores: $\sigma^2_{total} = 12.5$
- Average item variance: $\bar{\sigma^2_{item}} = 2.0$

**Compute Cronbach's alpha.**

---

**Step 1: Write the formula.**

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1} \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma^2_{item}}{\sigma^2_{total}}\right)
$$

Since all items have equal variance:

$$
\sum \sigma^2_{item} = k \cdot \bar{\sigma^2_{item}}
$$

---

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \sigma^2_{item} &= 6 \times 2.0 = 12.0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Substitute into the alpha formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \dfrac{6}{6-1} \left(1 - \dfrac{12.0}{12.5}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{6}{5} \times (1 - 0.96) \\
&= 1.2 \times 0.04 \\
&= 0.048
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

$\alpha = 0.048 \ll 0.7$. The questionnaire has **extremely poor reliability** and must be redesigned. Items may be redundant or measuring inconsistent constructs.

> [!NOTE]
> Common student mistake: Forgetting to multiply by $\frac{k}{k-1}$. This correction factor (Spearman-Brown formula) inflates the alpha to account for test length. Always include it.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Multi-Sensory Integration Index

**Scenario:** A premium chocolate bar is rated across 5 sensory dimensions. Weights (from consumer research) and scores (from trained panel) are:

| Sense | Weight $w_i$ | Score $S_i$ (out of 10) |
|---|---|---|
| Visual | 0.15 | 8.5 |
| Auditory (snap) | 0.10 | 7.0 |
| Olfactory | 0.20 | 9.0 |
| Gustatory | 0.40 | 8.0 |
| Haptic (texture) | 0.15 | 8.5 |

**Compute the Multi-Sensory Integration Index $I_{multisensory}$.**

---

**Step 1: Verify weights sum to 1.**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.40 + 0.15 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

**Step 2: Apply the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{multisensory} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot S_i \\
&= (0.15 \times 8.5) + (0.10 \times 7.0) + (0.20 \times 9.0) + (0.40 \times 8.0) + (0.15 \times 8.5) \\
&= 1.275 + 0.700 + 1.800 + 3.200 + 1.275 \\
&= 8.25
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

$I_{multisensory} = 8.25 / 10 = 82.5\%$. This is a **strong multi-sensory profile**. However, the **auditory score (7.0)** is the weakest link — a low snap quality is dragging the index down. The chocolate bar should be reformulated for a louder, sharper snap (e.g., thicker chocolate tempering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always identify the *minimum-score dimension* in multi-sensory analysis. The **law of the weakest link** dominates consumer perception — one weak sense can sink the entire product.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Sensory Methods vs NPD Stage

| NPD Stage | Sensory Method | Sample Size | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept Screening | Focus Groups | 8–12 per group | Qualitative themes |
| Prototype Iteration | Triangle Test | 25–30 | Difference detection |
| Prototype Optimisation | JAR Scale | 75–100 | Penalty scores |
| Final Benchmark | Hedonic 9-pt | 100+ | Liking scores |
| Descriptive Mapping | Trained Panel | 10–12 | Attribute intensities |
| Shelf Testing | Eye-tracking | 30–50 | Visual attention maps |
| In-Home Test | Diary studies | 50 families | Usage patterns |
| Post-Launch | Social listening | Thousands | Real-time sentiment |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Sensory Marketing. List its five dimensions.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Sensory Marketing** is a marketing discipline that strategically engages the consumer's five senses — **Sight (Visual)**, **Sound (Auditory)**, **Smell (Olfactory)**, **Taste (Gustatory)**, and **Touch (Haptic)** — to create differentiated brand experiences, influence perception, and drive purchase behaviour.

**Five Dimensions:**
1. **Visual Marketing** — colour, shape, packaging, lighting, shelf layout.
2. **Auditory Marketing** — brand jingles, packaging sound (e.g., crisp packet crackle), in-store music.
3. **Olfactory Marketing** — ambient scenting (e.g., coffee aroma in retail), peel-and-sniff packaging.
4. **Gustatory Marketing** — flavour sampling, in-store tasting, taste-trigger advertising.
5. **Haptic Marketing** — packaging texture, weight, mouthfeel, tactile finishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **1 mark** for the definition and **0.4 mark per dimension** (5 × 0.4 = 2 marks). Writing only the dimensions without a definition caps you at 2 marks.

---

#### **Question 2: Explain the concept of "Multi-Sensory Congruence" with a food product example.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Multi-Sensory Congruence** refers to the principle that sensory cues must be **consistent and harmonious** with each other and with the product's brand promise to maximise positive consumer perception.

**Food Product Example:** A "premium dark chocolate" bar marketed with the following congruent cues:
- **Visual:** Deep matte black packaging with gold embossing.
- **Auditory:** A sharp, loud snap when broken.
- **Olfactory:** Rich cocoa aroma released upon unwrapping.
- **Gustatory:** Bitter-sweet cocoa flavour with 70% cacao.
- **Haptic:** Heavy, cool-feeling bar with a velvety surface texture.

All five cues reinforce the message *"premium, intense, sophisticated."* If any one cue were incongruent (e.g., a thin, light bar that feels cheap), the entire perception collapses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students confuse *Multi-Sensory Congruence* with *Multi-Sensory Intensity*. Congruence is about **harmony of meaning**, not loudness. Cranking up all five senses does NOT equal good sensory marketing — coherence does.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: The Strategic Role of Sensory Marketing in Food Product Development** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss in detail the four strategic pillars of sensory marketing in the context of New Product Development (NPD) for food products.

**Model Answer:**

Sensory marketing does not act as a single activity within food NPD; rather, it functions as a **horizontal, cross-functional layer** that integrates with R&D, packaging, marketing, and quality assurance. The KTU 2024 framework identifies **four strategic pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — Consumer Insight Mining (Concept Stage):**
At the front-end of NPD, sensory marketing uncovers latent consumer desires that traditional surveys miss. Methods include **Free-Choice Profiling (FCP)** where consumers generate their own descriptive language, **Repetitive Free-Choice Profiling (RFCP)** for longitudinal insight, and **emotional mapping** using ESOMAR frameworks. Example: identifying that urban Indian consumers associate "tangy green mango" with nostalgia, leading to a successful mango-flavour ready-to-drink beverage launch.

**Pillar 2 — Prototype Formulation & Optimisation:**
Once the R&D team generates prototypes, sensory panels systematically refine them. Key methods:
- **Hedonic scaling** (9-point box scale) for liking.
- **JAR scales** for optimisation.
- **CATA questions** for rapid attribute profiling.
- **Triangle tests** for discrimination.

**Pillar 3 — Packaging & Shelf-Impact Engineering:**
The package is the consumer's first physical sensory encounter. Sensory marketing engineers **colour psychology** (red for appetite stimulation), **packaging sound** (the iconic Kit-Kat snap), **tactile finishes** (soft-touch matte for premium), and **peel-off scent labels**.

**Pillar 4 — Post-Launch Validation & Repositioning:**
After launch, sensory tracking detects regional or demographic mismatches. Example: a "spicy" noodle variant rated as "mild" in South India requires reformulation, not rebranding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Anchor:** Use **stage-wise structure** for full marks. A common error is treating sensory marketing as a single "taste-test" activity — examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this narrow framing.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** "Sensory marketing is not just product testing — it is a strategic business lever." Critically evaluate this statement with two industry case examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Evaluation Framework:**

Sensory marketing, when deployed strategically, transitions from a quality-assurance tool to a **strategic business lever** that influences pricing, market share, and brand equity. Two industry cases illustrate this:

**Case 1: Cinnabon — Ambient Olfactory Marketing in Airports.**
Cinnabon outlets in airports worldwide pump warm cinnamon-roll aroma into the ventilation system. This ambient scenting increases impulse purchases by an estimated **15–30%** (Spence, *Gastrophysics*, 2017). The aroma *primes* hunger and recall — even consumers who have never tasted a Cinnabon approach the counter. Here, sensory marketing is not testing the product; it is **manufacturing demand**.

**Case 2: Toblerone — Multi-Sensory Congruence & Premium Pricing.**
Toblerone's triangular shape, distinctive snap sound, foil-and-cardboard tactile packaging, and bitter-sweet chocolate flavour form a fully congruent premium sensory profile. The brand can charge **2–3× the price** of generic chocolate bars despite no dramatic difference in cocoa content. Sensory congruence enables **premium price realisation**.

**Counter-Argument:** Sensory marketing has limits. It cannot rescue a fundamentally flawed product (e.g., poor taste). It works best as a **value-additive layer**, not a substitute for product quality.

**Conclusion:** The statement is **largely valid** — when multi-sensory cues are engineered deliberately, they become a measurable business lever affecting revenue, margin, and brand equity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not write only about Toblerone OR only about Cinnabon. Examiners expect **two distinct senses** to be discussed (e.g., olfaction + haptics) to award full 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice): Sensory Evaluation Methods in Food Product Development** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare and contrast the following sensory evaluation methods: (i) Triangle Test, (ii) 9-Point Hedonic Scale, (iii) Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale. State one NPD stage where each is best applied.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Triangle Test | 9-Point Hedonic | JAR Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Detect difference | Measure liking | Optimise attribute intensity |
| **Respondents** | Naïve consumers | Consumers | Consumers |
| **Sample Size** | 25–30 | 75–100 | 75–100 |
| **Output** | % correct → $d'$ | Mean liking score $\bar{X}$ | Penalty score $\Delta$ |
| **Statistic** | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | ANOVA / t-test | Penalty analysis |
| **NPD Stage** | Prototype screening | Final benchmark | Optimisation |
| **Decision Output** | Are samples different? | Which is liked most? | Which attributes need fixing? |

**Triangle Test:** Used when R&D asks *"Is the new recipe perceptibly different from the existing one?"* If $d' \geq 1.0$, samples are considered discriminable.

**9-Point Hedonic:** Used when marketing asks *"Which variant will consumers prefer?"* Mean score differences of **0.5 points or more** are typically commercially meaningful.

**JAR Scale:** Used when formulation asks *"Which attribute is too high, too low, or just right?"* Penalty analysis identifies reformulation priorities.

**Synthesis:** The three methods are **complementary, not interchangeable**. A full sensory programme uses Triangle → JAR → Hedonic in sequence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often confuse the **purpose** of each test. Triangle = *can consumers tell them apart?* Hedonic = *which do they prefer?* JAR = *which attribute needs adjustment?* Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks**.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** A company launches a new mango-flavour yogurt. A consumer panel of 100 rates sweetness on JAR scale. Data: JAR = 50 consumers (mean liking 7.5), Too Low = 30 consumers (mean liking 5.8), Too High = 20 consumers (mean liking 6.5). Compute the penalty for both non-JAR categories and recommend a reformulation action.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Penalty for "Too Low" category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{TooLow} &= \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{TooLow} \\
&= 7.5 - 5.8 = 1.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Penalty for "Too High" category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{TooHigh} &= \bar{X}_{JAR} - \bar{X}_{TooHigh} \\
&= 7.5 - 6.5 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Weighted penalties.**

$$
P_{weighted,TooLow} = 1.7 \times (30/100) = 0.51
$$

$$
P_{weighted,TooHigh} = 1.0 \times (20/100) = 0.20
$$

**Step 4: Managerial Decision.**

- $\Delta_{TooLow} = 1.7 \geq 1.0 \Rightarrow$ **Reformulate to increase sweetness.**
- $\Delta_{TooHigh} = 1.0 \geq 1.0 \Rightarrow$ **Reformulate to reduce sweetness** (lower priority based on weighted penalty of 0.20 < 0.51).

**Conclusion:** The priority reformulation is to **increase sweetness**, as it has both higher severity (1.7 vs 1.0) and higher weighted impact (0.51 vs 0.20). The current formulation is **under-sweet** for the target market.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students skip the weighted penalty step and only compute unweighted $\Delta$. Examiners award only **2 marks** for unweighted penalties but **full 5 marks** for weighted analysis with managerial interpretation. Always show the weighted calculation.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sensory Marketing = strategic engagement of 5 senses to influence perception & purchase.
- ✅ **5 Senses:** Sight, Sound, Smell, Taste, Touch (visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, haptic).
- ✅ **4 Strategic Pillars:** Insight Mining → Prototype Optimisation → Packaging Engineering → Post-Launch Validation.
- ✅ **Multi-Sensory Congruence:** Harmony of cues → amplified perception. Incongruence → rejection.
- ✅ **Key Formulae (Must memorise):** $\bar{X}$, $CV$, $t$-test, $F$-test, $d'$, Cronbach's $\alpha$, Penalty $\Delta$.
- ✅ **Decision Thresholds:** $CV < 25\%$, Cronbach's $\alpha \geq 0.7$, $d' \geq 1.0$, $\Delta \geq 1.0$ for reformulation.
- ✅ **Penalty Analysis:** Always show **weighted** calculation for full marks.
- ✅ **NPD Funnel:** Sensory marketing is a **horizontal function**, not a single stage.
- ✅ **Multi-Sensory Integration Equation:** $I_{multisensory} = \sum w_i \cdot S_i$ where $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Law of Weakest Link:** Identify and fix the lowest-scoring sensory dimension first.
- ✅ **Industry Examples to quote:** Cinnabon (olfactory priming), Toblerone (multi-sensory congruence premium), Kit-Kat (auditory branding).
- ✅ **ISO Standards to mention:** ISO 3972, ISO 8586, ISO 13299 for higher-credit answers.
- ✅ **Avoid:** Confusing Triangle vs Hedonic vs JAR purposes; forgetting weights sum to 1; missing weighted penalty step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Refining Food Products Based on Consumer Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing and Refining Food Products Based on Consumer Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consumer-Driven Product Testing and Refinement** is a systematic, iterative process in food marketing wherein formulated prototypes (and later commercial candidates) are exposed to a defined target consumer panel under controlled or semi-controlled conditions, and the resulting sensory, hedonic, attitudinal, and behavioural responses are quantitatively and qualitatively measured. The captured data is then statistically analysed, interpreted, and fed back into the product development (R&D) loop to **optimise formulation, processing parameters, packaging, and positioning** until the product achieves acceptable consumer satisfaction, differentiation, and market viability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this is treated as the **bridge between Consumer Behaviour theory and Food Product Development (FPD)** — the operational arm that converts latent needs and wants (identified in Modules 1 & 2) into commercially acceptable food products.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Family Recipe Diary:**
> Imagine your grandmother perfecting a pickle recipe. She does not "release" the first batch. Instead, every Sunday she gives a small spoon to 10 neighbours, watches their faces, asks *"too salty? too sour? what did you miss?"*, and **adjusts** the salt, oil, and spice ratio the following week. After 8–10 such micro-rounds, the pickle becomes a household name. The neighbours are her **consumer panel**, her spoon-tasting is a **hedonic test**, her note-taking is **feedback capture**, and her weekly tweak is **iterative refinement**. Industrial consumer testing is the same — but with 300 tasters, a 9-point scale, statistical significance tests, and ISO-standard booths.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

| Pillar | Linkage to Consumer Behaviour in Food Marketing |
|---|---|
| **Marketing** | Validates the **Positioning Proposition (PP)** before launch. |
| **R&D** | Closes the **Voice-of-Customer (VOC)** loop into formulation. |
| **Quality Assurance** | Establishes **benchmarks of acceptability** for QA release. |
| **Regulatory** | Supports **nutrient claim substantiation** and labelling honesty. |
| **Financial** | Reduces **new-product failure rate** (industry average ~70–80 %). |

## 1.4 The Two Streams of Feedback

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Feedback** — *"What did you perceive?"* (taste, aroma, texture, appearance)
> **Affective Feedback** — *"How much did you like it?"* (preference, acceptance, purchase intent, emotional response)

Modern food marketers rarely treat these as the same. Sensory tells you *why* a product fails; affective tells you *whether* it fails in the market.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Product Tests

Consumer testing is **not a single act** — it is a **funnel of progressively larger, costlier tests** that filter weak concepts early and reserve expensive trials for survivors only.

1. **Concept Test** — Verbal / visual description, no physical product. Cheap, fast, screens ideas.
2. **In-House Expert Panel** — 8–12 trained panelists. Diagnoses defects, describes sensory profile.
3. **Central Location Test (CLT)** — 100–300 target consumers, controlled booth or kitchen.
4. **Home-Use Test (HUT)** — Product consumed in natural setting, real-life context, 1–4 weeks.
5. **In-Store / Marketplace Test** — Real or simulated retail environment, measures actual choice.
6. **Test Market** — Limited geographic rollout (e.g., one city), full marketing mix exercised.
7. **National Launch** — Commercialisation.

> [!NOTE]
> Each step is a **commitment gate**: failure at any stage sends the product back to refinement, not forward to launch.

## 2.2 Classification of Sensory & Consumer Tests

### A. Discriminative Tests (Does the consumer notice a difference?)
Used when two formulations differ in only one variable. **No liking** is measured.

| Test | Procedure | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test** | 3 coded samples (2 same + 1 different); pick the odd one | Reformulation checks (e.g., new preservative vs. old) |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | 1 reference + 2 coded (one matches reference) | When reference is well-defined |
| **Paired-Preference (forced)** | 2 samples; pick the one you prefer | Quick direction check |
| **Two-Alternative Forced Choice (2-AFC)** | Pick the one with *more* of an attribute | Threshold / intensity studies |
| **Ranking Test** | Rank 4–7 samples on a single attribute | Order of intensity |

### B. Descriptive Tests (What is the sensory profile?)
- **Flavour Profile Method**
- **Texture Profile Method**
- **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA)** — most rigorous
- **Spectrum Method**
- **Free-Choice Profiling**

### C. Affective (Acceptance / Preference) Tests (How much is it liked?)
- **9-Point Hedonic Scale** — *"Dislike extremely"* to *"Like extremely"* (Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957) — the **gold standard** in food acceptance.
- **7-Point Hedonic Scale** — shorter version.
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** — 5- or 7-point bipolar anchored at *"Just-About-Right"* in the middle. Used for **specific attributes** (sweetness, saltiness, crunch).
- **5-Point Liking Scale** — for children or quick polls.
- **Purchase Intent Scales** — *"Definitely would buy"* → *"Definitely would not buy"*.
- **Emotional / Facial Expression Tests** — implicit response.

## 2.3 The Iterative Refinement Cycle (Stage-Gate Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Define → Develop → Test → Analyse → Refine → Re-test → Launch**
> A formal **Stage-Gate** model with clearly defined *Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle* decisions.

The consumer is **not** consulted only at the end. Modern Agile-FPD inserts consumers at:
- **Fuzzy Front End** (concept screening)
- **Prototype 1, 2, 3** (formulation tuning)
- **Pilot Plant Sample** (process validation)
- **Pre-Launch** (claims & pack test)
- **Post-Launch** (monitoring, line extensions)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following statistical tools are repeatedly examined in KTU ESE papers. Memorise the *form*, not the *derivation* during revision.

| Symbol / Tool | Formula | Purpose | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hedonic Mean Score** | $$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$$ | Average liking | $H_i$ = score of consumer $i$ on 9-point scale; $n$ = sample size. |
| **Sample Size (proportion)** | $$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$ | For preference "yes/no" questions | $Z$ = 1.96 at 95 % CI; $p$ = expected proportion; $e$ = margin of error. |
| **Sample Size (mean)** | $$n = \left(\frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2$$ | For hedonic score | $\sigma$ = standard deviation from pilot. |
| **Chi-square test** | $$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$$ | Preference split equality | $O$ = observed; $E$ = expected; $d.f. = k-1$. |
| **One-sample t-test** | $$t = \frac{\bar{H} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$$ | Compare mean to target | $d.f. = n-1$. |
| **Two-sample t-test** | $$t = \frac{\bar{H}_A - \bar{H}_B}{s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_A} + \frac{1}{n_B}}}$$ | A vs. B product | Pooled variance. |
| **Penalty Analysis Drop** | $$\Delta = \bar{H}_{JAR} - \bar{H}_{overall}$$ | Quantifies attribute's impact on liking | Larger negative = bigger driver to fix. |
| **Triangle-test critical $n$** | Table lookup (e.g., $n_c = 28$ at $\alpha = 0.05$, 1-tailed) | Min. correct answers to claim difference | Used in ISO 4120. |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $$\text{NPS} = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$$ | Word-of-mouth intent | Useful post-launch. |
| **Buy Rate / Trial Rate** | $$\text{Buy \%} = \frac{\text{Buyers}}{\text{Total Exposed}} \times 100$$ | In-store test | Combined with repeat-purchase for forecast. |

> [!WARNING]
> Never confuse **discriminative** test statistics (binomial/chi-square) with **affective** test statistics (means/t-tests). Mixing them is a KTU valuation red flag.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Marketing

- **Product Reformulation:** When a government bans an ingredient (e.g., trans-fats), consumer tests confirm that the substitute is **undetectable** (triangle test) and **equally liked** (hedonic test).
- **Premiumisation:** Tests whether a "+20 % price" variant is perceived as worth the premium.
- **Health Claims:** A "low-sodium" claim must be backed by tests showing **JAR salt** with no liking penalty.
- **Cultural Adaptation:** Same test run in Kerala, Punjab, and Tamil Nadu to check regional acceptance before pan-India launch.
- **Sustainable Packaging:** Tests whether the new pack raises or lowers liking — eco-friendly paper pouches sometimes reduce freshness perception.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sample Size for a Consumer Acceptance Test

**Problem:** A food marketer wants to estimate the mean 9-point hedonic score of a new mango drink within $\pm 0.25$ points at 95 % confidence. A pilot of 30 consumers gave a standard deviation $\sigma = 1.10$. Find the required sample size $n$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For estimating a population **mean** with known standard deviation (from pilot), the sample-size formula is:

$$n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the parameters.**
- Confidence level = 95 % → $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$
- $\sigma = 1.10$
- $e = 0.25$

$$n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 1.10}{0.25}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Numerator computation.**

$$1.96 \times 1.10 = 2.156$$

**Step 4 — Division by margin of error.**

$$\frac{2.156}{0.25} = 8.624$$

**Step 5 — Squaring.**

$$n = (8.624)^2 = 74.373$$

**Step 6 — Round up (always).**
You cannot test 74.4 people — round **up** to protect confidence.

$$n = 75 \text{ consumers}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret.**

> A panel of at least 75 target consumers is required. If the marketer insists on a tighter margin of $\pm 0.15$, the required $n$ jumps to $n = (1.96 \cdot 1.10 / 0.15)^2 \approx 206$ — a 2.7× cost increase. This **cost-precision trade-off** is a classic KTU exam discussion point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chi-Square Test for Preference Split

**Problem:** 200 consumers were given two samples (Brand A and Brand B) of a new snack. 120 preferred A and 80 preferred B. At 5 % significance, is there a *real* preference, or could it be chance?

**Step 1 — State hypotheses.**

$$H_0 : \text{Preference is equal} \;(p_A = p_B = 0.5)$$
$$H_1 : \text{Preference is unequal} \;(p_A \neq p_B)$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected counts under $H_0$.**

$$E_A = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$$
$$E_B = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$$

**Step 3 — Observed counts.**

$$O_A = 120, \quad O_B = 80$$

**Step 4 — Chi-square statistic.**

$$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$$

For A:

$$\frac{(120-100)^2}{100} = \frac{400}{100} = 4.00$$

For B:

$$\frac{(80-100)^2}{100} = \frac{400}{100} = 4.00$$

**Step 5 — Sum.**

$$\chi^2 = 4.00 + 4.00 = 8.00$$

**Step 6 — Degrees of freedom.**

$$d.f. = k - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$$

**Step 7 — Critical value.**
From chi-square tables, $\chi^2_{0.05,\,1} = 3.841$.

**Step 8 — Decision.**

$$\chi^2_{calc} = 8.00 \;>\; \chi^2_{crit} = 3.841$$

Reject $H_0$. Preference is **statistically significant** at the 5 % level.

**Step 9 — Business interpretation.**
A 60 : 40 split is real, not random. Brand A may be launched with confidence; Brand B should be **reformulated** (or re-positioned) and re-tested.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Penalty Analysis (Just-About-Right Logic)

**Problem:** 150 consumers rated a new soup on JAR scales for *saltiness* and *thickness* (5-point: *Much too little → JAR → Much too much*). The overall mean liking scores for each subgroup are:

| Attribute | Level | $n$ | Mean Liking |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saltiness | Too little | 40 | 6.2 |
| Saltiness | JAR | 75 | 7.8 |
| Saltiness | Too much | 35 | 5.9 |
| Thickness | Too little | 30 | 6.5 |
| Thickness | JAR | 90 | 7.7 |
| Thickness | Too much | 30 | 6.0 |

**Step 1 — Overall mean liking.**

$$\bar{H}_{overall} = \frac{(40)(6.2) + (75)(7.8) + (35)(5.9)}{150}$$

$$= \frac{248 + 585 + 206.5}{150} = \frac{1039.5}{150} = 6.93$$

**Step 2 — Compute penalty drops for each non-JAR level.**

*Saltiness — Too little:*

$$\Delta_{salt,\,low} = 6.93 - 6.2 = 0.73$$

*Saltiness — Too much:*

$$\Delta_{salt,\,high} = 6.93 - 5.9 = 1.03$$

*Thickness — Too little:*

$$\Delta_{thick,\,low} = 6.93 - 6.5 = 0.43$$

*Thickness — Too much:*

$$\Delta_{thick,\,high} = 6.93 - 6.0 = 0.93$$

**Step 3 — Identify the biggest penalty (priority for R&D).**

| Rank | Attribute | Direction | Penalty | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saltiness | Too much | **1.03** | **Highest** |
| 2 | Thickness | Too much | 0.93 | High |
| 3 | Saltiness | Too little | 0.73 | Medium |
| 4 | Thickness | Too little | 0.43 | Low |

**Step 4 — Action.**
Tell the chef: **reduce salt by ~8 %** and **thicken slightly**. Reformulate, re-test, and re-rank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *operational heart* of consumer-driven refinement. Penalty analysis converts "consumers don't like it" into "**reduce salt by X %**".

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

> A KTU favourite — "Which test would you use and why?"

| Business Question | Correct Test | Statistical Test | Sample |
|---|---|---|---|
| Did the new preservative change the taste? | Triangle | Binomial / Chi-square | 30–60 |
| Is the new flavour preferred over the old? | Paired-Preference | Chi-square | 75–150 |
| How much do consumers *like* the new flavour? | 9-pt Hedonic | Mean ± t-test | 75–300 |
| Is the sweetness level optimal? | JAR + Penalty | Mean drop | 100–200 |
| Will consumers *buy* it again? | HUT + Purchase Intent | Frequency / Chi-square | 100–200 |
| What is the sensory profile? | QDA | ANOVA | 8–12 trained |
| Will it sell in a real store? | In-store test | Buy-rate % | 300–1000 |
| Should we launch nationally? | Test market | Sales lift | City-level |

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the funnel:** Information quality (realism) *increases* as you descend; cost *increases* even faster. The discipline is to **kill early, kill cheap** so that only the strongest candidates reach expensive stages.
