# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **discriminative** and **affective** sensory tests. Give one example of each as used in food product refinement.

**Model Answer:**
Discriminative tests determine whether a consumer can *detect* a difference between samples without measuring liking; example — the **triangle test** to verify that a new emulsifier has not altered the flavour of an ice cream.
Affective tests measure *how much* a consumer likes or accepts a product; example — the **9-point hedonic scale** to score liking for a new mango beverage.
Key distinction: discriminative tests answer *"different?"*; affective tests answer *"how liked?"*.

> **[Marks split: Definition 1 + Example 1 + Comparison line 1]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is a **Just-About-Right (JAR) scale**? How is **penalty analysis** used to refine a food product?

**Model Answer:**
A JAR scale is a 5- or 7-point bipolar scale anchored with *"Just-About-Right"* at the centre, used to measure the *appropriateness* of a specific attribute (e.g., sweetness, saltiness, crispness). Consumers mark whether the attribute is "much too low", "slightly too low", "JAR", "slightly too high", or "much too high".
Penalty analysis then computes the **drop in overall liking** for each non-JAR group. The largest drop indicates the attribute direction (too high/low) that R&D must fix first. It is the operational tool that converts qualitative complaints into quantitative reformulation targets.

> **[Marks split: JAR definition 1 + Procedure 1 + Penalty explanation 1]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit the rationale for choosing a test** — a 2-mark deduction is typical.
> - Mixing up **binomial** (preference) with **t-test** (liking) statistics costs 1–2 marks.
> - Failure to specify **sample size and justification** is the single most common 3-mark deduction in refinement questions.
> - Forgetting the **iterative** nature of refinement — i.e., what happens *after* the test — is a structural penalty.
> - Citing "consumers don't like it" without using **JAR + Penalty Analysis** to *diagnose the attribute* is a fatal flaw in the Model Answer.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

A Kerala-based start-up is launching a **ready-to-drink tender coconut water with natural lemon**. Discuss in detail the **step-by-step consumer-driven testing and refinement process** the start-up should follow before national launch.

**(a) Design the testing funnel: which tests, in which order, with sample sizes, and decision criteria. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept Test (Screening).**
Survey 100 target consumers (urban Kerala, 18–35 yrs, health-aware) with the verbal concept "Tender coconut water with a hint of natural lemon — zero preservatives". Measure purchase intent (5-pt) and uniqueness perception.
Decision: ≥ 60 % "would probably/definitely try" → proceed.

**Step 2 — Expert Descriptive Panel.**
8 trained panelists profile prototypes P1, P2, P3 (varying lemon %, Brix 5–7 °).
Output: sensory lexicon; flag defects (e.g., sourness, cooked-coconut note).

**Step 3 — Central Location Test (CLT).**
- $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.06)^2} \approx 267 \Rightarrow 270$ consumers.
- 9-pt hedonic on overall liking, plus JAR on *sweetness*, *lemon intensity*, *coconut freshness*.
- Decision: mean liking ≥ **7.0** AND at least 65 % JAR on each attribute.

**Step 4 — Triangle Test for Shelf-Stability Variant.**
After 30 days at 25 °C, conduct triangle test vs. fresh product to confirm consumers cannot detect a difference (critical $n_c = 28$ at $\alpha = 0.05$).

**Step 5 — Home-Use Test (HUT).**
200 consumers, 1 week, 4 servings. Measure: liking, repurchase intent, occasion fit, pack convenience.
Decision: ≥ 50 % "would buy regularly".

**Step 6 — In-Store Test.**
3 retail outlets in Kochi, 300 exposed, measure trial and buy-rate.
Decision: trial ≥ 25 %, conversion ≥ 40 %.

**Step 7 — Test Market — Kochi only, 8 weeks.**
Decision: projected first-year sales ≥ break-even threshold.

**Step 8 — National Launch + Post-Launch Monitoring.**
Track social-media sentiment, repeat-purchase rate at 3, 6, 12 months.

> **[Marks: 1 for test sequence, 1 for sample-size calculation, 1 for thresholds, 1 for JAR use, 1 for triangle use, 1 for HUT rationale, 1 for in-store rationale]**

---

**(b) If the CLT returns a mean hedonic score of 6.4 with the lemon JAR showing "too strong" for 55 % of consumers, outline the **refinement loop** including statistical analysis and re-test. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diagnose with Penalty Analysis.**
Compute penalty drops for "lemon too strong" and "lemon just right" subgroups. Hypothetical:

$$\bar{H}_{overall} = 6.4$$
$$\bar{H}_{lemon\,JAR} = 7.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta = 6.4 - 7.5 = -1.1$$
$$\bar{H}_{lemon\,strong} = 5.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta = 6.4 - 5.5 = +0.9$$

**Step 2 — Statistical confirmation.**
A two-sample t-test between JAR and "too strong" groups:
- $n_{JAR} = 120$, $\bar{x}_1 = 7.5$, $s_1 = 0.9$
- $n_{strong} = 150$, $\bar{x}_2 = 5.5$, $s_2 = 1.1$

$$t = \frac{7.5 - 5.5}{\sqrt{\frac{(0.9)^2}{120} + \frac{(1.1)^2}{150}}}$$

$$= \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{0.00675 + 0.00807}} = \frac{2.0}{\sqrt{0.01482}} = \frac{2.0}{0.1218} \approx 16.42$$

$d.f. \approx 268$, critical $t_{0.05} = 1.97$. Since $16.42 \gg 1.97$, the liking gap is **highly significant** — lemon intensity is the prime suspect.

**Step 3 — Refinement hypothesis.**
Reduce lemon juice from 8 % to 5 % of formulation; maintain Brix at 6 °.

**Step 4 — Re-test CLT with new formulation.**
Repeat CLT on $n = 270$ consumers. Hypothetical outcome: mean liking rises to 7.2, JAR on lemon rises to 70 %.

**Step 5 — Re-test passes → move to HUT.**

**Step 6 — Iterate or launch.**
If HUT also passes, proceed to in-store. If not, **return to Step 3** with a further reduction (e.g., 3 % lemon) — the iterative loop is officially engaged.

> **[Marks: 1 penalty calculation, 1 t-test derivation, 1 refinement hypothesis, 1 re-test design, 1 iteration logic, 1 Go/Kill gate, 1 documentation trace]**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

A multinational FMCG is **reformulating** an instant noodle brand to **reduce sodium by 25 %** in response to FSSAI pressure. The current product scores 7.2 on the 9-pt hedonic scale. The R&D team has produced three lower-sodium prototypes (P1, P2, P3).

**(a) Design a **complete consumer test protocol** to validate that the chosen reformulation is acceptable and indistinguishable on liking. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-screening with expert panel.**
Trained panel (n = 10) profiles the four samples (Control, P1, P2, P3) for *saltiness, umami, flavour intensity, aftertaste*. Eliminate samples with obvious defects.

**Step 2 — Triangle test (Control vs. chosen prototype).**
If consumers cannot reliably tell them apart, the reformulation is "sensory-safe".
Critical $n$ at $\alpha = 0.05$, 1-tailed, for $p = 1/3$ is **28 correct of 36 minimum**.

**Step 3 — Central Location Test with hedonic + JAR.**

Sample size:
$$n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 1.1}{0.25}\right)^2 = 75$$
(Use larger $n = 150$ for subgroup analysis.)

Protocol:
- 9-pt hedonic on overall liking.
- JAR on saltiness, flavour intensity, mouthfeel, aftertaste.
- Affective (liking) and JAR scored *separately*.

**Step 4 — Statistical thresholds.**
- Mean hedonic of reformulation must be **≥ 6.8** (≥ 95 % of current 7.2).
- Mean hedonic of reformulation must **not differ significantly** from control by paired t-test ($p > 0.05$).
- JAR on saltiness: at least 60 % in JAR category (some "too low" is expected, but not overwhelming).

**Step 5 — Home-Use Test (HUT) on the winning prototype.**
200 consumers, 2 weeks. Confirm in-context liking and absence of complaint about blandness.

**Step 6 — Nutrient-claim communication test.**
Show pack mock-up with "25 % less sodium" claim; measure believability and purchase intent (5-pt). Threshold: ≥ 65 % "believable".

**Step 7 — Decision gate.**
If all gates pass, scale to pilot plant, then full commercialisation. If hedonic < 6.8, **return to R&D with feedback** — increase salt back partially, add umami boosters (yeast extract, MSG), and re-test.

> **[Marks: 1 panel logic, 1 triangle logic, 1 sample-size calc, 1 hedonic + JAR design, 1 thresholds, 1 HUT purpose, 1 iteration gate]**

---

**(b) Suppose the JAR analysis reveals 60 % of consumers rate saltiness as "too low" and overall liking drops to 5.9. Apply **Penalty Analysis** to diagnose the drivers and recommend a refinement strategy. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the penalty matrix.**

| Attribute | JAR Group $n$ | Mean Liking | Non-JAR Group $n$ | Mean Liking | Penalty $\Delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Saltiness | 90 | 7.0 | 150 | 5.4 | **1.6** |
| Umami | 110 | 6.8 | 130 | 5.5 | 1.3 |
| Aftertaste | 130 | 6.7 | 110 | 5.6 | 1.1 |
| Mouthfeel | 140 | 6.6 | 100 | 5.5 | 1.1 |

Overall mean = 5.9 (given).

**Step 2 — Rank penalties.**
Saltiness penalty (1.6) is the **biggest driver** of the liking drop. The reformulation has over-corrected.

**Step 3 — Directional diagnosis.**
Within non-JAR saltiness:
- "Slightly too low" (60 %): mean liking 5.8
- "Much too low" (40 %): mean liking 4.8
The 60 % "slightly too low" suggests a **mild correction** is needed, not a full revert.

**Step 4 — Refinement recommendation.**
- Increase salt by 4 % (partial restoration; still 21 % below original).
- Add 0.3 % yeast extract and 0.1 % potassium chloride (NaCl-KCl blend) to maintain perception of saltiness without full sodium restitution.
- Hold MSG level constant.

**Step 5 — Communicate to R&D as a closed-loop brief.**

> *"Saltiness is the dominant driver (penalty 1.6). Direction is 'slightly low'. Recommendation: salt +4 %, add yeast extract 0.3 %, KCl 0.1 %. Maintain all other parameters. Re-test with $n = 150$ CLT; expect mean liking ≥ 6.8 and JAR-salt ≥ 60 %."*

**Step 6 — Re-test and iterate.**
Conduct CLT on new prototype. If pass, scale; if fail, re-enter refinement loop with one more iteration (e.g., +2 % salt only).

> **[Marks: 1 matrix construction, 1 penalty ranking, 1 directional reading, 1 quantitative brief, 1 communications trace, 1 iteration logic, 1 documentation discipline]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Testing & Refining Food Products**

- ☐ **Two streams of feedback** = Sensory (what?) + Affective (how liked?).
- ☐ **Test funnel** = Concept → Expert → CLT → HUT → In-store → Test Market → Launch.
- ☐ **Discriminative tests** answer *"different?"* — triangle, duo-trio, 2-AFC.
- ☐ **Affective tests** answer *"how liked?"* — 9-pt hedonic, JAR, purchase intent.
- ☐ **9-point hedonic** is the gold-standard acceptance scale; anchored 1 = dislike extremely → 9 = like extremely.
- ☐ **JAR + Penalty Analysis** is the bridge between consumer complaint and R&D action.
- ☐ **Sample size formula** for proportion: $n = Z^2 p(1-p) / e^2$; for mean: $n = (Z \sigma / e)^2$.
- ☐ **Always round up** sample size to protect confidence.
- ☐ **Chi-square** for preference; **t-test** for hedonic means — never swap.
- ☐ **Triangle test** critical $n$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ is **28 of 36 minimum** (ISO 4120).
- ☐ **Refinement is iterative** — not a single test but a closed loop.
- ☐ **Cost rises faster than information quality** — kill early, kill cheap.
- ☐ **Quantitative + Qualitative** together: focus groups diagnose "why"; surveys measure "how many".
- ☐ **Post-launch monitoring** is the *last* loop, not the end of testing.
- ☐ Always specify **sample size, target, decision criterion** in any KTU answer on this topic.
- ☐ **HUT** captures real-life context; **CLT** captures controlled comparison; both have value.
- ☐ Penalty drop > 0.5 is usually considered a meaningful driver to fix.

> **End of Module 3 — Topic Notes: Testing and Refining Food Products Based on Consumer Feedback**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health and Wellness Trends Affecting Food Choices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT757 - CONSUMER BEHAVIOR IN FOOD MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Health and Wellness Trends Affecting Food Choices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Health and Wellness Trends** in food marketing refer to the dynamic, multidimensional shifts in consumer purchasing behavior, preferences, and consumption patterns that are driven by an increased awareness of the relationship between diet, physical health, mental well-being, and holistic lifestyle outcomes. According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework, these trends encompass the influence of perceived health benefits, nutritional literacy, ethical concerns, sustainability, and preventive healthcare motivations on the food selection process.

In consumer behavior theory (Schiffman & Kanuk framework adopted in the PEFTT757 syllabus), health and wellness trends are classified under the **external environmental influences** and **psychological internal factors** that mediate the consumer decision-making process for food products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Health and wellness trends affecting food choices are the macro-level shifts in consumer attitudes, beliefs, and purchase behavior toward foods that are perceived to promote physical health, mental wellness, disease prevention, ethical sourcing, and sustainable production, thereby altering the structure of the food marketplace."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **lighthouse on a coastline**. In the old days, ships (consumers) sailed into the harbor (marketplace) and picked whatever was at the dock (food available). Today, a powerful lighthouse (health & wellness awareness) beams bright signals. The ships now steer toward green channels, avoid polluted zones, and dock only at "healthy piers" — those selling organic, low-sugar, plant-based, or functional foods. The lighthouse doesn't push the ships; it **guides their navigation**.

**Another analogy:** Think of health and wellness trends as a **"new pair of glasses"** the consumer has started wearing. With these glasses, the same apple on the shelf is no longer just an apple — it becomes a question: *Is it organic? Pesticide-free? Locally grown? Does it support gut health?* The food product hasn't changed, but the **consumer's perception and evaluation framework** has.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of the Trend (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Physical Health** — nutrition, calorie control, functional ingredients
> 2. **Mental Wellness** — stress reduction, mood foods, adaptogens
> 3. **Ethical/Sustainable Wellness** — organic, fair trade, plant-based, eco-friendly

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Indicators

The following metrics are the **standard health-and-wellness consumption indicators** used by global market research bodies (Nielsen, Euromonitor, IRI) and adopted in KTU case-study analysis:

| Metric | Definition | Standard Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **% Share of Health-Wellness (HW) Food Spend** | Proportion of total food expenditure on health-positioned products | **>15%** in mature markets |
| **Organic Food CAGR** | Compound Annual Growth Rate of organic food segment | **~10–12%** globally |
| **Clean Label Adoption Rate** | % of new food launches with "no artificial" claims | **>30%** of SKUs |
| **Plant-Based Penetration** | % of households purchasing plant-based alternatives | **~40%** in urban India |
| **Functional Food Market Size** | Total market value of fortified/functional foods | **USD 250+ Billion** globally |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always anchor your answers with at least **one quantified trend statistic**. The Kerala Board Examiner gives bonus marks for any answer that demonstrates awareness of the **scale** of the health-wellness movement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Drivers Framework

The health and wellness trend affecting food choices is best understood as a **convergence of five mutually reinforcing drivers**. KTU 2024 examiners frequently frame Part B questions around this driver-model.

**Driver 1 — Demographic & Epidemiological Shifts**
- Aging global population (60+ consumers): highest spend on preventive-health foods
- Rising incidence of **lifestyle diseases**: diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disorders, hypertension
- WHO estimates that **non-communicable diseases (NCDs)** account for ~71% of global deaths
- *Why it matters:* This is the **structural, non-reversible** driver of the trend

**Driver 2 — Information & Digital Empowerment**
- Explosion of health information via social media, wellness influencers, YouTube nutritionists
- Rise of **"Dr. Google" syndrome** — consumers self-diagnose dietary needs
- Nutrition labeling apps, QR-coded traceability
- *Why it matters:* This is the **accelerator** of the trend

**Driver 3 — Income & Disposable Spending Power**
- Engel's Law inverse: as incomes rise, consumers shift from **quantity to quality**
- "Lipstick effect" on wellness — even during recessions, consumers maintain wellness purchases
- *Why it matters:* This is the **economic enabler**

**Driver 4 — Pandemic-Induced Behavioral Reset**
- COVID-19 (2020–2022) created a permanent shift toward **immunity-boosting**, **hygienic**, and **home-cooked** food choices
- Surge in "**eat clean**" movement
- *Why it matters:* This is the **catalyst event** — KTU frequently asks this in case-based questions

**Driver 5 — Ethical, Environmental & Moral Consciousness**
- Climate change awareness → **plant-based** and **regenerative** food choices
- Animal welfare concerns → **veganism, dairy alternatives, lab-grown meat**
- *Why it matters:* This is the **values-driven** driver

### 2.2 Key Theoretical Models (KTU High-Yield)

#### Model A: The **Health Belief Model (HBM)** — Rosenstock (1966), Becker (1974)

This is the foundational model explaining **why** consumers adopt health-oriented food choices. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists HBM under consumer decision frameworks.

The consumer's likelihood of choosing a healthy food is a function of:

$$P_{action} = f(Perceived\ Susceptibility + Perceived\ Severity + Perceived\ Benefits - Perceived\ Barriers + Cues\ to\ Action + Self\ Efficacy)$$

**Where:**
- $P_{action}$ = Probability the consumer will choose the healthy food
- $Perceived\ Susceptibility$ = Belief about risk of disease (e.g., "I might get diabetes if I eat too much sugar")
- $Perceived\ Severity$ = Belief about consequences ("Diabetes can cause heart disease")
- $Perceived\ Benefits$ = Belief that the healthy food will reduce risk ("Sugar-free will protect me")
- $Perceived\ Barriers$ = Cost, taste, convenience trade-offs
- $Cues\ to\ Action$ = External triggers — doctor's advice, advertisement, packaging claim
- $Self\ Efficacy$ = Consumer's confidence in executing the choice ("I can resist the samosa")

#### Model B: **Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)** — Ajzen (1991)

The intention to choose healthy food is determined by:

$$BI_{healthy} = w_1 \cdot A_{att} + w_2 \cdot SN_{subj} + w_3 \cdot PBC_{ctrl}$$

**Where:**
- $BI_{healthy}$ = Behavioral Intention to choose healthy food
- $A_{att}$ = Attitude toward the behavior
- $SN_{subj}$ = Subjective Norm (peer/family pressure)
- $PBC_{ctrl}$ = Perceived Behavioral Control
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = Relative importance weights (summing to 1)

#### Model C: **The Wellness Consumer Segmentation Matrix** (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Segment | Motivation | Typical Behavior | Marketing Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wellness Seekers** | Holistic well-being | Buy across categories | Premium pricing acceptable |
| **Health-Focused** | Disease prevention | Read labels, low-sugar, low-fat | Functional food focus |
| **Fitness Enthusiasts** | Performance | Protein, energy bars, sports nutrition | High-frequency buyers |
| **Ethical Consumers** | Sustainability, animal welfare | Plant-based, organic, fair trade | Story-driven branding |
| **Lifestyle Light Users** | Casual interest | Occasional "better-for-you" buys | Convenience-based positioning |

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Definition | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HBM Action Probability | $P = f(Susc + Sev + Ben - Bar + Cue + SE)$ | Predicts preventive food buying |
| 2 | TPB Intention | $BI = w_1 A + w_2 SN + w_3 PBC$ | Predicts health-food intent |
| 3 | Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Stage | Problem Recognition → Search → Evaluation → Choice → Outcome | Maps the wellness decision journey |
| 4 | Maslow Hierarchy Applied to Food | Physiological → Safety → Belonging → Esteem → Self-actualization | Maps "wellness" to self-actualization |
| 5 | Wellness Index (custom metric) | $WI = \frac{HW\ Spend}{Total\ Food\ Spend} \times 100$ | Measures HW orientation |
| 6 | Health Halo Effect | $H_{halo} = \frac{Perceived\ Health\ Premium}{Price\ Premium\ Asked}$ | Explains price tolerance for "healthy" labels |
| 7 | Nudge Elasticity (Thaler-Sunstein) | $E_{nudge} = \frac{\%\Delta\ Choice}{\%\Delta\ Nudge\ Intensity}$ | Measures how shelf/label design shifts choice |
| 8 | Functional Food ROI | $ROI = \frac{Functional\ Claim\ Value}{Marketing\ Investment}$ | Validates health-claim investments |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Formula Pitfall:** When writing the HBM equation in your answer sheet, **do NOT collapse** the "+" and "-" terms. Examiners deduct marks for writing it as a single weighted sum. Always show the **perception construct signs explicitly**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Marketing Utility

Although this is a marketing course, the analytical frameworks taught here are used by:
- **Product development teams** at ITC, Nestlé, Britannia to design new SKUs
- **Brand managers** to position functional foods (e.g., Horlicks, D Protinex, Yoga Bar)
- **Public health policy makers** for designing food-label regulations (FSSAI in India)
- **Retail merchandisers** for shelf placement and category management
- **Digital marketing teams** for influencer-based health campaigns

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Applying HBM to a Real Food Choice

**Case Setup (KTU 14-Mark Favorite):**
> *"Rohit, a 35-year-old IT professional in Bengaluru, is standing in a supermarket aisle choosing between a regular 250 ml chocolate milkshake and a 250 ml 'Sugar-Free, High-Protein, Fortified with Vitamins' milkshake. Both are priced at ₹60 and ₹85 respectively. Using the Health Belief Model, analyze the factors that will determine his choice."*

### Step 1 — Decompose the HBM Constructs

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{action} &= (Perceived\ Susceptibility) \\
&\quad + (Perceived\ Severity) \\
&\quad + (Perceived\ Benefits) \\
&\quad - (Perceived\ Barriers) \\
&\quad + (Cues\ to\ Action) \\
&\quad + (Self\ Efficacy)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Quantify Each Construct (0 to 10 scale)

| Construct | Score | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Perceived Susceptibility | 7 | Rohit's father has Type-2 diabetes; family history alerts him |
| Perceived Severity | 6 | Aware diabetes is serious but feels "it won't happen to me soon" |
| Perceived Benefits | 8 | Believes fortified shakes prevent deficiencies and weight gain |
| Perceived Barriers | 4 | ₹25 price difference is noticeable; taste concern |
| Cues to Action | 9 | Doctor's recent checkup flagged borderline sugar levels |
| Self Efficacy | 7 | Has successfully switched to green tea previously |

### Step 3 — Compute the Net Action Score

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{action} &= 7 + 6 + 8 - 4 + 9 + 7 \\
&= 33\ (net\ score)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Score Against Threshold

The HBM threshold for action is empirically set at **>20** (out of theoretical max 60). Rohit's score of 33 is **well above threshold**, indicating a **strong likelihood** he chooses the fortified, sugar-free variant.

### Step 5 — Marketing Implication Derived

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Strategic\ Levers\ for\ Brand\ Manager} &= \uparrow\ Cues\ to\ Action \\
&\quad + \uparrow\ Perceived\ Severity \\
&\quad - \downarrow\ Perceived\ Barriers
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion for the board exam answer:** *Rohit's choice is determined less by the absolute price (₹85) and more by the cumulative effect of six perception constructs, with **Cues to Action (doctor's advice) and Perceived Benefits** being the dominant triggers. Marketers must engineer the product, packaging, and promotional mix to strengthen cues and reduce barriers.*

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 4Ps of Health & Wellness Food Marketing

| Pillar | Traditional Approach | Health-Wellness Adapted Approach | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Product** | Taste, cost focus | Functional ingredients, clean label, fortification, organic certification | Amul Sugar-Free Pro-Biotic Buttermilk |
| **Price** | Competitive parity | Premium pricing (10–40% above) justified by functional claim | Yoga Bar (vs.普通 biscuits) |
| **Place** | Mass distribution | Specialty stores, e-commerce, organic aisles, D2C websites | BigBasket, Organic Tattva stores |
| **Promotion** | Mass-media advertising | Influencer marketing, nutrition expert endorsements, FSSAI-claim-based ads | Bhumi Pednekar for plant-based brand |
| **People** (Extended P) | Sales staff | Trained nutritionists, wellness coaches on call | Himalaya Wellness customer care |
| **Process** (Extended P) | Standard retail | QR-code traceability, blockchain-verified sourcing | Saffola Masala Oats QR codes |
| **Physical Evidence** (Extended P) | Basic packaging | Health-claim callouts, certification logos, "no artificial" badges | FSSAI + India Organic + Vegan Society logo |

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: TPB Weight Calculation

Suppose a survey of 500 consumers yields the following correlations with healthy food purchase intention:

| Construct | Correlation $r$ with Intention | Squared $r$ (proxy for $w$) | Normalized Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attitude ($A$) | 0.55 | 0.3025 | 0.38 |
| Subjective Norm ($SN$) | 0.40 | 0.1600 | 0.20 |
| PBC | 0.65 | 0.4225 | 0.42 |
| **Total** | — | **0.8850** | **1.00** |

**Step-by-step normalization:**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \frac{0.3025}{0.8850} = 0.342 \\
w_2 &= \frac{0.1600}{0.8850} = 0.181 \\
w_3 &= \frac{0.4225}{0.8850} = 0.477
\end{aligned}$$

**Resulting TPB equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BI_{healthy} = 0.342 \cdot A + 0.181 \cdot SN + 0.477 \cdot PBC
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** PBC (Perceived Behavioral Control) is the **dominant driver** — marketers should focus on making healthy eating feel **easy, accessible, and self-confident** for the consumer, rather than relying solely on family pressure (Subjective Norm has the lowest weight).

### 3.4 Trend Mapping: Evolution of Health & Wellness Food Consumption

| Era | Dominant Consumer Motto | Product Innovation | Communication Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1980s** | "Avoid illness" | Low-fat, diet products | TV advertising |
| **1990s** | "Live longer" | Fortified cereals, omega-3 | Magazine health columns |
| **2000s** | "Look good" | Antioxidants, weight management | Internet, early blogs |
| **2010s** | "Feel good" | Functional foods, probiotics, organic | Social media, influencers |
| **2020s (Post-COVID)** | "Stay safe, stay strong" | Immunity, plant-based, clean label | Instagram Reels, OTT, podcasts |
| **2025+ (KTU future-focus)** | "Holistic, personalized wellness" | Personalized nutrition, AI-diet, lab-grown | Wearable tech integration, AI nutritionists |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
